Figure fig:hcg96 shows that the molecular gas contains multiple components, including spiral structure, a bar component and a ring. Thus the molecular gas in HCG 96a is morphologically classified as both a spiral and a bar+ring.

sources.summary: The paper fits variable NGC 7674 spectra from XMM-Newton and NuSTAR, modeling a 7 keV excess and soft X-rays with two thermal components.

quotes.quote: NGC 7674: We fit the non-simultaneous spectra obtained by \ and . We allow for both intrinsic and absorption-induced variability. We also allow the fraction of scattered (or unabsorbed) power law to vary between the two observations. The spectra showed an excess at 7 keV that possibly corresponds to Fe 26 . We modeled it by adding a Gaussian emission line.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 7674 as the LIRG and hard X-ray AGN in a stage-A merger, with variable absorption measured across several X-ray observatories.

quotes.quote: IRAS F23254+0830 W/E (NGC 7674 and MCG+01-59-081): IRAS F23254+0830 has two galaxies in a stage-A merger, separated by 33 3 (19.5 kpc). NGC 7674 is a LIRG, while MCG+01-59-081 (= NGC 7674A) has a lower IR luminosity (log L_ IR / L_ = 10.01; Ricci2017bMNRAS ). We identify NGC 7674 as the hard X-ray detected AGN. Bianchi2005 report a decline of the 2--10 keV flux between the Ginga (in 1989) and BeppoSAX observations (in 1996), which may be the reason for the large bolometric-to-X-ray correction factor, as noted in Section subsub6-1-1_Xweak . Gandhi2017 analyze the broadband X-ray spectra of NuSTAR, Suzaku/XIS, and Swift/XRT, and indicate the presence of a reflection-dominated CT AGN ( N_ H 3 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 ) assuming three geometries of the nuclear obscurer and reflector.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 7674 as a near-IR-variable Seyfert with possible nuclear dust features, no needed AGN component, and a well-constrained moderate-silicate torus fit.

quotes.quote: Nine out of the 13 galaxies in our sample have been reported to show variability in the near-IR: NGC 7674 (Quillen et al. 2000), NGC 1068, IC 4329A, NGC 2110, MCG 5-23-16, NGC 5506, and NGC 7469 (Glass 2004), NGC 3227 (Suganuma et al. 2006), and NGC 4151 (Koshida et al. 2009).

In our sample, Circinus, IC 5063, NGC 5506, NGC 7582, NGC 2110, NGC 7172, and possibly NGC 7674 show dust features in the nuclear (central 1-2 ) regions (see e.g., Quillen et al. 1999; Maiolino et al. 2000; Martini et al. 2003).

Figure 8 suggests that for NGC 5506, NGC 7582, NGC 7674, and MCG 5-23-16 we do not need to add the AGN component. For NGC 2110 we obtained comparable bolometric luminosities (and other torus model parameters) with and without the AGN component. This is probably due to the lack of photometric points in the near-IR for this galaxy. Finally we note that when we included the AGN component to fit the SED + spectroscopy data of NGC 5506 we could not fit the data with the viewing angles inferred from X-ray data (Section 3.2). This is in agreement with the luminosity comparison. The fits of the Seyfert 2s with broad lines detected in the near-IR are shown in Figure 6, while the marginal posterior distributions are displayed in Appendix A (Figures A8 to A12). For the three galaxies with moderate silicate features (MCG 5-23-16, NGC 2110, and NGC 7674, see Table 4) we are able to reproduce reasonably well the photometric SED points and the mid-IR spectroscopy, and the torus model parameters are mostly well constrained.

sources.summary: GNOSIS observations of NGC 7674 show its FeII emission in a dense OH-line region, where the suppressed spectrum makes the line clearer than the control spectrum.

quotes.quote: We discuss how future instruments can best benefit from the potential of OH suppression and what changes are necessary to do so ( sec:disc ). We give two examples of [ FeII ] emission from the Seyfert galaxies NGC 7674 and NGC 7714.
In the case of NGC 7714 the [ FeII ] emission falls between the OH lines and is clearly visible in both suppressed and non-suppressed spectra.
(v) The suggested benefit of OH suppression at low resolution is supported by our observations of Seyfert galaxies ( sec:sciobs ).

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 7674 has Circinus-like radial velocities and asymmetric northeastern emission, with a model where one cone is visible and the other obscured.

quotes.quote: NGC 7674 NGC 7674 is a Seyfert 2 with a distribution of radial velocities similar to Circinus with asymmetric extended emission to the northeast with high blueshifts and near systemic redshifts. The modeled kinematics provide an excellent fit to the data, clearly fitting the two observed components and what remains of the other velocities. Our model suggests the extended emission is due to a single cone, with the other cone obscured by the host disk. Radio data Mom03 concurs with our model as jet axes are projected along the same position angle as the NLR bicone in our model, which also suggests a qualitatively good fit.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7674 as a Seyfert/LIRG with a Z-shaped radio jet and dual VLBA cores interpreted as two accreting supermassive black holes in a very close binary.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, we have found suggestions of a binary black hole system through multi-frequency VLBA observations of the Seyfert / luminous infrared galaxy (LIRG) NGC 7674 Kharb17b . This galaxy possesses a Z-shaped 0.7 kpc radio jet Momjian03 . The dual VLBA cores have T _b 2 - 6 10^7 K and inverted spectral indices, consistent with being the synchrotron self-absorbed bases of two jets launched from two accreting supermassive black holes. Their projected separation of 0.65 mas (=0.35 parsec) makes them the closest separation binary black hole pair to be imaged with VLBI.

sources.summary: The paper says Mrk 533 matches previous double radio maps, with the brightest component south of the optical position and a mas-scale linear east-west source.

quotes.quote: Mrk 533 (2325+085) Y : Our image is consistent with the image of , the double structure seen in the 8.4 GHz VLA A configuration map of and the 1.6 GHz MERLIN map of . The structural P.A. is 60 ^ . The peak of the brightest radio component (component `C' of ) is 0.4 arcsec to the south of the optical position of . G : On mas-scales, we detect only component `C' of , which forms a linear radio source extending in the east-west direction.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7674 as a high-luminosity Compton-thick AGN and uses it to test whether the MIR to hard-X-ray correlation holds for such objects.

quotes.quote: NGC 7674 : NGC 7674 appears to be a Compton-thick AGN of high luminosity (see Appendix indxrays for details). Strictly speaking, it does not match our target selection criteria as its intrinsic hard X-ray luminosity cannot be inferred with good precision. It is, however an interesting test case in order to check whether the MIR -- hard X-ray correlation holds for Compton-thick objects.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes X-ray spectra of NGC 7674, describing its best-fit components, Fe K emission or flux behavior, and comparison with previous modeling.

quotes.quote: NGC 7674 The global XMM-Newton spectra were best fit with a double absorbed power law. To fit the local continuum between 3 - 8 keV, the data were binned to a minimum of 3 counts and the C-statistic was utilized; the Fe K feature was detected a statistically significant level. Analysis of the broad-band XMM-Newton spectra for this source has not been previously published.

sources.summary: The atlas gives NGC 7674's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 7674 is an infrared-luminous, nearly face-on late-type spiral galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0289 ( D 126 Mpc) hosting a Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 with polarized broad emission lines miller_spectropolarimetry_1990 . It features a compact radio core with kiloparsec-scale, bended jet-like emission to the east and west unger_radio_1988,momjian_sensitive_2003 , which is roughly aligned with the biconical 4.4arcsec 2.5 kpc extended emission (PA 120 ; schmitt_hubble_2003 ). After first being detected in the MIR with , NGC 7674 was followed up with ground-based MIR observations aitken_8-13_1985,edelson_broad-band_1987,carico_iras_1988,roche_atlas_1991,maiolino_new_1995,miles_high-resolution_1996 .

sources.summary: The paper notes NGC 7674 looked like a classic spiral morphologically but behaved more like an OH megamaser in the near- to mid-infrared; follow-up was blocked by RFI, so it was removed from the SED sample.

quotes.quote: This number was originally 58 SEDs; however, after some examination, NGC 7674 seems to behave much more like an OHM in the near- to mid-IR like just a spiral galaxy, despite looking like a classic spiral morphologically. We attempted to do follow-up observations to determine if it potentially possessed both emission lines; however the OH line cannot be observed due to RFI. We therefore removed this galaxy from our SED sample.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 7674 for a robustness test, describing it as a typical-mass, typical-SFR but distant galaxy whose observations are strongly affected by beam smearing and require matched binning and masking.

quotes.quote: We perform this test on NGC 7674, which is fairly typical in terms of mass [ (M_*/ M _ )=11.39 ] and star formation rate [ ( SFR/M _ yr ^ 1 )=1.14 ] in our sample, but is one of the more distant galaxies ( D=117.93 Mpc) and thus the most strongly affected by beam smearing. For this galaxy, we construct a pure, uncorrelated noise map with the same resolution as the real map, and set the metallicity fluctuation in each pixel to a Gaussian random value with zero mean and unit variance. We then convolve the noise map with a Gaussian beam with the same FWHM that NGC 7674 has and apply the same pixel mask as for the original map.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7674 as an interacting galaxy observed with PSPC, with evidence for extended emission and a soft component modeled as a power law.

quotes.quote: In the case of NGC 7674, only using the first method are the errors small enough to determine that the emission is extended with confidence.
This component is a thermal plasma in Mrk 78 and a power law in NGC 7674.
NGC 7674 NGC 7674 is interacting with UGC 12608, located 17 kpc ( 32 ) toward the northeast.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7674 as a Seyfert galaxy with simple linear radio structure, a likely low-luminosity compact symmetric object, and measurable component motion.

quotes.quote: The component configurations in NGC 7674 and NGC 2110 are simple, linear structures, whereas the configurations in NGC 5506 and Mrk 1210 have multiple components with no clear axis of symmetry.
We suggest that NGC 7674 is a low-luminosity compact symmetric object.

Comparing the images at different epochs, we find a proper motion in NGC 7674 of (0.92 0.07) between the two central components separated by 282 pc and, in NGC 5506 , we find a 3 upper limit of 0.50 for the components separated by 3.8 pc .

gmw_00000263.name: NGC 7679
gmw_00000263.overview: NGC 7679 is an infrared-luminous, peculiar barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770] that has been variously classified as a Seyfert 1 [arXiv:1109.0921], Seyfert 2 [arXiv:2312.03237], or LINER-type active galaxy [arXiv:2112.07084]. It is undergoing a tidal interaction with NGC 7682 [arXiv:2107.12529, arXiv:astro-ph/0106165], which likely triggers its active nuclear starburst [arXiv:2107.12529, arXiv:astro-ph/0106165].

sources.summary: The survey identifies NGC 7679 as a gas-rich type 2 AGN and [C I]-dark candidate, with young nuclear starburst signatures and likely tidal interaction with NGC 7682.

quotes.quote: During this survey, we further discovered the [ CI ] non-detection case of gas-rich type 2 AGN, NGC 7679. Figure fig:Mdust_CO_SFR-CI shows that the upper limits of [ C 1 ] are small when we compare the global properties of galaxies, i.e., M_ dust , SFR, and L'_ CO(1-0) , demonstrating that NGC 6052 and NGC 7679 are candidates for the [ C 1 ] dark galaxies. Two possible scenarios of weak [ C 1 ] (1--0) emission are C ^0 poor/CO-rich environments or an environment with an extremely large [ C 1 ] (1--0) missing flux.

Young starbursts are also observed in NGC 7679 as well. For example, Gu_2001 detected clear higher-order Balmer absorption lines implying the presence of active star-forming regions in the central 2 2 region. By modeling the spectrum, they found that young ( <10 Myr) and intermediate ( 100 Myr) stellar age populations are dominant in the nuclear region of NGC 7679.

The nuclear starburst activity in NGC 7679 may be related to the tidal interaction with NGC 7682 (with a separation of 90 kpc). The keywords ``galaxy interaction" and ``young starburst" are common among NGC 7679 and NGC 6052 Michiyama_2020 , whose [ C 1 ] (1--0) was not detected in our survey.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7679 as a composite Seyfert 1 starburst whose X-ray spectrum needs both power-law and thermal components and shows long-term variability.

quotes.quote: NGC 7679 Is a composite Seyfert 1 starburst galaxy. It is sometimes misclassified as Seyfert 2 (see Shi2010 ). The hard X-ray spectrum is well reproduced by a power-law model, but we had to add a soft thermal plasma component to account for the soft X-ray excess. DellaCeca2001 reported X-ray fluxes 7 times larger in the soft and hard bands from the analysis of contemporaneous (1998) ASCA and BeppoSax observations of this galaxy. Previous X-ray observations of NGC 7679 are available with Einstein (1981) and ROSAT (1990).

The fluxes in the 0.2--4 keV ( Einstein ) and 0.1--2.4 keV ( ROSAT ) bands are factor of 2 larger than those measured in the \ data DellaCeca2001 . These flux variations reflect the long term variability of the X-ray emission of this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7679 as an interacting Seyfert 2 with low [C i]/CO emission, young starbursts, and conditions that may produce low C/O gas.

quotes.quote: NGC 7679 (Mrk 534) is a low-redshift ( z=0.00177 ) Seyfert 2 galaxy whose metallicity has not yet been reasonably estimated. Its star-formation rate is SFR 10-21 M yr ^ 1 DeLooze14,Davies16 . Recently, Michiyama21 reported a low [ C i ] (1-0) / CO(4-3) luminosity ratio of L_ CI /L_ CO <0.08 , similar to NGC 6052. They further reported young starbursts, a finding that supports the hypothesis that a larger population of massive stars may exist.

NGC 7679 is undergoing interaction, possibly with NGC 7682 Michiyama21 . Through SNe type II, these conditions may create enhanced environments resulting in low C/O ratios. Like with NGC 6052 discussed above, our models show that an environment reminiscent to that of O _0 C _1 D _0 15 and O _0 C _1 D _0 15-2 models may explain the very weak detection of [ C i ] (1-0) line.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 7679 as an infrared-luminous Seyfert 2 galaxy with a recent nuclear starburst, mixed-age nuclear stellar populations, and likely tidal triggering by NGC 7682.

quotes.quote: We present our recent spectrophotometric results of the infrared luminous Seyfert 2 galaxy NGC 7679. We find compelling evidence of the presence of a recent nuclear starburst, revealed by the observations of a) the spectral features of high order Balmer absorption lines, b) the weak equivalent widths for CaIIK 3933, CN 4200, G-band 4300 and MgIb 5173, and ) the suggested UV stellar wind resonance lines (N V 1240, Si IV 1400 and C IV 1550) in the IUE spectrum. Using the simple stellar population synthesis model, we find that in the nuclear 2" 2" region, the contributions from the old, intermediate and young components are 21.7 , 42.9 and 35.4 , respectively.

NGC 7679 is one member of Arp 216, the other is NGC 7682, their projected separation is 89.6kpc (Arp ). We suggest that the starburst activity in this galaxy might be related to the tidal interaction with NGC 7682, as indicated by recent numerical simulations (Barnes Hernquist ; Mihos Hernquist ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 7679 as peculiar: optically a pure Seyfert 2, yet showing negligible X-ray absorption, possibly from unusual dust/gas or a hole in the absorber.

quotes.quote: NGC 7679: This source is peculiar within our sample, for it is the only one with negligible X-ray absorption. The optical classification (pure Seyfert 2) suggests that the BLR emission is obscured by dust by at least 3-4 magnitudes . This, assuming a galactic dust-to-gas ratio, would imply an absorbing column density of at least 6-7 10 ^ 21 cm ^ 2 . We can explain this discrepancy in two ways: a) the dust-to-gas ratio could be much higher than galactic, or the dust could be associated to a warm absorber; b) there could be a ``hole'' in the absorber of the nuclear source, so that the X-ray emission from the accretion disk is unabsorbed, but the broad lines, emitted by the larger Broad line Region, are obscured. This scenario is not unplausible, given the recent evidence of a complex structure in the Seyfert 2 absorbers (Risaliti et al 2002).

sources.summary: The paper revisits NGC 7679's internal gas emission, classifying the nucleus as LINER-like, finding extended He II emission, post-starburst signatures, and elongated [O III] structure possibly involving ionization cones.

quotes.quote: While the search results for distant clouds near NGC 7679 were reported in , we discuss its internal gas emission here since these were not considered in the earlier paper and we have new relevant data. The nucleus itself may be better described as a LINER than a Seyfert 2 based on the usual BPT diagrams, in particular having [ O III ] /H < 1 (Fig. fig-IC1481NGC7679spec ), but He II 4686 is relatively strong not only at the nucleus but throughout much of the extended emission region. Like IC 1481, the stellar population shows post-starburst or quenching signatures, with the higher-order Balmer lines prominent in absorption. The [ O III ] structure is elongated, but if it includes ionization cones, there must be a substantial additional contribution near the nucleus broadening its central area.

sources.summary: The paper notes NGC 7679 as a Seyfert 1.9 object whose X-ray spectrum appears Seyfert 1-like and whose nucleus shows starburst evidence.

quotes.quote: NGC 7679 : Another interesting object; being of Seyfert type 1.9 (broad H _ line but no broad H _ ) one would expect the X-ray spectrum to show a low-energy cutoff typical for absorbed systems. However, in their analysis dellac01 found NGC 7679 to appear as a Seyfert 1 in X-rays. Moreover, they found evidence for a starburst in the nucleus of this object.

sources.summary: The atlas gives NGC 7679's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 7679 is an infrared-luminous, low-inclination peculiar barred spiral galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0171 ( D 71.7 Mpc), forming a wide pair with NGC 7682 4.5 to the east (Arp 216). It was initially classified as a LINER/H II transition object dahari_nuclear_1985 and contains both a circum-nuclear and a nuclear starburst, as well as an AGN pogge_star_1993,gu_nuclear_2001,buson_ngc_2006 . The AGN was later optically classified either as Sy 1.9 or Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010,veilleux_optical_1995 .

gmw_00000264.name: NGC 7743
gmw_00000264.overview: NGC 7743 is a barred lenticular [arXiv:1106.5323, arXiv:1310.2770] or spiral galaxy [arXiv:1804.03330] hosting a Seyfert 2 or LINER nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770]. It features a complex, dust-origin nuclear spiral [arXiv:astro-ph/0311271], an ionized-gas disk significantly inclined to its stellar disk [arXiv:1106.5323], and molecular gas outflowing from the active nucleus [arXiv:2201.05080].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7743 as showing a regular two-armed spiral, inner isophote twists, and a dust-origin multi-armed nuclear spiral visible in optical residual images.

quotes.quote: NGC 7743 A regular two-armed spiral is seen in the ground-based images, most clearly in residual images (Fig. 7743m ). The turn of the isophotes, inside 50'' , was explained by Wozniak et al. ( ) as the combined influence of a bar and a triaxial bulge, with the bulge dominating at <10'' . Our analysis of residual images and the behavior of the m=2 Fourier harmonic show that the bar has a smaller size, and that the spiral structure distorts the elliptical isophotes at >20''-30'' .

The sharp isophote twist, at <3'' , can be seen as well in ground-based as in HST images (see Fig. 7743m ). The WFPC2 residual optical images reveal a complex multi-armed spiral (Fig. 7743m ). This spiral completely disappears in the near-infrared NICMOS images. Apparently, this spiral has a dust origin (like the gaseous-dusty spiral in NGC 6951 ).

sources.summary: NGC 7743 has a nuclear spiral and ambiguous bar morphology; its ellipticity peak is attributed to outer spiral arms rather than the bar, leading to an MD or intermediate classification.

quotes.quote: In addition, erw02 comment that NGC 7743 has a nuclear spiral structure which stands out clearly as a blue feature in the colour map and colour profile we present here (Figs. gals and res7743 , respectively). In this case too, this might be the cause of the ellipticity peak we find in the nuclear region ( 1-2 arcsec).

NGC 7743 is a more ambivalent case. The plateau is of very short extent and reaches, in the deprojected case, a relatively small value ( 0.4 ), considerably smaller than that of the other, clear MH-type bars, whose maximum deprojected ellipticity is, in all cases, about 0.6. Furthermore, a closer inspection of the images and ellipse fits of NGC 7743 (Figs. gals and res7743 ) reveals that the peak in the ellipticity profile and the corresponding steep drop are not caused by the bar, as in the cases discussed above, but by the inner parts of the outer spirals arms. All these arguments taken together lead us to classify NGC 7743 as an MD-type bar, rather than an MH one.
NGC 7743 could be an intermediate type, with an intermediate amount of angular momentum exchanged.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 7743 as a detailed disk-break case where deeper imaging reveals symmetric outer arms, a Type II break, and possible tidal origin.

quotes.quote: NGC 7743 shows an even starker example; in the S ^ 4 G image (central panel of Fig.  fig:lsbdemo ), the only hint of outer spiral structure comes from two extremely faint ansae at the northeast and southwest sides of the visible disk (which occur just beyond the break radius). However, deep imaging of the galaxy from the Halos and Environments of Nearby Galaxies (HERON) survey rich17 clearly shows two symmetric spiral arms emerging from the ends of these ansae, at the peak surface brightness of which is a very clear Type II break ( 115 ; bottom-right panel of Fig.  fig:lsbdemo ). This galaxy should therefore be classified as an oval galaxy of Type IId, with an outer disk offset nearly 90 ^ from the inner, presumably elliptical inner disk The spiral arms in this galaxy's inner disk wind in the opposite direction of those in the outer disk, making this an extremely peculiar galaxy. . None of this structure is visible in the S ^ 4 G imaging, and only a hint of it is visible in SDSS imaging.

Whether or not extremely faint, extended spiral arms like those of NGC 7743 (Fig.  fig:lsbdemo ) are tidal in nature is also unclear. NGC 7743 itself does lie at roughly the same redshift distance as NGC 7742 devau91 , and is separated by only 50 \ in projection ( 320 kpc at 20 Mpc). NGC 7742 contains no bar, yet still hosts a star-forming nuclear ring comeron10 , as well as a counter-rotating gas disk silchenko06 , implying a past merger event. These galaxies' local environment thus is or recently has been somewhat active, implying the outer disk of NGC 7743 may also be tidal in origin.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 7743 as a barred lenticular galaxy with an ionized-gas disk strongly inclined to the stellar disk, likely accreted from a gas-rich satellite environment.

quotes.quote: We used deep long-slit spectra and integral-field spectral data to study the stars and ionized gas kinematics and stellar population properties in the lenticular barred galaxy NGC 7743. We have shown that ionized gas at the distances larger than 1.5 kpc from the nucleus settles in the disk which is significantly inclined to the stellar disk of the galaxy. Making different assumptions about the geometry of the disks and involving different sets of emission lines into the fitting, under the assumption of thin flat disk circular rotation, we obtain the full possible range of angle between the disks to be 34 9 or 77 9 . The most probable origin of the inclined disk is the external gas accretion from a satellite, orbiting the host galaxy with a corresponding angular momentum direction. The published data on the HI distribution around NGC 7743 suggest that the galaxy has a gas-rich environment.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7743 as a barred S0-a galaxy whose apparent low disk M/L may be explained by bar effects and low metallicity rather than an anomalous stellar population.

quotes.quote: NGC 7743 is an S0-a galaxy with a bar. As in the previous two cases, the abnormally low M/L for the disk may be caused by the influence of the bar on the rotation velocity. It may also partially be attributed to the galaxy's low metallicity ( Z = 0.008 according to Katkov et al. 2011). In Table table4 , the two values of v_ max disk obtained photometrically for two different metallicities are compared with v_ max reached at R = 5 kpc. For Z = 0.008 , the velocities v_ max disk and v_ max turn out to be close (see Table table4 ). Consequently, as has been noted above, the abnormally low M/L in NGC 7743 is not evident if to take into account a low metallicity of the stellar population and the influence of the bar on the v_ max estimate.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC7743 among low X-ray-to-[OIII] Seyfert-2 candidates, finding low spectral absorption but possible hard-energy flattening consistent with hidden or reflected emission.

quotes.quote: There are also 5 Seyfert-2 galaxies (NGC676, NGC1167, NGC3254, NGC6951 and NGC7743) occupying the low F_ 2-10 keV /F_ [ OIII ] regime. We do not consider NGC4169 because of the large error in the estimation of the [ OIII ] flux (see Ho et al. 1997). These, according to the expected N_H F_ 2-10 keV /F_ [ OIII ] relation, should present high values of N_H . According to Fig. oiii , the minimum N_H value is 2 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 for NGC676, 6 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 for NGC6951 and 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 for NGC1167 and NGC7743. However, the X-ray spectral fittings show low absorption ( <10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) while in addition there is no indication for a flat photon index or strong FeK _ line.

Note however, that in the spectrum of at least two sources (NGC1167 and NGC7743) there is some indication for a flattening at hard energies which could suggest a heavily buried or reflected component.

sources.summary: The atlas classifies NGC7743 as Core-halo and links inner ionized-gas components to AGN jet interaction, altered H-alpha contour orientations, and molecular gas outflowing from the AGN.

quotes.quote: NGC7743 (see Fig. Figure_AB10 ): We have classified the ionised gas morphology as Core-halo . The analysis of both long-slit and IFS spectroscopic data made by conclude that the ionised gas present several components in the inner 1-2 , one of them probably produced by the interaction of the jet from the AGN with the ambient interstellar medium. This could be related to what we see in our image, that the H contours in that regions shows a slightly different orientation than the outer contours (north-south vs northeast-southwest). The H _ 2 velocity field derived from K-band IFS with SINFONI allowed to conclude that the molecular gas is outflowing from the AGN.

sources.summary: The atlas gives NGC 7743's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 7743 is a low-inclination barred lenticular galaxy at a distance of D= 19.2 1.6 Mpc jensen_measuring_2003 with a borderline Sy 2/LINER nucleus ho_reevaluation_1993,ho_search_1997-1,alonso-herrero_nature_2000 . At radio wavelengths, it appears as a compact source at arcsecond resolution nagar_radio_1999,ho_radio_2001 . NGC 7743 was weakly detected with in the MIR and followed up with /IRAC, IRS and MIPS observations.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7743 as a barred lenticular Seyfert with an obscured nucleus, mixed stellar populations, and an infrared line spectrum resembling NGC 2655.

quotes.quote: NGC 7743 (S2) NGC 7743 is a barred lenticular galaxy whose active nucleus appears to be highly obscured Gonzalez-Martin09b . Raimann01 find that the nuclear optical light is emitted by three stellar components, 40 each from an old, metal-rich and a 1 Gyr component, with the remaining 20 from a 100 Myr population. According to Silchenko06 , the luminosity-weighted mean age is < 2 Gyr.

sources.summary: NGC 7743 is discussed as a barred Seyfert spiral with an apparent giant northeastern loop, though deeper STSS imaging indicates the stream candidate is mainly Galactic cirrus.

quotes.quote: The first column presents NGC 7743, a galaxy with an apparently clear stellar tidal stream candidate in its outskirts; however, with STSS observations this feature is shown to be (at least predominantly) Galactic cirrus.

NGC 7743 is a barred Seyfert spiral galaxy showing a giant, 18 \ loop structure to the northeast. Galactic dust clouds dominate the field of view in longer exposures, as shown in the first column of Fig. fig:misk_vs_stss .

gmw_00000265.name: PG 0026+129
gmw_00000265.overview: PG 0026+129 is a bright, radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:2010.09871, arXiv:2212.06183] classified optically as a Seyfert 1.2 galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770]. It features a compact, core-dominated radio structure [arXiv:2307.13599] alongside a diffuse, extended radio component spanning nearly 20 kiloparsecs [arXiv:astro-ph/0606540].

sources.summary: The paper reports reverberation mapping of quasar PG 0026+129, finding variable continuum and broad lines, distinct emitting regions, and a central black-hole virial mass near 2.9e7 solar masses.

quotes.quote: We report on the results of a new spectroscopic monitoring campaign of the quasar PG 0026+129 at the Calar Alto Observatory 2.2m telescope from July 2017 to February 2020. Significant variations in the fluxes of the continuum and broad-emission lines, including \ and , were observed in the first and third years, and clear time lags between them are measured. The broad \ line profile consists of two Gaussian components: an intermediate-width \ with a full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 1964 18 \ and another very broad \ with a FWHM of 7570 83 . \ has long time lags of 40--60 days in the rest frame, while \ shows nearly zero time delay with respect to the optical continuum at 5100 .

PG 0026+129 is a bright radio-quiet quasar with V band magnitude of 15.4 and redshift z =0.1454. This value is given by the shift of the \ 5007 line in our spectra, and is slightly larger than that in the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED; http://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ ). See Sections sec-fit for details. The full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of its broad \ line given by boroson92 is 1860 , allowing it to be classified as an NLS1.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PG 0026+129 as a bright radio-quiet quasar, fitting its X-ray spectrum with reflection models and deriving cutoff, reflection, spin, and iron-abundance constraints.

quotes.quote: From the analysis of this observation, Mrk 382 comes out to have a steep primary continuum with =1.72 0.14 1992A&A...262...49S ; PG 0026+129 (z=0.142) is a bright radio-quiet quasar. It has been observed in the X-ray band by EXOSAT in 1983 and 1984. The EXOSAT spectrum was described by an absorbed power-law with spectral index 1.5 1988ApJ...330..178T .

PG 0026+129 We started the analysis using the phenomenological Model A. This model yielded a very good fit with ^2 = 810 for 761 dof. The primary X-ray component is characterized by a photon index value of =1.93 0.07 and a cutoff energy of E_c=61^ +50 _ 18 . We found a lower limit for the reflection fraction of the neutral reflection component: R_ refl >1.96 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PG0026+129 as core-dominated on VLBA and VLA scales, with inverted/flat radio slopes indicating compact, self-absorbed emission that remains optically thick to high frequency and may vary.

quotes.quote: PG0026+129 has an inverted slope with _ total > +0.9 and _ core > +0.8 . It was observed with the VLA A configuration at 1.4, 4.8, 8.4, and 45 GHz. The 1.4 GHz image shows a slightly extended structure with a flux level of 3-4 mJy Kukula1998 . The flux densities at 4.8, 8.4, and 45 GHz are 0.2-0.3 mJy Kellermann1989,Leipski2006,Baldi2022 , which is consistent with our VLBA measurement. This indicates that the core emission on the VLBA scale dominates also on the VLA scale.

The source therefore is compact and variability may happen, as demonstrated by the VLBA core/total flux ratio 0.7 and the VLBA/VLA flux ratio > 1 . The consistency with the Gaia position ( 5.5 mas) and the high T_ B ( > 10^ 7.0 K), again indicate a compact optically thick source.

sources.summary: The paper finds PG0026+129 compact in HST and high-resolution VLA images, but detects a nearly 20 kpc diffuse radio component in lower-resolution B-array data.

quotes.quote: PG0026+129 . This source shows no remarkable extent in neither the HST nor the high--resolution VLA image (Fig. 1). Kellermann et al. (1989) found that only 4 of the total flux is concentrated in the core of the object (at 4.8 GHz) but they did not find any extended structures. Our A--Array radio map does not show any extent either, not even on a tapered map. However, on an additional map taken with the VLA in the B--configuration, we detect diffuse emission with an extent of nearly 20 kpc that was totally missed by our A--Array observations. Only 12 of the B--Array flux was previously detected with the A--Array.

sources.summary: The paper describes 0026+129 as the sample's most luminous quasar, with low central extinction, mixed H II/AGN diagnostic ratios, and high O III/H near its nucleus.

quotes.quote: The values of A_V that we find for the centres of our quasars range from 1.1 in 0026+129 to 2.7 in 1448+273 , with a median value of 1.9. We note that that 0026+129 is our most luminous quasar, while our least luminous quasar, 1244+026 , has the third highest extinction value of 1.7.

In the remaining three objects, 0026+129 , 0838+770 , and 1448+273 , about half of the points fall in the overlap between the H 2 region area and the AGN narrow-line region area, suggesting that we are either observing H 2 regions distributed within the AGN narrow-line region or we are observing star-forming regions only but with a range of ages (i.e., ages that increase towards the centre of the host galaxy).
In particular, we see high O 3 /H ratios near the nucleus of 0026+129 , our most luminous quasar (see below for further discussion).

sources.summary: The paper identifies PG 0026 as one of two mapped PG QSOs with only a weak optical spectral-index to continuum-luminosity trend, with LTS fits giving a slope about three times its uncertainty.

quotes.quote: (1): No strong dependence of the optical spectral index on the continuum luminosity can be found for all the 17 QSOs, besides two objects (PG 0026 and PG 1613) having some weak trends (with 3 confidence level) for the relationship.

It is clear that there are no apparently strong correlations between the optical spectral index and the continuum luminosity for the mapped QSOs, besides weak trends for the relationship (coefficient still larger than -0.5 but the two-sided significance level p_ null much smaller than 0.01) for the two PG QSOs of PG 0026 and PG 1613.
It is more clearer that besides the PG 0026 and PG 1613, none of the QSOs has the slope 3 times larger than the corresponding uncertainty of the slope.

sources.summary: The atlas gives PG 0026+129's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PG 0026+129 is a radio-quiet quasar at a redshift of z= 0.142 (D 691 Mpc) with an optical Sy 1.2 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It was detected at radio wavelengths with a compact core and extended kiloparsec-scale biconical emission in the north-south direction while its emission is unresolved leipski_radio_2006 . Pioneering MIR observations were performed by , followed by and .

gmw_00000266.name: PG 0052+251
gmw_00000266.overview: PG 0052+251 is a radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:0808.0918, arXiv:1310.2770] hosted by a spiral galaxy [arXiv:0808.0918, arXiv:1408.3335] with a companion [arXiv:0808.0918] and a young stellar population [arXiv:1408.3335]. The host galaxy features a clear bar, a bulge, and an outer ring structure [arXiv:2102.09190].

sources.summary: The paper compares PG0052+251 optical line emission with radio maps and concludes the previously reported south-west radio extension is unconfirmed and not correlated with [O iii] emission.

quotes.quote: PG0052+251 . For this source, Bennert et al. (2002) reported an extended structure in their HST [ O iii ] emission-line map. This image is reproduced as a contour plot in Fig. 2. The corresponding radio map is also shown, but there is no elongation in the direction of the optical line emission component.

The extension to the south--west on a 1.4 GHz map mentioned by Kukula et al. ( ) in agreement with the optical image is not confirmed in our deep 4.8 GHz map. As Kukula et al. ( ) noted, this structure is possibly not real. Thus, there is no correlation between [ O iii ] emission and radio emission for this separated knot.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 0052+251 as a radio-quiet quasar host with spiral morphology, a companion, star-forming emission lines, HII regions, and a young effective stellar age.

quotes.quote: PG 0052+251 PG 0052+251 (labeled 1 in our plots) is a radio-quiet quasar with M _ V = 24.1 and L _ 5 GHz =2.4x10 ^ 39 erg s ^ 1 . The host galaxy, at M _ V = 22.5, is obviously a spiral in the HST images and was classified as such by both Bahcall et al. and Hamilton et al. (also studied by bog82 , BPO85 , SM87 , HJN89 , dunlop93 , MR94 , BKS96 ). It has a companion at a distance of 14.1 . Bahcall et al. suggest that this galaxy is an Sb spiral with a fairly large bulge component, best fit by an exponential disk 2-D profile. This host galaxy is the smallest (R _ e =4.8 kpc) and brightest ( _ e =19.35 mag arcsec ^ 1 ) of our sample.

sources.summary: The paper uses PG 0052+251 as a real quasar host-galaxy test case, finding it is a spiral with younger dominant stellar populations and comparing recovered stellar ages.

quotes.quote: But to show that these results can be extended to real observations, we run multiple realizations of degraded Keck LRIS observations of the galaxy PG0052+251 Sheinis-2002,Wold-2010 through our code with both the TB and DFK-AVG basis sets. These data are longslit observations of both the QSO and the host galaxy at a position offset from the QSO by 3 arcsec.

The results of running sspmodel on the high signal-to-noise data with the DFK-AVG basis set were consistent with previous studies of this galaxy. PG 0052+251 is classified as a spiral galaxy Hamilton-2002,Bahcall-1997 and we find using the DFK-AVG basis set that the dominant stellar populations are from the I1 and I2 age bins--indicative of younger stellar populations. We can also calculate the light weighted logarithmic age, t _ L , of this object's stellar populations as done by Wold-2010 -their equation 3--in order to compare results.

sources.summary: The paper studies the reverberation-mapped quasar PG 0052+251, arguing that its Balmer-line widths, decrement, and PCA line cores indicate a seriously obscured intermediate BLR.

quotes.quote: Then based on the 55 public spectra of PG0052, BLR of PG0052 is been carefully studied in detail. We find that line width ratio of total observed broad H to total observed broad H is 0.7, which is much smaller than theoretical/observational value of 0.9. Furthermore, flux ratio of total broad H to total broad H is about 6.8 (Balmer Decrement), which is not one reasonable value for BLUE quasar PG 0052+251. Moreover, properties of line cores based on PCA technique confirm there is one inner broad component and one seriously obscured intermediate broad component in BLR of PG0052.

sources.summary: The atlas gives PG 0052+251's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PG 0052+251 is a radio-quiet quasar at a redshift of z= 0.154 (D 759 Mpc) with an optical Sy 1.2 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 in a low-inclination spiral host galaxy bahcall_apparently_1996 . It was detected at radio wavelengths with a slightly elongated core in the east-west direction, while its emission is extended in the north-south direction leipski_radio_2006,bennert_size_2002 . PG 0052+251 was first detected in the MIR by , and then followed up with haas_dust_2000 and /IRS and MIPS.

sources.summary: The host of PG 0052+251 has a clear bar, an n=1 bulge fit, and residual outer ring structure left outside the model.

quotes.quote: PG 0052+251: A bar component is clearly present, but to account for it the \ index of the bulge component has to be fixed. The smallest residuals are achieved using a bulge with n=1 . The best-fit model leaves a residual outer ring structure, which we do not attempt to fit.

gmw_00000267.name: PG 0844+349
gmw_00000267.overview: PG 0844+349 is a radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:1801.06097, arXiv:1310.2770] and Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1908.07272, arXiv:1310.2770] hosted by an interacting barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770]. The active nucleus features an unresolved radio core [arXiv:1801.06097, arXiv:1310.2770] and exhibits X-ray flux variability of up to a factor of ten [arXiv:1010.4453, arXiv:1908.07272].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes multi-epoch X-ray observations of the quasar 08, finding a 2009 X-ray weak, reflection-dominated state whose long- and short-term variability can be explained by light bending.

quotes.quote: In March 2009 the well-studied quasar, 08, was discovered with \ to be in an X-ray weak state. A follow-up \ observation several weeks later generated a good quality spectrum of the source, showing substantial curvature and spectral hardening. In combination with archival data at two previous epochs when the source was in a bright state, we examine the long-term spectral and timing properties of 08 \ spanning nearly ten years and a factor of ten in brightness.

In terms of the blurred reflection model, 08 \ is in a reflection dominated state during the 2009 X-ray weak observations, which can be understood in terms of light bending. Moreover, the light bending scenario can also account for the short-term (i.e. 1000 ) spectral variability in the source. Other models cannot be decisively ruled out, but we note distinguishing features of the models that can be explored for in higher signal-to-noise data from current and future observatories.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 0844 + 349 as a variable Seyfert 1 whose X-ray weak state is reflection dominated and whose high-density reflection model fits broad iron and soft excess.

quotes.quote: PG 0844 + 349 PG 0844 + 349 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy veron06 that has shown a large flux variability of a factor of 10 in history. gallo11b analysed the \ observation taken during the X-ray weak state of PG 0844 + 349 and found the spectrum is dominated by the disc reflection component, indicating strong light-bending effects. We first fit the spectrum with an absorbed power-law model and the ratio plot is shown in Fig. pic_fe .

A disc reflection component with a high disc density parameter of (n_ e )=17.2^ +0.06 _ 0.27 can account for both the broad iron line and the soft excess emission. We obtain a high BH spin of a_*>0.95 and an inclination angle of i=32^ +12 _ 15 ^ . The inclination angle is consistent with the measurement in i 34^ gallo11b .

sources.summary: The paper says PG 0844 is the only source whose optical/UV broadband SED could not be fitted by a thin accretion-disc spectrum, citing unreliable reverberation measurements.

quotes.quote: PG 0844 is the only source where the optical/UV broadband SED could not be modelled by thin AD spectrum. The inconsistent on the time-lag measurements from line-to-line see Peterson1999 as well as the residuals on the Balmer narrow-emission lines in this object turns the reverberation mapping results to be of rather low quality. Thus, the BLR size measurement is considered unreliable and does not enable us to find a satisfactory fit to the optical/UV OM photometric points. We believe that the unreliable time-lag measurements enable to find an accurate black hole mass measurements.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG0844+349 as a radio-quiet quasar with an unresolved radio nucleus, narrow-line Seyfert-like optical and X-ray traits, optical polarization, and reverberation black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: PG0844+349 is a radio-quiet quasar at a cosmological redshift of z=0.064 that, in comparison to most quasars, was not originally detected in the radio frequency: at radio wavelengths, its nucleus is unresolved 1994AJ....108.1163K . It was first discovered in the Palomar Green sample 1983ApJ...269..352S and found to possess strong Fe II emission and weak forbidden narrow lines, a behavior that is expected from narrow-line Seyfert-1s (NLS1). On the other hand, the X-ray properties of PG 0844+349 are aligned with the NLS1 classification (a steep soft X-ray spectrum and strong variability, see 2001ASPC..251..112B ), and the optical polarization measurements achieved by 2011AstL...37..302A also point towards a regular NLS1 object (optical continuum polarization of 0.85 0.10 ). Hence, using Type-1 AGN reverberation mapping techniques, 2004ApJ...613..682P estimated the mass of the central black hole to be (9.24 3.81) d8 .

sources.summary: The atlas gives PG 0844+349's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PG 0844+349 is a radio-quiet quasar at a redshift of z= 0.0640 (D 302 Mpc) with an optical Sy 1.0 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 in an interacting barred spiral host galaxy hutchings_images_1990 . Its properties are quite similar to a narrow-line Sy 1 type AGN brinkmann_xmm-newton_2003 . At radio wavelengths, the nucleus is unresolved kellermann_radio_1994 .

gmw_00000268.name: PG 2130+099
gmw_00000268.overview: PG 2130+099 is a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy [arXiv:0909.2180, arXiv:1310.2770] hosted by a highly inclined peculiar spiral galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770] with star-forming outer spiral structures [arXiv:2206.13189]. It features a triple radio structure [arXiv:2210.16764, arXiv:astro-ph/0606540] and exhibits correlated optical variability [arXiv:2111.07385] driven by a super-Eddington accreting central engine [arXiv:2001.03086].

sources.summary: UGC 11763 is analyzed as an active NLS1/intermediate Seyfert with XMM-Newton; the paper finds soft excess and two ionized absorbing phases in pressure equilibrium.

quotes.quote: , also known as II Zw 136, Mrk 1513 or PG 2130+099, among other names, has been classified as a NLS1 galaxy by . The width of the H line in this object is between 2250-2800 1992ApJS...80..109Boroson,2004AJ....127..156Grupe,2008MNRAS.385...53Mullaney,2008ApJ...688..837Grier , only slightly above the upper limit for the NLS1 classification. It was included in the NLS1 sample of and although excluded it from their list. On the other hand, have classified it as an intermediate Seyfert (Seyfert 1.5). estimated an optical redshift z = 0.062977 for this object whose position coordinates are _ 2000 =21 ^h 32 ^m 27 81, _ 2000 =+10 ^ 08 19 46 1981MNRAS.197..829Clements .

The absorption signatures in the spectra of \ are consistent with the presence of a two phase ionized material ( U=1.65^ +0.07 _ 0.08 ; 2.6 0.1 and N_ H = 21.2 0.2 ; 21.51 0.01 cm ^ 2 , respectively) in the line of sight. The physical conditions found are consistent with the two phases being in pressure equilibrium. The low ionization component is more ionized than typically found for warm absorbers in other Seyfert 1 galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents optical continuum reverberation mapping of the Seyfert 1 galaxy PG 2130+099, measuring inter-band lags, disk temperature scaling, and host-subtracted AGN luminosity.

quotes.quote: We present the results of an intensive six-month optical continuum reverberation mapping campaign of the Seyfert 1 galaxy PG 2130+099 at redshift z=0.063 . The ground-based photometric monitoring was conducted on a daily basis with the robotic 46 cm telescope of the Wise observatory located in Israel. Specially designed narrowband filters were used to observe the central engine of the active galactic nucleus (AGN), avoiding line contamination from the broad-line region (BLR). We aim to measure inter-band continuum time lags across the optical range and determine the size-wavelength relation for this system.

PG 2130+099 displays correlated variability across the optical range, and we successfully detect significant time lags of up to 3 days between the multiband light curves. We find that the wavelength-dependent lags, ( ) , generally follow the relation ( ) ^ 4/3 , as expected for the temperature radial profile T R^ 3/4 of an optically thick, geometrically thin accretion disk. Despite that, the derived time lags can also be fitted by ( ) ^2 , implying the possibility of a slim, rather than thin, accretion disk. Using the flux variation gradient (FVG) method, we determined the AGN's host-galaxy-subtracted rest frame 5100 \ luminosity at the time of our monitoring campaign with an uncertainty of 18 ( L_ 5100 = (2.40 0.42) 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper studies Mrk 1513 as a local, high-Eddington Seyfert with unusual Fe-K spectral structure, evaluating whether it hosts a persistent ultra-fast accretion-disc wind.

quotes.quote: We discuss the origin of a very unusual spectral structure observed in the Fe-K band of the Seyfert galaxy Mrk 1513, a local ( z = 0.063) active galactic nucleus (AGN) that is efficiently accreting matter onto its central supermassive black hole ( L_ bol / L_ Edd 0.5). We consider the highest quality X-ray observation of this source available to date, performed in 2003 by XMM--Newton . The hard X-ray spectrum is characterised by a remarkable spectral drop at 7 keV, which can be interpreted as either the onset of a broad absorption trough or the blue wing of a relativistic emission line. Overall, this complex feature is significant at > 5 , and it is qualitatively reminiscent of a P-Cygni profile.

A serendipitous spectrum of lower quality taken by XMM--Newton in 2015 qualitatively confirms the presence of similar Fe-K structures. Although it is not possible to distinguish between the two physical scenarios on sheer statistical grounds with the current data, several considerations lend weight to the possibility that Mrk 1513 is actually hosting a persistent outflow at accretion-disc scales, thus adding to the handful of known AGN in which a wide-angle X-ray wind has been identified so far.
Here we discuss the intriguing possibility that Mrk 1513 actually hosts even an ultra-fast disc wind.

sources.summary: The paper constrains several PG 2130+099 BLR parameters, including opening angle, inclination, and radius, while finding degeneracies and broad posteriors for others.

quotes.quote: PG 2130+099 Some of the parameters can be constrained: we find = pg2130_opening degrees, = pg2130_inc degrees and = pg2130_rmin light-days, consistent within the uncertainties with the full light-curve result. The parameter is found to be lower than zero, favouring scenarios in which the particles on the far side of the BLR contribute with more emission. However, this is not a strong constraint as the posterior probability distribution for this parameter has a tail extending to positive values, indicating possible degeneracies. However, the posterior distribution for from the full light-curve modelling is also broad. The remaining parameters have very broad posterior probability distributions (Fig. posterior_pg2130 ), and cannot be accurately constrained.

sources.summary: The paper presents a two-year high-cadence reverberation mapping campaign of PG 2130+099, measuring broad-line lags, BLR kinematics, black-hole mass, and a super-Eddington accretion rate.

quotes.quote: As one of the most interesting Seyfert 1 galaxies, PG 2130+099 has been the target of several reverberation mapping (RM) campaigns over the years. However, its measured broad H line responses have been inconsistent, with time lags of 200 days, 25 days, and 10 days being reported for different epochs while its optical luminosity changed no more than 40 . To investigate this issue, we conducted a new RM-campaign with homogenous and high cadence (about 3 days) for two years during 2017--2019 to measure the kinematics and structure of the ionized gas. We successfully detected time lags of broad , , , and \ lines with respect to the varying 5100 \ continuum, revealing a stratified structure that is likely virialized with Keplerian kinematics in the first year of observations, but an inflow kinematics of the broad-line region from the second year. With a central black hole mass of 0.97_ 0.18 ^ +0.15 10^7 , PG 2130+099 has an accretion rate of 10^ 2.1 0.5 L_ Edd ^ 2 , where L_ Edd is the Eddington luminosity and is speed of light, implying that it is a super-Eddington accretor and likely possesses a slim, rather than thin, accretion disk.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 1513 as a strong COS far-UV spectral example, noting intrinsic absorption, weaker detected lines, prominent Seyfert 1.5 emission lines, and a narrow-line, disk-dominated AGN spectrum.

quotes.quote: Several AGN exhibit narrow intrinsic absorption lines (e.g., Mrk 817, Mrk 290, Mrk 1513, PG 1115+407).

We also detect a number of weaker lines, known from atomic data and clearly seen in Figure 2 in high-S/N spectra of Mrk 817 and Mrk 1513. These include line blends of \ 1304 and \ 1308 , \ 1640 , ] 1605 , \ 1335 , \ 1085 , \ 1126 , and ] 1486 .

Figure 2 shows two outstanding examples of far-UV spectra taken by COS, labeling prominent emission lines in the low-redshift Seyfert 1.5 galaxies, Mrk 817 and Mrk 1513. According to the classification scheme proposed by Richards \ (2011), Mrk 817 is a ``wind-dominated AGN" with weak , , and \ emission lines and blue-shifted \ and ] . Mrk 1513 is a narrow-line Seyfert galaxy, exhibiting a ``disk-dominated" spectrum with prominent emission lines ( , , ).

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 2130+099 as having regular gas with an outer spiral-like structure, disk-like rotation, a flat rotation curve, and lower dispersion in the outer substructure.

quotes.quote: PG 2130+099: The regular gas distribution shows an outer spiral-like structure that matches the morphology seen in the HST/WFC3 broad-band image. Disk-like rotation can be seen from the LOS velocity map, which is sufficiently extended to allow the detection of the flat part of the rotation curve. We measure a projected peak-to-peak rotational velocity of v_ rot i 470 km s ^ 1 from the galaxy outskirts. The outer sub-structure seen at R 10 kpc presents systematically lower velocity dispersion compared to the central, more disky region.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 2130+099 as a quasar host with outer spiral-like clumpy H emission, star-forming clumps, a central disk-like region, and possible shocks.

quotes.quote: PG 2130+099: The H emission traces an outer spiral-like clumpy structure that extends out to the outskirts of the galaxy. The clumpy regions are consistently classified as star-forming zones. A central clumpy disk-like region is seen toward the center. The [ N ii ] /H map suggests ongoing star formation, although shocks cannot be completely discarded, according to the [ S ii ] /H map.

sources.summary: The atlas gives PG 2130+099's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PG 2130+099 is a borderline quasar/Seyfert object at a redshift of z= 0.063 (D 288 Mpc) with an optical classification as Sy 1.5 veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 in a highly-inclined peculiar spiral host galaxy. At radio wavelengths, it appears as a compact radio core with two off-nuclear compact sources at 1 1.2 kpc to the south-east and 1.5 1.8 kpc to the north-west (PA 135 ; kukula_radio_1998,leipski_radio_2006 ). Pioneering MIR observations of PG 2130+099 were performed by , followed by , elvis_atlas_1994 , , and .

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 2130+099/Mrk 1513 as a steep-spectrum source with triple FRI-type VLA structure and a core not detected in the authors' VLBA image.

quotes.quote: PG 2130+099 PG 2130+099 (Mrk 1513) showed a triple FRI-type structure in the VLA-A images, with a bright core straddled by two weaker steep-spectrum lobes and with an overall extent of 2.5 \ 1993MNRAS.263..425M,1994AJ....108.1163K,1998MNRAS.297..366K . The overall source has a steep spectral index of 2.32 between 1.4 and 8.4 GHz 1998MNRAS.297..366K,2006A&A...455..161L . The VLA core is not detected in our VLBA image and in .

sources.summary: The paper identifies PG2130+099 as an aligned triple radio source spanning about 2.5 arcseconds, with a north-west component whose transverse elongation may mark a shock.

quotes.quote: PG2130+099 . Another aligned triple radio source, but with well separated and clearly detected components (Fig 11, right). The overall extent is nearly 2 5 , orientated north--west/south--east. Especially the component to the north--west displays a remarkable sub--structure which is elongated perpendicular to the jet--direction, possibly indicating a transverse shock.

gmw_00000269.name: Pictor A
gmw_00000269.overview: Pictor A is a nearby Fanaroff-Riley II broad-line radio galaxy [arXiv:1311.4820, arXiv:1501.06054] that features an elliptical host galaxy [arXiv:1506.04758] and a relativistic jet [arXiv:1311.4820, arXiv:1405.6704]. The system exhibits a 60-kiloparsec tidal tail [arXiv:1506.04758] and displays long-term optical and gamma-ray variability [arXiv:1201.0034, arXiv:1003.5700].

sources.summary: The paper studies Pictor A as a Fanaroff-Riley II broad-line radio galaxy and candidate UHECR source, deriving proton-luminosity constraints from gamma-ray upper limits.

quotes.quote: Two particular sources Pictor A and NGC 7469 are studied in detail. Pictor A is a Fanarof-Riley II galaxy with high radio activity. Its jet is clearly visible by Chandra and it is oriented at a large angle with respect to Earth, therefore the gamma-ray emission is not expected to be high. The telescopes observed Pictor A for 7.9 h, no signal was detected and an upper limit on the integral flux of gamma-rays was obtained.

Pictor A Pictor A is an interesting Fanarof-Riley II broad emission line radio galaxy . Due to its relative proximity ( z=0.0342 ) it has been extensively observed in many wavelength ranges. Radio observations have shown a relativistic jet with rich hot spots in the termination shock region . Recently, Fermi-LAT has detected GeV gamma-rays (0.2 < E < 300 GeV) with a very low flux ( F_ 0.2 < E < 300 GeV = (5.8 0.7) 10^ 9 cm^ 2 s^ 1 ) coming from this source .

sources.summary: The thesis treats Pictor A as a nearby AGN galaxy with an unusually large relativistic jet and uses optical data to constrain emission models for that jet.

quotes.quote: Pictor A is a particularly interesting galaxy because it is both nearby (redshift, z , of .035), and features from its AGN are larger than similar sources. From the AGN core of Pictor A comes a jet of relativistically moving particles, which is about 1 kiloparsec ( kpc ) wide, and extends hundreds of kiloparsecs. For comparison, the nearby galaxy M87 ( z=.004 ), has a well-studied jet which is only parsecs wide, and extends only a few kiloparsecs. The combination of Pictor A's size and proximity means we are able to resolve multiple layers of substructure, probing a regime of high energy processes which are not well understood. In this thesis we will look at recent observations of Pictor A, and make inferences about the radiative processes which are present.

Conclusions Significance Early in the study of the jet of Pictor A, data strongly suggested that most of the observed flux was produced by non-thermal processes, such as synchrotron emission and inverse Compton scattering . The precise nature of this emission was unknown though -- even by the time of Wilson's x-ray data, the results were not strong enough to identify the emission mechanism; in particular, he noted that he could not even rule out an unbroken spectral power law connecting radio and x-ray fluxes. We have extended the understanding of Pictor A by obtaining optical data and using it to place meaningful constraints on the emission models of Pictor A's jet.

sources.summary: The paper presents HST optical detections of Pictor A's jet and newly found tidal tail, models its elliptical host-galaxy light, and argues the jet emission is synchrotron rather than boosted IC/CMB.

quotes.quote: New images from the Hubble Space Telescope of the FRII radio galaxy Pictor A reveal a previously undiscovered tidal tail, as well as a number of jet knots coinciding with a known X-ray and radio jet. The tidal tail is approximately 5 \ wide (3 kpc projected), starting 18 \ (12 kpc) from the center of Pictor A, and extends more than 90 \ (60 kpc).

Pictor A is an FR II radio galaxy at a redshift of 0.035 . (For H_0 = 70.5 km/s/Mpc, at this redshift, 1 \ corresponds to about 700 pc.) Pictor A's pencil-like X-ray jet was discovered in Chandra observations by Wilson01 , extending 1 9 from the core, oriented toward the resolved NW hotspot 4 2 from the core. The jet is barely visible in radio images due to its low brightness relative to the core and the extended lobes Perley97,1999ApJS..123..447S,Marshall10 .

The program galfit GALFIT was used to generate a model of the Pictor A host galaxy and also generate uncertainty maps. The generated models consisted of a PSF core (caused by the central AGN), along with 2 generalized profiles to account for the elliptical host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper maps Pictor A’s inner gas, finding a low-metallicity elongated feature with non-rotating kinematics that is photoionized by the AGN and likely tied to a past merger.

quotes.quote: We present optical integral field spectroscopy of the inner 2.5 3.4 kpc ^2 of the broad-line radio galaxy Pictor A, at a spatial resolution of 400 pc. Line emission is observed over the whole field-of-view, being strongest at the nucleus and in an elongated linear feature (ELF) crossing the nucleus from the south-west to the north-east along PA 70^ . Although the broad double-peaked H line and the [ O i ] 6300/H and [ S ii ] 6717+31/H ratios are typical of AGNs, the [ N ii ] 6584/H ratio ( 0.15-0.25 ) is unusually low.

We suggest that this is due to the unusually low metallicity of the gas. Centroid velocity maps show mostly blueshifts to the south and redshifts to the north of the nucleus, but the velocity field is not well fitted by a rotation model. Velocity dispersions are low ( < 100 ) along the ELF, ruling out a jet-cloud interaction as the origin of this structure. The ELF shows both blueshifts and redshifts in channel maps, suggesting that it is close to the plane of the sky. The ELF is evidently photoionized by the AGN, but its kinematics and inferred low metallicity suggest that this structure may have originated in a past merger event with another galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper reports NuSTAR, Chandra, and XMM-Newton observations of Pictor A's western hot spot, detecting hard X-rays and a featureless power-law spectrum that constrains synchrotron particle acceleration.

quotes.quote: Utilizing Chandra , XMM-Newton and NuSTAR , a wide-band X-ray spectrum through 0.2 to 20 keV is reported from the western hot spot of Pictor A. In particular, the X-ray emission is significantly detected in the 3 to 20 keV band at 30 sigma by NuSTAR . This is the first detection of hard X-rays with energies above 10 keV from a jet termination hot spot of active galactic nuclei. The hard X-ray spectrum is well described with a power-law model with a photon index of =1.8 0.2 , and the flux is obtained to be (4.5 0.4) 10^ 13 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in the 3 to 20 keV band. The obtained spectrum is smoothly connected with those soft X-ray spectra observed by Chandra and XMM-Newton .

Assuming the X-rays as synchrotron radiation of the electrons, the energy index of the electrons is estimated as p=2 1=3.14 0.06 from the wide-band spectrum. Given that the X-ray synchrotron emitting electrons quickly lose their initial energies via synchrotron radiation, the energy index of electrons at acceleration sites is estimated as p_ acc =p-1=2.14 0.06 . This is consistent with the prediction of the diffusive shock acceleration.

sources.summary: The paper treats Pictor A's western hotspot as a resolved radio-galaxy shock target and finds X-ray spectral steepening across the hotspot, with efficient electron acceleration at the upstream shock front.

quotes.quote: Among the other targets, the western (W) hotspot in the radio galaxy Pictor A is exceptionally well suited for deep observational studies, due to the combination of its relatively large angular size, very large angular separation from the bright galactic nucleus, and its high surface brightness. As such, it was subjected to a number of multiwavelength programs, including the radio domain with the Very Large Array VLA; Perley97 , the mid-infrared range with the IRAC camera onboard the Spitzer Space Telescope Werner12 , the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer WISE; Isobe17 , and the SPIRE camera of the Herschel Space Observatory Isobe20 , at optical wavelengths with the Faint Object Camera on the Hubble Space Telescope HST; Thomson95 , in X-rays with the Advanced CCD Imaging Spectrometer (ACIS) onboard the Chandra X-ray Observatory Wilson01,Hardcastle16,Thimmappa20 , as well as the EPIC MOS1 camera of the XMM- Newton Migliori07 , and lastly in hard X-rays with NuSTAR Sunada22b . The hotspot was also the target of high-resolution radio imaging by the Very Long Baseline Array VLBA; Tingay08 .

The resulting 0.5--7.0 keV photon index map, along with the electron index map of the W hotspot in Pictor A, corresponding to the 0.5 px resolution HR map discussed above, are given in the bottom panels of Figure fig:indices . It is however important to emphasize at this point, that the broad-band spectrum of ultra-relativistic electrons accelerated at the shock front, may be much more complex than a single power-law. A single power-law model is used here rather for illustrative purposes, to give a basic insight into the slope of the high-energy segment of the electron distribution, and the amount of spectral steepening observed across the shock front in the Pictor A hotspot. The gradient in the HR values across the terminal reverse shock we have found, has several important implications for understanding particle acceleration at relativistic shocks in general. First, the fact that the hardest X-ray spectra we see are concentrated at the upstream edge of the X-ray intensity peak, means that the efficient electron acceleration --- forming flat electron energy distributions with indices s 2.2 (when approximated by a single power-law) and maximum electron energies of the order 10-100 TeV --- takes place at the very front of the mildly-relativistic shock with perpendicular magnetic field configuration , and not in the far downstream, for example, where compact radio knots have been found in VLBA observations Tingay08 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Pictor A as the nearest broad-line radio galaxy with double lobes, a parsec-scale jet and low-mass black hole, finding TESS optical variability consistent with accretion-disc timescales.

quotes.quote: To this end, we have extracted and analyzed a high-cadence, high-precision optical light curve of the nearest broad-line radio galaxy (BLRG), Pictor A (PKS 0518-548), from the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS).

Studied extensively by , Pictor A has a compact, parsec-scale radio jet confirmed in VLBI imaging, and gas kinematics indicating a strong interaction of the radio jet with the interstellar medium. The galaxy is also a known gamma-ray emitter, with variable gamma-ray flux ( BrownAdams2012 ), and X-ray knots co-spatial with radio knots in the jet, which also show strong variability ( Marshall2010 ). The black hole mass in Pictor A is likely quite low, similar to the mass of Sagittarius A ^* , M_ BH  = 5.9   10^6 M_ Koss2022a .

As a first example, we used Quaver to extract the TESS light curves available for the radio-loud AGN galaxy Pictor A, and perform a variability analysis. The obtained light curves are well fit with a DRW model. We find that the source exhibits characteristic timescales _ DRW 3-6 days during periods when flares are not dominant, and _ DRW 1 day during periods with numerous flares, even when the flares are removed from the DRW analysis. The observed timescales are consistent with the dynamical, orbital and thermal timescales expected for the BH mass of Pictor A, as observed also in other sources by recent optical/UV variability studies.

sources.summary: The paper presents NuSTAR and Chandra observations of the FR II radio galaxy Pictor A, detecting its western hotspot above 10 keV and finding variability consistent with synchrotron emission rather than IC-CMB.

quotes.quote: Here we present NuSTAR and Chandra observations of the nearby powerful radio galaxy Pictor A, in which we clearly detect the western hot spot at 4' from the host galaxy, the first detection of a kpc-scale jet above 10 keV. The NuSTAR 3 20 keV spectrum is best fit by a powerlaw of index = 2.03 0.04 . Using a maximum likelihood model, we confirm previous reports of variations in the soft X-ray flux detected by Chandra over the 2000 to 2015 period, at a 6.7 level, higher than the 3.4 significance level. This rises to >8 in the common 3-8 keV band using the full 22-year span of Chandra and NuSTAR observations. The variability of Pictor A rules out IC-CMB as the source of the X-ray emission, and the NuSTAR observations are in keeping with a synchrotron origin for the X-ray emission from the western hotspot of Pictor A, with no sign of a cutoff to the flat spectrum.

Given these limitations, the outer jet and western hot spot of FR II radio galaxy Pictor A ( z = 0.035 ; 0.69 kpc/") is nearly the only jet with the sufficient angular separation ( 4' ) from the core to be a viable target for hard X-ray study and the only one bright enough to be detected by NuSTAR in less than approximately 1 Ms of exposure. Pictor A is among the most well-studied X-ray jets in the literature, and has been the subject of a number of detailed studies over the past two decades e.g., WILSON2001,HARDCASTLE2004,HARDCASTLE2005,MIGLIORI2007,TINGAY2008,MARSHALL2010,HARDCASTLE2016,THIMMAPPA2020 .
In this paper, we report the results of a comprehensive X-ray spectral analysis of the western hotspot of Pictor A, including new and archival NuSTAR observations totalling 348 ks.

sources.summary: The paper presents XMM-Newton observations of Pictor A, finding non-thermal inverse-Compton X-ray emission from both FRII radio lobes and a magnetic-field discrepancy from equipartition.

quotes.quote: X-ray emission from the eastern radio lobe of the FRII Radio Galaxy Pictor A was serendipitously discovered by a short observation of XMM-Newton in 2001. The X-ray spectrum, accumulated on a region covering about half of the entire radio lobe, was well described by both a thermal model and a power law, making non-univocal the physical interpretation. A new XMM-Newton observation performed in 2005 has allowed the detection of the X-ray emission from both radio lobes and unambiguously revealed its non-thermal origin. The X-ray emission is due to Inverse Compton (IC) of the cosmic microwave background photons by relativistic electrons in the lobe. We confirm the discrepancy between the magnetic field, as deduced from the comparison of the IC X-ray and radio fluxes, and the equipartition value.

Pictor A is a nearby ( z =0.035) double lobe radio source with a Fanaroff Riley II (FRII) morphology. VLA observations (Perley et al. 1997) show two nearly circular radio lobes with hot spots and a faint radio jet connecting the nucleus to the west hot spot. The spectral indices are fairly uniform throughout each lobe < _ R >=0.8 . XMM-Newton discovered X-ray radiation associated to the extended radio lobes (Grandi et al. 2003).

sources.summary: The paper reports Fermi LAT gamma-ray detection of the BLRG Pictor A, finds possible yearly variability, and argues its gamma rays likely originate in the jet rather than the western hot spot SSC component.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of high-energy ray emission from the Broad Line Radio Galaxy (BLRG) Pictor A with a significance of 5.8 (TS =33.4 ), based on three years of observations with the Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) detector. The three-year averaged E>0.2 GeV ray spectrum is adequately described by a power-law, with a photon index, , of 2.93 0.03 and a resultant integrated flux of F_ =(5.8 0.7) 10^ 9 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . A temporal investigation of the observed ray flux, which binned the flux into year long intervals, reveals that the flux in the third year was 50 higher than the three-year average flux.

Pictor A's year-binned light curve suggested variability in the ray flux, with the third year flux level being 50 larger than the three year average. However, further observations are needed to confirm this observational property. SSC modelling of the WHS SED found that it was only possible to account for the ray emission with an SSC model at the expense of greatly over-predicting the flux at X-ray energies. Given that the X-ray emission in the WHS has been resolved with the current suite of X-ray detectors, we suggest that the ray emission from Pictor A originates from within its jet, in agreement with other BLRGs detected with Fermi .

sources.summary: Pictor A is presented as a nearby FR-II radio galaxy whose brightness, isolation, compact apparent size, and improved 100 MHz-2 GHz power-law spectrum make it a strong EoR calibrator.

quotes.quote: The brightest and least-resolved calibrator in this region is Pictor A (5h19m49.1,-45d46m45.0). Pictor A is a nearby FR-II type radio galaxy similar to Cygnus A. At 400Jy, Pictor is bright and sufficiently distant from other bright sources to make it eminently suitable as both a phase and flux calibrator. Its apparent size of 8 is smaller than the scales being probed by current EoR instruments, making it suitable for precision calibration, with only a modest level of resolution effects.

Of particular importance is the new Pictor A flux model, which is accurate to 1.4 and shows, in contrast to previous models, that between 100 MHz and 2 GHz, the spectrum of Pictor A is consistent with a single power law given by a flux at 150 MHz of 382 5.4 Jy, and a spectral index of -0.76 0.01. This accuracy represents an order of magnitude improvement over previous measurements in this band, and is limited by the uncertainty in the catalog measurements used to estimate the absolute flux scale. The simplicity and improved accuracy of Pictor A's spectrum make it an excellent calibrator in a band of importance to experiments seeking to measure emission from the Epoch of Reionization.

sources.summary: Deep Chandra and ATCA data show Pictor A's jet and hotspot vary in X-rays, support synchrotron jet emission over inverse-Compton models, and confirm inverse-Compton lobe spectra shaped by magnetic-field structure.

quotes.quote: We report on deep Chandra observations of the nearby broad-line radio galaxy Pictor A, which we combine with new Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) observations. The new X-ray data have a factor 4 more exposure than observations previously presented and span a 15-year time baseline, allowing a detailed study of the spatial, temporal and spectral properties of the AGN, jet, hotspot and lobes. We present evidence for further time variation of the jet, though the flare that we reported in previous work remains the most significantly detected time-varying feature. We also confirm previous tentative evidence for a faint counterjet.

Based on the radio through X-ray spectrum of the jet and its detailed spatial structure, and on the properties of the counterjet, we argue that inverse-Compton models can be conclusively rejected, and propose that the X-ray emission from the jet is synchrotron emission from particles accelerated in the boundary layer of a relativistic jet. For the first time, we find evidence that the bright western hotspot is also time-varying in X-rays, and we connect this to the small-scale structure in the hotspot seen in high-resolution radio observations. The new data allow us to confirm that the spectrum of the lobes is in good agreement with the predictions of an inverse-Compton model and we show that the data favour models in which the filaments seen in the radio images are predominantly the result of spatial variation of magnetic fields in the presence of a relatively uniform electron distribution.

sources.summary: The paper studies Pictor A as a nearby broad-line radio galaxy, resolving its core and western hotspot and modeling its ultraviolet to gamma-ray emission.

quotes.quote: Pictor A is a nearby BLRG Danziger1977 located at a redshift of z = 0.035 . The core and the primary hotspot of Pictor A, the western hotspot (WHS), are resolved in radio, infrared, optical and X-ray bands. The proximity and brightness of Pictor A makes it an ideal candidate for spatial study of the large scale jets in FR II sources. A detailed study of Pictor A in the radio band revealed parsec-scale jet structure near core emission and complex compact components in WHS e. g. Perley1997, Tingay2000,Tingay2008 .

A comprehensive study of the nucleus and western hotspot of Pictor A is carried out using AstroSat observations, 13 years of Fermi , and archival Swift observations, along with other published data. We report the first detection of the western hotspot of Pictor A in the far-UV band using observations from AstroSat UVIT. The broad-band SED of the western hotspot is explained by a multizone emission scenario where X-ray emission is caused by synchrotron emission process in the substructures embedded in the diffuse region, while the emission in radio to optical is caused by synchrotron emission process in the diffuse region.

sources.summary: The thesis images Pictor A’s northwest hot spot at the highest VLBI resolution then argues parsec-scale structures imply shocks and synchrotron-dominated Chandra-band X-rays.
