quotes.quote: Pictor A The first VLBI image of the north-west hot spot of Pictor A ( z=0.035 ) has been made using wide-field VLBI techniques. The image is the highest spatial resolution image (16 pc), by a factor of three, of such an object to date. The north-west hot spot of Pictor A is resolved into a complex set of components that coincide with the bright part of the hot spot imaged at arcsecond-scales with the VLA at radio wavelengths and with the HST at optical wavelengths. The detection of parsec-scale structures in radio galaxy hot spots has implications for the models used to explain the X-ray emission from the hot spot.

In contrast, our observations suggest that the small-scale structures indicate regions of strong shocks in the fluid flow and so represent regions of recently accelerated electrons, which have higher break frequencies than the hot spot electrons on larger spatial scales. A consequence of this is that the X-rays in the Chandra band are dominated by synchrotron X-rays, with a relatively weak SSC contribution.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Chandra and VLA data for Pictor A's eastern radio lobe, emphasizing X-ray features, radio correlations, and a newly identified filament near the jet termination region.

quotes.quote: Here we present detailed analysis of the distinct X-ray emission features present within the Eastern radio lobe of the Pictor A galaxy, around the jet termination region, utilising the data obtained from the Chandra X-ray Observatory. Various emission features have been selected for the study based on their enhanced X-ray surface brightness, including five sources that appear point-like, as well as three extended regions, one characterised by a filamentary morphology. For those, we perform a basic spectral analysis within the 0.5--7 keV range. We also investigate various correlations between the X-ray emission features and the non-thermal radio emission, utilising the high-resolution radio maps from the Very Large Array at GHz frequencies.

Pictor A, classified as a Broad-Line Radio Galaxy (BLRG) with the ``classical double'' (Fanaroff-Riley type II) large-scale radio morphology Simkin99 , and located at the redshift z=0.035 Eracleous04 , is one of the most prominent radio galaxies in the sky, that has become the prime target for detailed multiwavelength investigations in the recent decades, from radio to the X-ray ranges. More recently, it has been also confirmed as a source of high-energy rays in the Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) all-sky survey Kataoka11,Brown12,Ackermann15 .

sources.summary: The review describes Pictor A as a nearby FRII radio galaxy with a long jet and counterjet, fading X-ray knot, short synchrotron cooling time, and a distinct second X-ray component.

quotes.quote: It was thus surprising when examined the case of the nearby ( z = 0.035 ) FRII Pictor A, a radio galaxy with a very long (arcminute-scale) radio jet and steep radio core, and a clearly visible counterjet. A knot in the jet of Pictor A was seen to fade over a timescale of a few years with a reported significance of 3.4 . and later confirmed these results at similar significances ( p < 0.011). While variability had been observed by this point in nearby FRI jets most dramatically in the mid-2000s outburst of HST-1 in the jet of M87; harris2003 variability on this timescale was completely unexpected. In the case of Pictor A, assuming minimum energy conditions and an emitting region the size of the jet cross-section, the synchrotron cooling timescale is on the order of 1200 years, implying that the X-rays arise from a region much smaller and with a magnetic field several times higher than equipartition.

With the exception of Pictor A, variability has not been reported for any other source in our sample of 53. Unlike the jets of M87 and Centaurus A, however, the X-ray emission in Pictor A is clearly from a second emission component hardcastle2005 .

sources.summary: The paper monitors Pictor A as one of seven double-peaked AGNs, finding broad H emission-line variability driven by excess-emission lumps that change shape and drift over years.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present results for seven objects whose long-term variability has not been studied in any detail: 3C 59, 1E 0450.3--1817, Pictor A, CBS 74, PKS 0921--213, PKS 1020--103, and PKS 1739+18C.
The primary goal of this paper is to characterize the observed variability patterns in these seven objects in a model-independent way.

In this paper we have characterized, in a model-independent way, the variability of the broad, double-peaked H emission lines in seven objects (Pictor A, PKS 0921--213, 1E 0450.3--1817, CBS 74, 3C 59, PKS 1739+18, and PKS 1020--103) which have been monitored over the past decade. As indicated by the rms profiles, the greatest variability occurs at larger projected velocities, and in particular many objects show a peak in the rms profile at large negative projected velocities. Difference spectra showed that the variability is caused primarily by discrete lumps of excess emission that change in morphology and amplitude on timescales of a few years and drift in projected velocity on longer timescales.

sources.summary: The paper uses Pictor A as its flux calibrator, noting that it is least resolved, poorly characterized at 100-200 MHz, and affected by inconsistent low-frequency flux measurements.

quotes.quote: Pictor A is chosen as a calibrator source of necessity; given the grating lobes of the synthesized beam generated by our maximum redundancy array configuration, sidelobes of Pictor A and Fornax A dominate nearly all beam pointings. Of these two sources, Pictor A is the least resolved. Unfortunately, the spectrum of Pictor A is particularly poorly characterized in the 100--200-MHz band where PAPER operates. Previous measurements imply that the spectrum below 400 MHz deviates substantially from the power law (with spectral index =-0.85 ) that holds at higher frequencies perley_et_al1997 . There are also serious inconsistencies in the measured flux of Pictor A below 1 GHz (e.g. slee1995 , kuhr_et_al1981 , large_et_al1981 , burgess_hunstead2006 ), and a dearth of measurements at nearby frequencies in order to establish the spectral slope of Pictor A in this band.

sources.summary: The paper describes Pictor A as an FR II radio galaxy at z = 0.035 whose west-lobe hot spot has multiwavelength synchrotron emission and a WISE mid-infrared excess requiring another electron population.

quotes.quote: The FR II radio galaxy Pictor A, located at the red shift of z = 0.035 eracleous04 , is known to host a distinctive radio hot spot at the edge of its west lobe e.g., perley97 . It is known as a representative of the low-luminosity hot spots hardcastle04 . Thanks to its high radio intensity, the object has been extensively studied not only in the radio band, but also in the near infrared meisenheimer97 , optical thomson95 and X-ray e.g., wilson01,hardcastle16 frequencies. The high resolution radio image derived with the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) resolved the hot spot into pc-scale sub structures tingay08 . The hot spot is included in the \ hot-spot sample by werner12 .

sources.summary: Pictor A is treated as one of the most significant local sources in S11 fits, important at the highest energies and capable of explaining the data with Hydra A.

quotes.quote: Surprisingly, also Centaurus A contributes only a negligible fraction to the total UHECR intensity, especially at high energies, while the most significant local sources on average are Virgo A, Fornax A, Pictor A, and Hydra A. The latter two are important in particular at the highest energies, E 100 EeV . The relative dominance between those four sources depends significantly on the EGMF strength and their lifetimes.
We can conclude that a local source sample consisting of either Hydra A and Pictor A or Virgo A/ 3C 270 and Fornax A is sufficient to explain the data.

Including more local sources---in particular at Galactic longitudes between 90 \ and 180 --leads to a multitude of possible scenarios where, e.g., Hydra A, Pictor A or Centaurus B can become the dominating UHECR sources without improving the fit significantly.

sources.summary: The paper describes Pictor A as a radio source with a straight unilateral jet, central black hole, measured redshift, viewing angle estimate, and selected HST knots.

quotes.quote: Pictor A: Pictor A has an unilateral and straight jet in the radio and energy bands Gentry2015 . This source harbors a SMBH of mass 4.0 10^ 7 Ito2021 . The measured redshift of the host galaxy is z = 0.0304 (1 = 0.697 \ kpc Hardcastle2016 . Tingay2000 have estimated a viewing angle 51 based on Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) observations. We select three knots, HST-32, HST-106, and HST-112, with radio, optical, and data taken from Gentry2015 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Pictor A as the strongest hotspot acceleration case, noting extended polarized optical synchrotron emission, co-spatial Chandra X-rays, and X-ray properties not explained by simple radio-optical extrapolation.

quotes.quote: By far the strongest case for in-situ acceleration reported in the literature is that of the western lobe of the radio galaxy Pictor A (Table 1). In this lobe, the linearly polarized optical synchrotron radiation is seen to extend up to 10 kpc from the hotspot (Perley et al. \ ; also, Thomson et al. 1995; R oser Meisenheimer 1987) and broadly co-spatial X-ray emission (of poorly understood origin) has recently been detected by Wilson et al. \ ( ) using CHANDRA . As noted by these authors, the level of X-ray emission is much above a simple extrapolation of the radio-optical spectrum, which shows a clear break in the near-IR region. Hence the X-ray flux cannot be synchrotron radiation from the same electron population. At the same time, inverse-Compton upscattering of the CMB photons by this electron population is also an unlikely explanation for the X-ray emission, for the X-ray spectral slope is found to be significantly different from that of the radio spectrum of the hotspot (Wilson et al. \ ).

sources.summary: The paper models Pictor A's western hot spot, arguing that its bar-shaped filament constrains the jet to lie nearly in the sky plane and results from jet surging driven by cocoon backflow.

quotes.quote: We compare these rendered images with observed features of Pictor A. A remarkable feature of Pictor A observations is a bar-shaped ``filament'' inclined almost at right angles to the inferred jet direction and extending 24'' ( 10.8 h^ 1 kpc ) along its longest axis. The constraints of reproducing the appearance of this structure in simulations indicate that the jet of Pictor A lies nearly in the plane of the sky. The results of the simulation are also consistent with other features found in the radio image of Pictor A. This filament arises from the surging behaviour of the jet near the hot-spot; the surging is provoked by alternate compression and decompression of the jet by the turbulent backflow in the cocoon.

sources.summary: The paper treats Pictor A as a radio hot-spot case where equipartition fields exceed radio/X-ray estimates; the revised formula reduces, but does not remove, the discrepancy.

quotes.quote: This is the case in the lobes of PKS 1343-601 (Tashiro et al. \ ) and 3C219 (Brunetti et al. \ ), and in the hot spots of Pictor A (Wilson et al. \ ), 3C351 (Brunetti et al. \ ) and 3C351 (Hardcastle et al. \ ). Dominating protons ( K 1 ) would even increase the discrepancy.

For example, in the bright core of the western hot spot of Pictor A, Wilson et al. ( ) found the largest discrepancy (more than 10) between the field strength derived from the classical equipartition formula ( 470 G, assuming K =1 ) and that from the radio/X-ray intensity ratio ( 33 G). Our revised formula (for K_0 =1 ) reduces the equipartition value only to 350 G, not enough to remove the discrepancy. If cosmic ray protons dominate, the equipartition field strength increases to 1 mG (for K_0 =100 and =0.8 ), so that the discrepancy between the field estimates increases further.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Pictor A's Chandra and XMM-Newton X-ray emission, interpreting its lobes, hotspot, jet, and possible counterjet in terms of inverse-Compton and synchrotron processes.

quotes.quote: We discuss the extended X-ray emission seen in three archival Chandra observations, and one archival XMM-Newton observation, of the FRII radio galaxy Pictor A. The overall properties of the X-ray lobes are consistent with the conclusions of earlier workers that the extended X-ray emission is largely due to the inverse-Compton process, and the implied departure from equipartition is in the range seen by us in other sources. In detail, we show that the X-ray/radio flux ratio varies quite strongly as a function of position throughout the source, and we discuss possible implications of this observation for the spatial variation of electron energy spectra and magnetic field strength through the lobe. We show that the radio and X-ray properties of the lobe are not consistent with a simple model in which variations in the magnetic field strength alone are responsible for the observed differences between emission at different frequencies. We also discuss the origins of the extended emission seen around the eastern hotspot, arguing that it may be diffuse synchrotron radiation tracing a region of distributed particle acceleration, and the implications of a possible weak X-ray counterjet detection which, taken together with the other properties of the bright X-ray jet, leads us to suggest that the X-ray jet and possible counterjet are also produced by synchrotron emission.

sources.summary: The paper presents Pictor A as a useful extragalactic polarisation calibrator, with WMAP 23 GHz polarised flux consistent with prior whole-source measurements.

quotes.quote: Pictor A has been identified as a suitable extragalactic polarisation calibrator for the Planck Low Frequency Instrument (LFI) because of its position (in the region of the Ecliptic Pole where the satellite scans once per minute) and its lack of variability from the hot spots that dominate the polarised signal (Burke-Spolaor et al. 2009).

In addition, a detection of polarised flux density at 23 GHz in the WMAP maps has also been obtained for Pictor A. It is among the targets observed in mosaic mode by Burke-Spolaor et al. (2009), where it was identified as the best polarisation calibrator among the extragalactic sources for arcmin resolution experiments (and in particular for the Planck experiment), despite its steep spectrum in the region 1-20 GHz. The detected value of 457 35 mJy at 23 GHz (listed in Table Table:wmap ) is comparable with the 500 60 mJy measured for polarised flux density at 18 GHz by Burke-Spolaor et al. (2009) over the whole source (listed in Table Table:fullTable and used in the following analysis).

sources.summary: The paper uses Pictor A to set PAPER's flux calibration, describing imaging of the Pictor field, Gaussian fitting of the Pictor A source, snapshot-averaged spectra, and channel-scale calibration uncertainty.

quotes.quote: An imaging-based flux density calibration was also applied using Pictor A fluxes derived from jacobs_et_al2013 .

The original absolute calibration reported in ali_et_al2015 was done by imaging the Pictor field (at LST =4 h) in each channel, correcting for a primary beam model and fitting a Gaussian to the extracted Pictor A source. This was done in ten minute snapshots with the resulting spectra averaged together. The standard deviation of the flux estimate was of order 25 Jy at 68 confidence on each channel.

sources.summary: The paper treats Pictor A as a bright, extended source omitted from GLEAM, attenuated by HERA's primary beam, still visible at a few Jy, and adequately represented by a point-source model for Phase I.

quotes.quote: Note that the GLEAM catalogue does not include bright, extended sources like Fornax A and Pictor A. As shown in fig:gsm_fields , the calibration fields are chosen such that these sources are heavily attenuated by the primary beam, but even still these sources can be seen at the level of a few Jansky for the 02-hour and 05-hour fields, for example. Fornax A and Pictor A can be included in the component list model for the GLEAM-02H and GLEAM-05H fields, respectively, by adopting point source models with spectral indices informed by recent low-frequency studies Jacobs2013, McKinley2015 . Although these sources have a non-zero angular extent to them, for HERA Phase I angular resolutions a point source model is a fair approximation.

sources.summary: The paper uses Pictor A as a comparison case where X-ray flaring occurred in the extended jet tens of kiloparsecs from the core, but below core luminosity.

quotes.quote: Another example is the X-ray flaring activity observed in the extended jet of Pictor A that originated at about 48 arcsec ( 33 kpc) from the core, indicating that variability can be observed in the outer regions of relativistic jets marshall10,hardcastle16a .

An X-ray flux increase was observed in the jet of Pictor A at about 35 kpc from the core marshall10,hardcastle16a . However, in this case the enhancement did not achieve the luminosity of the core of Pictor A.

We notice that at the redshift of PKS 1127 145 the X-ray flaring component observed in the jet of Pictor A would be at about 4 arcsec from the core (i.e. closer than component O), while M87 HST-1 would be at 12 milliarcsecond from the core, in the innermost pc-scale structure.

sources.summary: The paper highlights Pictor A west as a difficult hotspot case where the synchrotron spectrum cuts off below the X-rays and SSC modeling requires a very low magnetic field.

quotes.quote: A particular intriguing case is the west hot spot of Pictor A (Wilson et al. 2001). The synchrotron spectrum shows an abrupt cut--off clearly indicated by a large number of optical data points and it falls orders of magnitude below the X--ray flux. On the other hand, the SSC interpretation would require a magnetic field strength about a factor 14 below the equipartition value to match the observed X--ray flux. In addition, the Chandra X--ray spectrum of the hot spot is relatively steep ( 1.1 ) and it is poorly fitted by the spectrum expected in case of SSC emission ( 0.74 ).

sources.summary: The atlas gives Pictor A's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: Pictor A is a radio-loud inclined lenticular galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0351 ( D 161 Mpc) with an FR II radio morphology. It contains an AGN optically classified as a borderline Sy 1.5/LINER veron-cetty_catalogue_1998,tueller_swift_2008 that belongs to the nine-month BAT AGN sample. Pictor A features two well-studied giant radio lobes extending 0.5 Mpc from the nucleus.

sources.summary: The catalog lists Pictor A among FR II broad-lined radio galaxies, marks it as a new LAT detection, and contrasts its result with a previous tentative detection.

quotes.quote: It joins the other two FR type-II sources listed in Table tab:magn : 3FGL J1442.6+5156 (3C 303) and 3FGL J0519.2 4542 (Pictor A). The latter are also broad-lined radio galaxies (BLRGs), and are new detections. The LAT detection of Pictor A was reported by The results of are in tension with those presented here, with their reported statistical uncertainties being very small given the low source flux. following a previous tentative detection kat11 .

sources.summary: The paper flags Pictor A as an exception: its hotspot SED is not fit by the single-zone models and may require a second high-energy electron population.

quotes.quote: Their SEDs can be fitted with the single-zone leptonic models, except for the hotspot of Pictor A and six knots of 3C 273.

Note that the X-ray spectra of the hotspot in Pictor A and six knots in 3C 273 are also soft with _ X >1 , but their SEDs from radio to X-ray bands cannot be represented by one synchrotron radiation component, and thus their X-ray emission may be produced by the synchrotron radiation of a second electron population with very high energy (e.g., Zhang et al. 2009; Zargaryan et al. 2017; Sun et al. 2017). We thus ignore the hotspot of Pictor A and the six knots of 3C 273 in our following analysis.

sources.summary: The review contrasts Pictor A with Cygnus A, emphasizing its one-sided X-ray jet, optical jet-side hotspot, SSCE flux deficit, and beaming-oriented hotspot interpretation.

quotes.quote: While Chandra observations of Cygnus A confirmed the SSCE picture , observations of Pictor A, another nearby powerful galaxy showed a very different picture: a.) an one-sided large scale X-ray jet on the same direction with the known VLBI jet ; b.) Detection of X-ray emission only from the hotspot on the jet side, which was also detected in the optical. In addition, SSCE models for the Pictor A hotspots underproduce the observed X-ray flux, requiring a magnetic field 14 times below its the equipartition value in order to achieve agreement with the observed the X-ray flux. The one-sided X-ray jet of Pictor A suggests the potential importance of relativistic beaming and orientation as a discriminant of the hotspot properties of these two sources.

sources.summary: The paper keeps Pic A as a broad-line radio galaxy comparison object with a likely synchrotron X-ray jet and unusually nearby status relative to the quasar sample.

quotes.quote: The final sample consisted of 9 objects of which all but one (Pic A) were quasars: Pic A is of course a broad-line radio galaxy and so probably a low-luminosity counterpart of a quasar. Although there are good arguments that the jet in Pic A is synchrotron in orgin (Wilson, Young Shopbell 2001; Hardcastle Croston 2005) I retained it in the sample, as it is the only low-redshift, lobe-dominated source known to show a strong linear X-ray jet of the type seen in CDQs, and so provides a useful comparison object.

The powers implied by this calculation, which of course assumes no protons are present in the jet, are of the order of 10^ 38 - 10^ 39 W (except in the case of the nearby Pic A), which are reasonable (cf. \ the jet powers derived on similar minimum-energy assumptions by Rawlings Saunders 1991).

gmw_00000270.name: PKS 1417-19
gmw_00000270.overview: PKS 1417-19 is a radio-loud galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.1200 [arXiv:1310.2770] and hosted by a Seyfert 1.5 nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770]. The galaxy features a biconical large-scale radio morphology with a north-east jet [arXiv:1310.2770], and it has been identified as a candidate compact symmetric object [arXiv:astro-ph/0407010].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1419 1928 as a galaxy flagged as a candidate compact symmetric object, but not clearly classifiable as a GPS or CSS source from its ATCA spectrum.

quotes.quote: J1419 1928: This galaxy has been noted as a candidate CSO by based on their 5 and 15 GHz imaging. As noted by , while there is some overlap between CSOs (defined by their morphology) and GPS/CSS sources (defined largely by their spectra) there is no clear correspondence between the two classes. Certainly the spectrum based on the ATCA data in Fig. Fig5 would not invite classification as either a GPS or CSS source. The higher PMN flux density of 1.021 Jy at 4.85 GHz gri94 , together with the `e' structure flag, suggest the source is partially resolved out on the 6 km ATCA baseline.

sources.summary: The atlas gives PKS 1417-19's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PKS 1417-19/LEDA 51213 is a radio-loud galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.1200 ( D 791 Mpc) with a Sy 1.5 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It possesses a biconical large-scale radio morphology with a jet along the north-east direction (PA 20 ; antonucci_vla_1985 ). PKS 1417-19 has not been detected with , and no observations are available.

gmw_00000271.name: PKS 1814-63
gmw_00000271.overview: PKS 1814-63 is a radio-loud, disk-dominated lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:2308.10071] with a Seyfert 2 nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770] and a warped, edge-on morphology indicating a recent merger [arXiv:1204.1391]. Classified as a compact steep-spectrum radio source [arXiv:2308.10071], it features a young radio jet [arXiv:2305.11263] and a central dust lane [arXiv:1310.2770].

sources.summary: The paper re-detects strong H I absorption in compact steep-spectrum source J181934--634548 and describes its warped edge-on host as evidence for recent merger activity.

quotes.quote: The previously known associated absorption system J181934--634548 (also known as PKS 1814--637) was observed in both sub-samples, as a reliability check of the system and data reduction pipeline.

J181934--634548: This compact steep spectrum source Tzioumis:2002 has a previously known strong absorption feature Veron-Cetty:2000,Morganti:2001 and was studied extensively at multiple wavelengths and angular scales by . In particular the host galaxy is known to have a strong edge-on disc morphology with heavily warped edges, consistent with recent merger activity. This is not easily apparent upon inspection of the blue optical image shown in Fig. figure:source_results , due to the presence of a bright coincident star.

We re-detect the absorption feature in both of our February and April observations, with derived peak and integrated optical depths that are consistent within the error. We estimate a peak observed optical depth of 0.193 0.001 and an integrated optical depth of 11.92 0.05 km s ^ 1 . The absorption line feature is best fit by four Gaussians, consisting of a deep, narrow component (combination of two Gaussians; v _ FWHM 20 km s ^ 1 and v _ FWHM 50 km s ^ 1 ), a shallow, broad component ( v _ FWHM 200 km s ^ 1 ), and a small redshifted component ( v _ FWHM 20 km s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1814--637 as a small CSS radio galaxy with a bright optical identification, narrow-line radio-galaxy classification, and a near-UV object likely associated with the radio source.

quotes.quote: 1814--637 : VLBI imaging shows a two component CSS galaxy e.g., Tzioumis02 . Although the radio source is small ( 0.4 , 0.3 kpc) its radio spectrum does not peak at 1 GHz. The optical identification corresponds to a m _v =18.0 galaxy at z = 0.063 Wall85 . We observe a bright, 16.38 magnitude point source. Danziger79 describe 1814--637 as a narrow line radio galaxy with a bright star-like nucleus.
The UV object is 2 arcsec to the Northeast of the radio source Ma98 . However, the registration between HST and radio frames can be off by as much as 1 , so it is probably the counterpart.

sources.summary: J181934-634548 has the survey's largest X-ray hydrogen column density among H i detections, a high neutral-gas spin-temperature limit, the highest 1.4 GHz flux density, and both deep and shallow H i components.

quotes.quote: J181934-634548 meanwhile has a considerably higher value of N _ H = 2.1 10 ^ 22 cm ^ 2 . This supports the idea that absorbed X-ray sources are potentially good tracers of H i content within the host galaxy or near the AGN.

In considering the two sources with H i detections, J181934-634548 has the largest column density of hydrogen in our sample. We obtain a high upper limit on its spin temperature of T_ spin < 961 K for its neutral hydrogen content.

We note that it is easier to detect H i absorption features against brighter radio galaxies, including shallow broad components with low optical depth typically offset from the spectroscopic redshift. This is seen in J181934-634548 (Fig. 4), which has the highest flux density of our sample at 1.4 GHz and has both deep and shallow H i components.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 1814 637 as a CSS source proposed to be young, marginally resolved at full resolution, with larger-scale symmetric north-south structure and no trackable jet component.

quotes.quote: 1814 637 This CSS source has been proposed as a putative young radio source and references therein 2010A&A...519A..45O . At full resolution, it shows a compact, marginally resolved component (see Fig. fig:wallpaper ). Using tapering, i.e., down-weighting the visibilities from the longest baselines, it is possible to recover possible emission on larger scales. In this case, we detect a symmetric structure in the north-south direction, reminiscent of classic CSOs (see Appendix app:maps ). No contemporaneous 22 GHz data are available for this source.
1814 637 The source is only marginally resolved in most of the epochs, therefore it is not possible to track any jet component.

sources.summary: The paper describes 5BZU 1819 6345 as a powerful, sometimes radio-galaxy-classified emitter with lobes, a young radio jet, Seyfert-like properties, and possible neutrino-production features.

quotes.quote: The latter, i.e. 5BZU 1819 6345, is a peculiar powerful radio emitter ODea:1990,Labiano:2007 , sometimes classified as a radio galaxy. In addition to a central compact radio component, it displays two lobes and, it may host a young radio-jet that is starting to expand through the near-nuclear interstellar medium ISM, Santoro:2020 . In such case, we might be witnessing an initially relativistic jet that is drilling its way out, and that is rapidly decelerated by collisions in a dense surrounding broad line region or the ISM Morganti:2011 . The radio power of 5BZU 1819 6345 (P _ 5GHz 10^ 25 W Hz ^ 1 ) is two orders of magnitude higher than the most powerful radio Seyfert galaxy, i.e. NGC 1068 (P _ 5GHz 10^ 23 W Hz ^ 1 ).

Both 5BZU J1647 + 4950 and 5BZU 1819 6345 show properties common to Seyfert-like galaxies. They are located in the nearby universe (within 200 Mpc and 280 Mpc, respectively), and present remarkable features, ideal to foster the production of high-energy neutrinos, e.g. presence of AGN-driven outflow and/or corona activity, prominent jet-cloud interaction, among others.

sources.summary: The atlas gives PKS 1814-63's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PKS 1814-63/LEDA 329451 is a radio-loud disturbed edge-on lenticular galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0627 ( D 302 Mpc) with a Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 and a dust lane crossing the galaxy's centre ramos_almeida_optical_2011 . The nucleus is extended in the north-south direction in subarcsecond-resolution radio observations and has a steep radio spectrum (e.g. tzioumis_sample_2002 ).

sources.summary: G4Jy 1472 is described as a rare disk-dominated powerful radio-loud AGN, classified as a compact steep-spectrum source and hard X-ray source with several aliases.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1472: is the rare case of a a powerful radio-loud AGN in disk dominated galaxy with radio luminosity similar to powerful FR II radio galaxies morganti11 . G4Jy 1472 (a.k.a. PKS 1814-63 and PMN J1819-6345) is classified as a CSS radio source see e.g., and references therein morganti97 with a z =0.06412 thompson90,tadhunter93,holt08,morganti11 , a similar redshift estimate to that reported by Danziger Goss (1979) and Grandi (1983). It is also a hard X-ray source maselli10 listed in the Roma-BZCAT a.k.a. BZU J1819-6345 massaro15b .

gmw_00000272.name: PKS 1932-46
gmw_00000272.overview: PKS 1932-46 is an FR II radio galaxy hosted by an elliptical galaxy with a Seyfert 1.9 nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770] and a broad-line radio galaxy classification [arXiv:2308.10071]. It exhibits a disturbed optical morphology and a complex gas distribution driven by a merger or interaction with a nearby companion [arXiv:2308.10071, arXiv:1310.2770].

sources.summary: G4Jy 1558 is described as a broad-line FR II radio galaxy with merger-driven gas, a large emission-line nebula, infrared luminosity, and group-scale star-forming debris.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1558: is a Broad Line Radio Galaxy (BLRG; a.k.a. PKS 1932-464), with a relatively broad and strong H emission line, with a z =0.2307 tadhunter93,villar98,hernan16 , and, a FR II radio morphology morganti93 . G4Jy 1558 shows a complex gas distribution resulting from the interaction with a nearby companion galaxy, and there is a knotty extended emission line nebula extending beyond the radio structure and the ionization cones, one of the largest ever detected around a radio galaxy at any redshift villar05 . The origin of the nebula is due to the presence of a star forming halo associated with the debris of the merger that triggered the activity. G4Jy 1558 has sufficient luminosity at mid- to far-IR wavelengths to be classified as a luminous infrared galaxy inskip07 . The star formation structure can extend on the scale of a galaxy group, beyond the old stellar halo of the host galaxy villar05 .

sources.summary: The atlas gives PKS 1932-46's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PKS 1932-46 is an FR II radio source in an elliptical galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.2307 ( D 1191 Mpc) with a Sy 1.9 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . The galaxy has a disturbed optical morphology, presumably caused by a past merger or the close-by star-forming companion galaxy villar-martin_giant_2005,inskip_pks1932-46:_2007 . PKS 1932-46 features a galactic-scale extended emission line region, which is just partly belonging to the AGN ionization cone, and extended double-lobe radio emission along a PA 72 villar-martin_pks_1998,villar-martin_giant_2005 .

gmw_00000273.name: PKS 2158-380
gmw_00000273.overview: PKS 2158-380 is a radio-loud lenticular galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.0334 [arXiv:1310.2770]. It hosts a Seyfert 2 nucleus, water maser emission, and three compact nuclear sources [arXiv:1310.2770].

sources.summary: The atlas gives PKS 2158-380's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PKS 2158-380/MCG-6-48-13 is a radio-loud lenticular galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0334 ( D 149 Mpc) with a Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 and was first studied in detail by . HST observations revealed three compact but resolved sources in the nucleus instead of one central source (total extend 1 0.7 kpc; PA 90 ; boyce_faint_1996,zirbel_ultraviolet_1998 ). In addition, water maser emission was detected in this object kondratko_discovery_2006 .

gmw_00000274.name: PKS 2354-35
gmw_00000274.overview: PKS 2354-35 is an elliptical brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 4059 with a LINER-type nucleus and a Fanaroff-Riley I radio classification [arXiv:0802.0499, arXiv:2110.07373]. The galaxy interacts with the surrounding intracluster medium, inflating X-ray cavities and driving feedback through its radio lobes [arXiv:astro-ph/0201107, arXiv:0802.0499].

sources.summary: Chandra observations describe PKS 2354--35 as the central FR-I radio galaxy in A4059, inflating ICM cavities, interacting with cluster gas, and having faded substantially in radio power.

quotes.quote: We find strong evidence that the FR-I radio galaxy PKS 2354--35 at the center of A4059 is inflating cavities with radii 20 in the intracluster medium (ICM), similar to the situation seen in Perseus A and Hydra A. We also find evidence for interaction between the ICM and PKS 2354--35 on small scales in the very center of the cluster. Arguments are presented suggesting that this radio galaxy has faded significantly in radio power (possibly from an FR-II state) over the past 10^8 . galaxies:jets, galaxies:clusters:individual (Abell 4059), hydrodynamics, X-rays: galaxies: clusters Clusters of galaxies are complex dynamical structures and their cores are subject to an array of interesting physical processes.

At a minimum, the radio galaxy has to perform ``p dV'' work against the ICM. Including the internal energy of the plasma within the cavities, this gives an integrated energy output of E_ tot 8 10^ 59 ergs . The radio galaxy had to inject this energy into the bubbles before they floated out of the cluster core. This buoyancy timescale is approximately twice the sound crossing time of the relevant region of the cluster, 4r_ bub /c_ s 8 10^ 7 , where we have used the sound speed for a 4 gas, c_ s 1000 . The time-averaged source power needed to produce the cavities is then L_ kin E_ b / 3 10^ 44 .

sources.summary: The paper links PKS 2354--35 to A4059 cavities, lobes, a weak shock, and asymmetric ridge gas shaped by jet-driven radio-galaxy feedback in the cluster core.

quotes.quote: These features are clearly related to the radio galaxy PKS 2354--35 which resides in the cD galaxy. Our new data confirm these previous findings and strengthen previous suggestions that the south-western ridge is colder and denser than, but in approximate pressure equilibrium with, the surrounding ICM atmosphere. In addition, we find evidence for a weak shock that wraps around the north and east sides of the cavity structure.

High-frequency radio studies ( 1.4-8.5 GHz) of this cluster indicate the presence of two radio lobes choi04,taylor94 associated with the radio galaxy PKS2354--35. The northwest lobe only partially covers the northwest X-ray cavity, and the southeast lobe simply misses the southeast X-ray cavity. Both X-ray cavities are therefore ghost cavities, probably formed from a previous powerful burst of activity by the central FR I radio galaxy, PKS 2354-35 heinz02,choi04 .

The jet axis of the central radio galaxy PKS 2354--35 is oriented on a SE to NW line, and this system has approximate reflection symmetry about a line passing through the AGN perpendicular to the jet axis; i.e., the jet and counter-jet sides look rather similar.

sources.summary: The paper finds PGC 073000 has three gas velocity components, an eastern filament plus southwest swelling, and kinematically aligned ionized gas with features suggesting outflows or settling gas.

quotes.quote: PGC 073000 Most of the line profiles within 2 \ are characterized by three velocity components (see Fig. fig:lines ). The first (and rotating) one is the more spatially extended, the second one is blue-shifted while the third one redshifted with respect to the first one.

PGC 073000 presents one thick filament in the East direction clearly visible in the \ and \ lines, while barely detectable in Balmer and \ lines and not at all present in \ and \ spatial maps. Moreover, a ``swelling'' appears in the south-west direction in all of the maps.

In PGC 073000, ionized-gas, \ and \ are kinematically aligned and share similar distributions pointing to a common origin. Moreover all the line profiles are characterised by three gas components with different velocities (for an example see Fig. fig:lines ) suggesting that ionized-gas, \ and \ are subjected to the same processes (e.g., outflows) within the galaxy. All spatially resolved maps show a second flux peak, in addition to the central one, in the west direction in correspondence to an X-ray arm Choi2004 .

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 349-10 as an elliptical Fanaroff-Riley I brightest-cluster galaxy in Abell 4059 with a LINER nucleus and ultra-steep radio spectrum.

quotes.quote: ESO 349-10 (LEDA 73000, PKS 2354-35) is the elliptical galaxy (E-S0, cD4) identified with a Fanaroff-Riley type I radio source at a redshift of z = 0.04905 (distance 222 Mpc, M_R=-24.53 0.51 ). It contains an optical LINER-type nucleus. ESO 349-10 is a member in a group Abell 4059 and classified by NED as brightest cluster galaxy (BrClG) with optical line emission. It is an Ultra Steep Spectrum radio source. Singh, V., Beelen, A., et al. show that the criterion of ultra steep spectral index remains a method to select populations of weaker radio-loud AGNs potentially hosted in obscured environments.

sources.summary: G4Jy 1858 is described as the FR I brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 4059, with ionized gas, LINER-like ratios, cooling flow, X-ray cavities, and asymmetric X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1858: is the FR I BCG (a.k.a. PKS 2354-35) of the galaxy cluster Abell 4059 abell58,abell89,green90 with a z =0.049021 green88,dacosta89,dacosta98,jones09 . The radio galaxy is classified as ``quiescent'' since it lacks any signature, in H emission, of recent major events that could have disturbed the gas hamer16 and also shows a thick filament of ionized gas, with low velocity dispersion, toward the eastern side with LINER-like line ratios pagotto21 . The galaxy cluster shows a relatively strong cooling-flow schwartz91 , and there is evidence that radio emission is inflating X-ray cavities located in the ICM heinz02,dunn06 , that could be ghosts of a previous burst of G4Jy 1854 due to the lack of any shock-heated ICM choi04 . There is also a central asymmetric ridge of X-ray emission extending for 30 kpc in the south-western direction from the galaxy cluster center see e.g., and references therein choi04 , colder and denser than the surrounding ICM, with super-solar metallicity and significant ICM asymmetry, possibly be due to a starburst/stripping event reynolds08 .

sources.summary: The atlas gives PKS 2354-35's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: PKS 2354-35 is an ultra-steep spectrum FR I radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy ESO 349-10 at a redshift of z= 0.0491 ( D 222 Mpc). It contains an optical LINER-type nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It was observed with /IRAC and MIPS and appears as a compact nuclear source embedded within diffuse host emission in the IRAC 5.8 and 8.0 m images, while only a weak point source was detected in the MIPS 24 m image.

gmw_00000275.name: Superantennae
gmw_00000275.overview: The Superantennae is a merging double ultraluminous infrared galaxy system with two nuclei separated by approximately 10 kiloparsecs and tidal tails extending over 300 kiloparsecs [arXiv:astro-ph/9912348, arXiv:2101.05296]. Its southern nucleus hosts a heavily obscured Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/9912348, arXiv:1510.04453], while the northern nucleus is dominated by starburst activity [arXiv:0907.2846, arXiv:astro-ph/0303259].

sources.summary: VIMOS spectroscopy resolves IRAS 19254-7245 as a two-nucleus ULIRG with tidal tails, southern AGN activity, northern star formation, complex gas components, and AGN-dominated mid-infrared emission.

quotes.quote: We present VIMOS integral field spectroscopic observations of the ultraluminous infrared galaxy (ULIRG) pair IRAS 19254-7245 (the Superantennae). We resolve H , [ N II ] , [ O I ] , and [ S II ] emission both spatially and spectroscopically and separate the emission into multiple velocity components. We identify spectral line emission characteristic of star formation associated with both galaxies, broad spectral line emission from the nucleus of the southern progenitor, and potential outflows with shock-excited spectral features near both nuclei. We estimate that 10 of the 24 m flux density originates from star formation, implying that most of the 24 m emission originates from the AGN in the southern nucleus.

We present here the first results from this survey: VIMOS integral field spectroscopic observations of IRAS 19254-7245 (the Superantennae). The object, which has two distinct nuclei and two tidal tails that extend over hundreds of kpc mlm91 , has been the subject of many detailed studies. The southern nucleus contains an AGN, as shown by studies at multiple wavelengths vblmpw02, cetal02, betal03, bffbp03, rti07 , but the northern nucleus appears to be completely dominated by star formation betal03, bffbp03 . Some evidence has been given for the presence of several distinct dynamical components within the inner arcmin of the galaxy, including a broad line component (full-width half-maximum (FWHM) 2000 2500 km s ^ 1 ) associated with the southern nucleus, narrower components (FWHM <500 km s ^ 1 ) associated with the progenitor discs, and some high-velocity clouds vblmpw02, rti07 .

sources.summary: The paper presents ROSAT and ASCA observations of IRAS19254-7245, finding hard X-ray emission consistent with a heavily obscured Seyfert-2 nucleus in a merging ULIRG.

quotes.quote: We present ROSAT HRI and ASCA observations of the well known ULIRG IRAS19254-7245 (the Superantennae). The object is not detected by ROSAT yielding a 3 upper limit of L_x 8 10^ 41 in the 0.1-2 keV band. However, we obtain a clear detection by ASCA yielding a luminosity in the 2-10 keV band of 2 10^ 42 . Its X-ray spectrum is very hard, equivalent to a photon index of =1.0 0.35 . We therefore, attempt to model the X-ray data with a "scatterer" model in which the intrinsic X-ray emission along our line of sight is obscured by an absorbing screen while some fraction, f, is scattered into our line of sight by an ionized medium; this is the standard model for the X-ray emission in obscured (but non Compton-thick) Seyfert galaxies.

The Superantennae ( L_ IR = 1.1 10^ 12 L_ ) is a remarkable ULIRG with a redshift of z=0.0617 (Mirabel, Lutz Maza 1991). It presents giant tails extending to an unprecedented size of 350 kpc. These emanate from a merger of two giant gas-rich galaxies whose nuclei are separated by 10 kpc.

sources.summary: The Superantennae is treated as a binary ULIRG with a Type 2 AGN, hard X-ray features, prior Compton-thick claims, and weak NuSTAR detections.

quotes.quote: The Superantennae, also known as IRAS F19254-7245, is a binary ULIRG whose southern nucleus is optically classified as a Type 2 AGN degrijp . Its XMM-Newton spectrum shows a hard power-law continuum above 2 keV ( = 1.3 ) and has an Fe line with an EW of 1.4 keV braito03 . It was marginally detected above 10 keV by Suzaku braito09 . The Suzaku data indicate that the Superantennae harbors a Compton-thick AGN with a column density of 3 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 . jia12 found that the relative line strength between the 6.4 and 6.7 keV lines as seen by Chandra varied between 2001 and 2009.

This galaxy was observed twice by NuSTAR , with a temporal separation of about four months. In both epochs, the Superantennae is weakly detected, with a 15--30 keV flux that is 30 times lower than that measured by braito09 with Suzaku .

sources.summary: The paper treats IRAS 19254-7245 as the Superantennae, an interacting Seyfert 2 system with tidal tails, a southern obscured AGN, starburst emission, and heavy infrared absorption.

quotes.quote: IRAS 19254-7245 The Superantennae galaxy is an interacting system with a separation of 10 kpc between the two nuclei, and prominent tidal tails. It is optically classified as a Seyfert 2 (Mirabel et al. 1991). The infrared emission originates from the central region with no significant contribution from the tails. Infrared images obtained with the ISO satellite shows that the dominant contribution to the total luminosity comes from the southern nucleus, which also contains most of the molecular gas present in the system (Charmandaris et al. 2002).

In our sample we can distinguish nuclei with an unobscured AGN (low EW_ 3.3 , flat continuum and absence of strong absorption) in IRAS 05189-2524, IRAS 23060+0505 and the brighter nucleus of NGC 6240, and sources with a heavily absorbed AGN (significant EW_ 3.3 , steep inverted continuum with strong dust and gas absorptions) in IRAS 19254-7245, IRAS 20551-4250 and the fainter nucleus of NGC 6240. The detection of PAH emission line points to the presence of starburst activity in all sources.

sources.summary: The paper describes The Super-antennae as an interacting double ULIRG with long tidal antennae, an embedded AGN, a warped disk, and starburst activity triggered by a mass-ratio encounter.

quotes.quote: The Super-antennae (IRAS 19254 7245) at a redshift of z 0.062 (distance of 260 Mpc assuming H_0 = 71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ; Spergel et al. 2003) is an interacting double galaxy system where the two components are separated by 8 ( 2 kpc). Visually the most notable feature of the system are the thin antennae extending up to 5 ( 350 kpc), far longer than similar feature of the Antennae galaxy ( 100 kpc). The starburst activity powering the total infrared luminosity of 10^ 11.91 L _ IR /L _ (Duc, Mirabel Maza 1997) in the system has been triggered by a 3 : 1 mass ratio encounter (Dasyra et al. 2006).

Deep K band spectroscopy reveals Pa arising in a warped disc with position angle of 330 and an inclination i=40-55 . The kinemetric parameters derived for H _2 are similar to Pa . Two high-ionization emission lines, and , are detected and we identify as the line observed at 1.94 m.

sources.summary: The paper reports ALMA detection of luminous 183 GHz water maser emission in the merging ULIRG Superantennae, linking it to AGN-illuminated dense nuclear gas and tidal-tail morphology.

quotes.quote: We present the results of ALMA band-5 ( 170 GHz) observations of the merging ultraluminous infrared galaxy, the ``Superantennae'' (IRAS 19254 7245) at z= 0.0617, which has been diagnosed as containing a luminous obscured active galactic nucleus (AGN). In addition to dense molecular line emission (HCN, HCO ^ + , and HNC J = 2--1), we detect a highly luminous ( 6 10 ^ 4 L_ ) 183 GHz H _ 2 O 3 _ 1,3 -2 _ 2,0 emission line. We interpret the strong H _ 2 O emission as largely originating in maser amplification in AGN-illuminated dense and warm molecular gas, based on (1) the spatially compact ( 220 pc) nature of the H _ 2 O emission, unlike spatially resolved ( 500 pc) dense molecular emission, and (2) a strikingly different velocity profile from, and (3) significantly elevated flux ratio relative to, dense molecular emission lines.

The ``Superantennae'' (IRAS 19254 7245) is a merging galaxy in the southern hemisphere (declination 72 ^ ), consisting of two galaxy nuclei with a separation of 8'' ( 10 kpc) along the north--south direction and prominent long ( > 300 kpc) tidal tails mel90,mir91,duc97 . It has infrared luminosity L_ IR 1 10 ^ 12 L_ mel90 , belonging to the class of ultraluminous infrared galaxies (ULIRGs) san96 . The Superantennae is a scaled-up version of the classical merging galaxy ``Antennae'' (NGC 4038/9), with > 3 times longer tidal tails and 10 times higher infrared luminosity san96 .

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 19254--7245 as the Superantennae Seyfert 2 ULIRG, detecting a significant polarized H-region residual and a broad polarized line structure.

quotes.quote: IRAS 19254--7245 . the Superantennae (Colina et al., 1991), has been classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy by Mirabel et al. (1991) on the basis of optical spectral properties. In their polarimetric analysis, Heisler et al. (1997) and Lumsden et al. (2001) fail in detecting scattered light and attribute the lack of polarized components to geometric effects: if the scattering particles lie close to the plane of the obscuring torus, an edge--on observer wouldn't be able to observe any polarized flux. In particular Lumsden et al. (2001) found only polarised narrow emission line, and this may be explained if one consider that we were looking at a slightly different location (Lumsden, 2002).

The analysis of our EFOSC2 integration on the Southern Nucleus leads to a detection of a significant polarized residual, in particular in the spectral region around H (see Figure 1, top left panel). The polarization degree turns out to be 1.8 0.4 . The width of this spectral structure in polarized light, which might be interpreted as the H + Nii blend although we do not have the spectral resolution to resolve it, is 70 , or 3500 240 km/s.

sources.summary: The paper studies IRAS 19254--7245/the Superantennae as a double-nucleated ULIRG merger, deriving a bimodal star-formation history and finding an obscured southern AGN dominates much of its infrared output.

quotes.quote: We have performed spatially--resolved spectroscopy of the double--nucleated Ultra-Luminous Infrared Galaxy IRAS 19254--7245, ``the Superantennae'', along the line connecting the two nuclei. These data are analysed with a spectral synthesis code, to derive the star formation and extinction properties of the galaxy. The star formation history (SFH) of the two nuclei is similarly characterized by two different main episodes: a recent burst, responsible of the observed emission lines, and an older one, occurred roughly 1 Gyr ago. We tentatively associate this bimodal SFH with a double encounter in the dynamical history of the merger.

We have complemented our study with a detailed analysis of the broad band spectral energy distribution of the Superantennae, built from published photometry, providing the separate optical-to-mm SEDs of the two nuclei. Our analysis shows that: a) the southern nucleus is responsible for about 80 of the total infrared luminosity of the system, b) the L-band luminosity in the southern nucleus is dominated by the emission from an obscured AGN, providing about 40 to 50 of the bolometric flux between 8 and 1000 m; ) the northern nucleus does not show evidence for AGN emission and appears to be in a post--starburst phase.

sources.summary: The paper reports AGN signatures in IRAS 19254-7245, including low-equivalent-width 3.3 micron emission, steep continuum, strong 3.4 micron absorption, and dust chemistry resembling Galactic stellar sources.

quotes.quote: IRAS 19254-7245 . The high quality spectrum of the southern nucleus show both a 3.3 m emission feature with an equivalent width (EW 25 nm), and two clear AGN features, i.e. a steep continuum ( =2.3 ) and a strong absorption at 3.4 m ( =0.8 ). The substructures of the absorption profile can be used to constrain the chemical composition of the absorbing dust. A detailed analysis of this spectrum is presented in risa03 .

Such analysis on the only other source of our sample showing the 3.4 m absorption feature, IRAS 19254-7245, led to the conclusion that the dust composition in that source is remarkably similar to that observed in Galactic stellar sources.

The main results can be summarized as follows: A direct evidence for the presence of an AGN (equivalent width of the 3.3 m emission feature EW _ 3.3 <50 nm, and/or steep and inverted continuum with <-1 , and/or 3.4 m absorption feature) has been detected in three cases (IRAS 19254-7245, IRAS 20551-4250, IRAS 23128-5919). All these three sources have been classified as AGNs in our XMM-Newton X-ray survey of ULIRGs ( franc03 ).

sources.summary: Suzaku observations of IRAS 19254-7245 reveal a strongly absorbed, QSO-luminosity AGN; its hard X-ray spectrum and Fe K line imply reflection/scattering from highly ionized matter.

quotes.quote: We discuss here a long Suzaku observation of IRAS 19254-7245 (also known as the Superantennae), one of the brightest and well studied Ultra Luminous Infrared Galaxies in the local Universe. This long observation provided the first detection of \ above 10 keV, and measured a 15--30 keV flux of 5 10^ 12 . The detection above 10 keV has allowed us to unveil, for the first time, the intrinsic luminosity of the AGN hosted in , which is strongly absorbed (N _ H 3 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 ) and has an intrinsic luminosity in the QSO regime (L(2--10 keV) 3 10^ 44 ). The 2-- 10 keV spectrum of \ is remarkably hard ( 1.2 ), and presents a strong iron line (EW 0.7 keV), clearly suggesting that below 10 keV we are seeing only reprocessed radiation. Since the energy of the Fe K emission is found to be at 6.7 keV, consistent with He-like Fe, and its EW is too high to be explained in a starburst dominated scenario, we suggest that the 2--10 keV emission of IRAS 19254-7245 is dominated by reflection/scattering from highly ionized matter.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Superantennae as an AGN-important nearby source; its compact infrared emission barely exceeds the starburst surface-brightness threshold, with possible underestimation from Seyfert geometry.

quotes.quote: The Superantennae galaxy is at declination 72 ^ , and is very far south even for the Gemini South telescope site, resulting in a larger air mass, even at the meridian, than other LIRGs. Nearby Sources with Energetically Important AGNs The following five sources at z < 0.1 are diagnosed to be AGN-important through infrared 2.5--35 m spectroscopy: NGC 1377, IRAS 08572+3915, IRAS 15250+3609, Superantennae, and IRAS 20551 4250 (Table 1, column 10). For Superantennae, IRAS 20551 4250, and NGC 1377, the lower limits of the emission surface brightnesses only barely exceed 10 ^ 13 L _ kpc ^ 2 . As Superantennae is classified optically as a Seyfert 2 (Table 1), the dust covering factor around that AGN could be smaller than the other buried AGN candidates (IRAS 20551 4250 and NGC 1377), so that the intrinsic emission surface brightness could be higher than our estimate in Table 6.

sources.summary: The atlas gives Superantennae's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: Superantennae is a ultra-luminous infrared system of a pair of merging galaxies at a redshift of z= 0.0617 ( D 287 Mpc) with a nuclear separation of 8 10 kpc in the north-south direction (PA 10 ; melnick_ntt_1990 ). The southern component, Superantennae S (AM 1925-724 NED02), contains a Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 , while the northern exhibits apparently pure star formation mirabel_superantennae_1991,colina_massive_1991,vanzi_multi-wavelength_2002,berta_spatially-resolved_2003 . After first being detected in the MIR with , the Superantennae were followed up with ground-based N band photometry mirabel_superantennae_1991 and space-based MIR observations with rigopoulou_large_1999,lutz_mid-infrared_1999,tran_isocam-cvf_2001,klaas_infrared_2001,charmandaris_mid-infrared_2002 and /IRAC, IRS and MIPS.

sources.summary: The Superantennae is included as a Seyfert 2 ULIRG with infrared and X-ray evidence for an obscured AGN and no prior interferometric molecular-gas observations before ALMA.

quotes.quote: The Superantennae (IRAS 19254 7245; z = 0.0617) is a ULIRG with L _ IR 10 ^ 12.1 L _ (Table 1), classified optically as a Seyfert 2 mir91 . Infrared ris03,ima10 and X-ray bra03,bra09 observations support the presence of an energetically important obscured AGN. Due to its very low declination ( 72 ^ ), no interferometric molecular gas observations were reported in the published literature prior to ALMA.

gmw_00000276.name: UGC 5101
gmw_00000276.overview: UGC 5101 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:1411.0361, arXiv:1509.04396] and late-stage merger [arXiv:2107.10855, arXiv:2402.10786] featuring a single red nucleus [arXiv:1509.04396, arXiv:2402.10786] and a 15 kpc tidal tail [arXiv:astro-ph/9811342]. It hosts a heavily obscured, Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1905.06964, arXiv:2107.10855] surrounded by a strong circumnuclear starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0603031, arXiv:2402.10786].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 05101 as a ULIRG merger with VLBI compact cores, AGN-dominated radio continuum, broad blended multi-peaked H I absorption, and unsettled neutral gas.

quotes.quote: UGC 05101 - IRAS F09320+6134 This radio source is hosted by an ultra-luminous far-infrared (ULIRG) Galaxy. The galaxy is undergoing a merger event, as also suggested by its optical morphology. The source has also been observed with a 11.6 9.9 mas resolution using VLBI   Lonsdale2003 . These observations show three compact ( 3-4 pc) cores connected by a fainter component. The size of the overall structure is 48 24 pc.

At the resolution of our observations, we are not able to disentangle the absorption seen against the different components: we detect a broad, blended line. The profile is also multi-peaked, reflecting the unsettled state of the neutral hydrogen disk and of the overall host galaxy. The \ has been detected in emission via Effelsberg Telescope observations, through the study of Polar-Ring Galaxy candidates PolarRing .

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 5101's nuclear infrared emission, separating AGN-heated dust from star formation and deriving a deeply obscured, high-covering-factor torus and luminous AGN.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the nuclear infrared (IR, 1.6 to 18 m) emission of the ultraluminous IR galaxy UGC 5101 to derive the properties of its active galactic nucleus (AGN) and its obscuring material. We use new mid-IR high angular resolution ( 0.3-0.5 arcsec) imaging using the Si-2 filter ( _ C =8.7 m) and 7.5-13 m spectroscopy taken with CanariCam (CC) on the 10.4m Gran Telescopio CANARIAS. We also use archival HST/NICMOS and Subaru/COMICS imaging and Spitzer /IRS spectroscopy.

The target of interest in this paper is the nearby ( z=0.039 ; Kim 1995) ULIRG UGC 5101 (IRAS 09320+6134) with a luminosity distance d=168 Mpc (1 arcsec =755 pc) for H_ 0 =73 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _ M =0.27 , _ =0.73, and an IR luminosity L_ IR =10^ 12 L _ (Sanders et al. 1988a). In the optical and near-IR the galaxy has a single, very red nucleus within a disturbed morphology, suggestive of a recent interaction and merger (Sanders et al. 1988b; Surace et al. 2000; Scoville et al. 2000). Optically the AGN has been classified as a Seyfert 1.5 (Sanders et al. 1988a), a LINER (Veilleux et al. 1995), and a Seyfert 2 (Yuan et al. 2010).

We found that CLUMPY torus models with a high covering factor and a high foreground extinction ( A_V 32 mag) fit well the silicate feature and unresolved mid-IR emission. The high value of the foreground extinction is similar to results for other deeply embedded AGN in local LIRGs and ULIRGs (see e.g. Alonso-Herrero et al. 2013 and Mori et al. 2014).

sources.summary: The paper says UGC 5101 has conflicting X-ray interpretations, with evidence ranging from pure starburst to heavily obscured or Compton-thick AGN, leaving its obscuration unresolved.

quotes.quote: UGC 5101 The first detection of the AGN/starburst composite nucleus of UGC 5101 in X-rays was presented by based on and observations. While they state that the nucleus is too faint for spectral analysis and consistent with a pure starburst, find evidence for an AGN in X-rays in the same data combined with data. The latter work states that the AGN dominates the emission of UGC 5101 and is heavily but not Compton-thick obscured. and come to the same conclusion based on the same data ( = 42.5 ; = 23.7 ). However, assume that the nucleus is Compton-thick obscured based on the flat X-ray spectral slope and low X-ray-- ratio with an intrinsic luminosity of = 43.97 .

The Compton-thick scenario would agree well with the prediction of the MIR--X-ray correlation ( = 44.07 0.38 ; = 24.74 0.33 ), assuming that the nuclear subarcsecond-scale MIR emission is AGN-dominated. Unfortunately, this assumption is not verifiable with the current available MIR data. Therefore, it remains unclear whether the AGN in UGC 5101 is indeed CT obscured or not.

sources.summary: The survey describes UGC 5101 as a LINER infrared galaxy with buried AGN evidence from mid-IR, hot dust, hard X-rays, VLBI, and extended near-IR emission.

quotes.quote: UGC 5101 v95 classify the optical spectrum of UGC 5101 as LINER. Although g98 suggest the mid-infrared radiation field is more similar to that of a starburst than an AGN, a04 find that [ NeV ] emission implies this source contains a buried AGN. a07 detect continuum emission from dust hotter than 300 K, which also indicates the presence of an AGN.

i03 detect hard X-ray emission, including the Fe line, which they interpret as evidence for a buried AGN. l03 use VLBI imaging to find that an AGN is responsible for at least 10 of the total radio flux. Near-infrared images show extended emission with a brighter nucleus sco00 .

sources.summary: UGC 5101 is presented as a disturbed LINER ULIRG with a red nucleus, circumnuclear starburst signatures, cold starburst-dominated infrared colors, and XMM evidence for a buried AGN.

quotes.quote: UGC 5101 ( z=0.039 ) has a single, very red nucleus within a disturbed morphology suggestive of a recent interaction. Optically, UGC 5101 is classified as a LINER (Veilleux et al., 1995). It has a high brightness temperature ( T>10^ 7 K) radio nucleus at 1.6 GHz which is resolved with the VLBA (Lonsdale et al., 1995). ISO SWS and PHT-S spectroscopy (Genzel el al., 1998) indicate a powerful, circumnuclear starburst. Based upon its IRAS colours, UGC 5101 is classified as a cold, starburst-dominated, far-infrared source.
However, XMM data indicate an obscured, but luminous, hard X-ray source with L_ x (2-10 keV) 5 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 and L_ x (2-10 keV)/ L_ IR 0.002 suggestive of a buried AGN (Imanishi et al., 2003).

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 05101 as a confirmed Compton-thick LINER, noting prior high-obscuration claims, flat-spectrum diagnostics, and measured FeK equivalent width.

quotes.quote: UGC 05101 and NGC 5005 have been also claimed to be Compton-thick by using an indirect method. A highly obscured nuclei (NH 10 ^ 24 cm ^ 3 ) has been reported by imanishi_x-ray_2003 for UGC 05101. Hereinafter we consider UGC 05101, NGC 3690B, and NGC 6240 objects as Compton-thick sources according to published literature results (hereinafter we refer to them as 'Confirmed Compton-thick ').

Concerning the indirect Compton-thick diagnostics used above, two of the confirmed Compton-thick show flat spectra (UGC 05101 and NGC 6240) whereas NGC 3690B seems to have a somewhat steeper spectrum. Also, we have been able to determine the EW(FeK ) of the three confirmed Compton-thick in our sample, finding EW(FeK )=280 180 keV , EW(FeK )=230 110 keV and EW(FeK )=380 60 keV , for UGC 05101, NGC 3690B and NGC 6240 respectively (shown as black stars in Figs. cha3:fig:EWNH2 and cha3:fig:LHOIII_fek ).

sources.summary: The paper interprets UGC 05101 HI absorption as a highly absorbed core in an inclined rotating disk, consistent with earlier CO maps showing regular molecular-gas rotation.

quotes.quote: In a couple of cases, such as UGC 05101 and Mrk 273, the absorption profiles are nearly symmetrical about zero velocity. For the Type 2 AGN UGC 05101, the profile is likely from absorption due to a slightly inclined rotating HI disk superposed on an obscured nucleus. This interpretation is consistent with CO maps of UGC 05101 showing smooth, regular rotation of the molecular gas genzel98, wilson08 .
UGC 05101 Multiple absorption components are seen in the GBT data. We interpret the HI absorption profile as a highly absorbed core coincident with an inclined regularly rotating disk.

sources.summary: UGC 05101 is described as an IR-bright interacting LINER with buried Seyfert-like AGN activity and multiple detected CO lines in its SPIRE spectrum.

quotes.quote: Analysis of Individual Sources UGC 05101 UGC 05101 is an IR bright galaxy ( = 10^ 11.95 ) located at 163 Mpc SandersRBGS . It has a peculiar morphology that suggests a recent interaction Sanders1988 . Its nuclear activity is classified as LINER from optical spectroscopy Yuan2010 . However the detection of high-ionization emission lines in the mid-IR ( [ Ne 5 ] and [ O 4 ] ; Armus07 ), the intense Fe K emission line at 6.4 keV Imanishi2003b , and its high hard X-ray luminosity ( L_ 2-10 keV = 7.5 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 ; Imanishi2003b ) indicate the presence of a buried AGN with a luminosity comparable to that of a Seyfert galaxy. In the \ spectrum of this galaxy we detect five unblended CO lines (see Table tab:lines ).

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 5101 as a questionable dual-megamaser case, noting a reported luminous water maser, failed follow-up detections, and exclusion from the main analysis.

quotes.quote: Previous studies have included UGC 5101 as a possible dual megamaser. A very luminous water maser was detected toward UGC 5101 (Martin 1989), which has not been detected in followup observations (Baan et al. 1992). We re-reduced VLBI 1665 MHz observations (VLBA project VLBA VSN000352) of UGC 5101 and were unable to either find a line or to image the emission. Of the tentative detections discussed, UGC 5101 is the only maser observation where follow up observations were unsuccessful in detecting a signal. For these reasons, we omit UGC 5101 in our analysis, but will provide some discussion on how our results change if the questionable case of UGC 5101 is considered.

sources.summary: The paper selects UGC05101 as an obscured local Seyfert and fits its broad X-ray spectrum, finding a Compton-thick absorption column and weak iron-line behavior.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:UGC05101_spectra (top-left panel) shows the full-band spectrum fitted with the phenomenological transmission model (reported in the top-right panel), including data-model residuals in units of . The black curve refers to the pn, while the red one shows the summed MOS1-2 spectrum; the green line indicates the ACIS-S3 spectrum, and blue and light blue lines indicate the FPMA and FPMB spectra, respectively. The continuum spectral components model the cut-off due to Galactic ( N_ H (Gal) =3 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 ) and host galaxy absorption ( N_ H,1 0.8 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ), plus a thermal emission with temperature kT 0.2 keV, a scattered component and a heavily absorbed primary component. The absorption intrinsic to the source has a value N_ H,2 =1.21^ +0.16 _ 0.14 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 . The derived N_ H,2 value is 1.21^ +0.19 _ 0.17 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 , consistent with the recent analysis carried out by .

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 5101 as a single-nucleus stage-D ULIRG merger with a hard X-ray Compton-thick AGN and variable column density.

quotes.quote: IRAS F09320+6134 (UGC 5101): The ULIRG UGC 5101 is a stage-D merger showing a single nucleus. We identify the source as the hard X-ray detected AGN. Ricci2015 detect a CT AGN by analyzing the XMM-Newton and Swift/BAT spectra. The NuSTAR study by Oda2017 finds the absorption column density to be N_ H 1.3 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 . By utilizing all the available data of NuSTAR, Chandra, XMM-Newton, Suzaku, and Swift/BAT, we obtain almost the same results as Oda2017 but newly find that the column density is variable from N_ H = (1.5^ +0.3 _ 0.2 ) 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 (Chandra, XMM-Newton, and Suzaku) to (9.6^ +0.4 _ 0.2 ) 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 (NuSTAR and Swift/BAT).

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 5101 as an ultraluminous infrared peculiar galaxy with a ring, tidal tail, starburst component, and Seyfert 1.8 or 1.9 nucleus.

quotes.quote: UGC 5101 . This is an ultra-luminous infrared galaxy ( L _ IR 10 ^ 12 L _ ); it is a peculiar galaxy with a large ring and a 15 kpc tidal tail extending to the west, which suggests a merger, although no companions are known to this galaxy. It has a single bright nucleus (Sanders et al. 1988). Optical spectra have been published by Sanders et al. who classified it as a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy on the basis of a relatively broad \ line, by Veilleux et al. (1995) and Wu et al. (1998a,b) who called it a Liner, and by Liu Kennicutt (1995). However, the published line ratios ( \ = 2.9--4.7, \ = 1.17--1.35, \ = 0.06--0.09) rather indicate a ``transition'' spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 5101 as an HC3N-poor LIRG exception and the only HC3N-poor galaxy with an OH megamaser strong relative to its molecular gas.

quotes.quote: Some galaxies can definitely be seen as HC _3 N-poor, since they have HC _3 N/HCN ratios (or upper limits for this ratio) less than or equal to 0.10: NGC 253 , NGC 1068 , NGC 1808 , NGC 3256 , NGC 4945 , NGC 6946 , and UGC 5101 . If the HC _3 N-rich and -poor galaxies are compared with the galaxy classifications of Table tab:galaxies , the most obvious trend is that most of the HC _3 N-poor galaxies are starbursts, with the exception for the LIRG UGC 5101 . A few of the HC _3 N-poor galaxies also have OH mega- or kilomaser activity ( NGC 253 , NGC 1068 , and UGC 5101 ). UGC 5101 is the only HC _3 N-poor galaxy with an OH megamaser that is strong compared to the amount of molecular gas in the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper highlights UGC 5101 as an interacting Seyfert candidate with nearly coincident CLASS offsets, Chandra emission, and 4-sigma positional deviations.

quotes.quote: SDSS J093551.58+612111.7 (UGC 5101; MCG +10-14-025) AGN class: Seyfert, Morphology: S?, Sb, pec (NED) Two CLASS detections of 31.2 mJy and 17.4 mJy, with almost the same position, are offset by 306 and 339 mas respectively (one or both of which may be NVSS J093551+612112) from the SDSS position. Compact Chandra source detected at the same position. UGC 5101 is a well-known interacting galaxy e.g. Armus2004, Lonsdale2003 , and the AGN is classified as a Seyfert 1 by . These offsets deviate from the fitted Rayleigh distribution with 4-sigma confidence.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 5101 as a strong HCN-vib source with high AGN fraction, faint high-velocity OH wings, and weak blueshifted OH absorption indicating gas moving toward us.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, at least two other sources with strong HCN-vib emission, NGC 4418 and UGC 5101, have AGN fractions 50 .

If we also consider the fact that UGC 5101 has faint high-velocity wings in its OH 119 m absorption lines vei13 , it turns out that all of the six most HCN-vib luminous sources have signatures of molecular outflows.

Looking at the spectra presented by vei13 , UGC 5101 and IRAS 17208-0014 even exhibit weak blueshifted line wings in the OH 119 m absorption lines. This indicates the presence of gas moving towards us, but as the absorptions at negative velocities are shallow, they do not have a strong effect on the median velocities of the lines.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 05101 as a disturbed intermediate Seyfert 1.5 late-stage merger with circumnuclear starburst features and a two-electron-population radio SED.

quotes.quote: UGC 05101: classified as an intermediate Seyfert 1.5 type galaxy Sanders88 with a disturbed morphology containing a single red nucleus. It is a late-stage merger Veilleux02 undergoing strong circumnuclear starburst suggested by the detection of characteristic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) features in MIR spectra Martinez15 . Although our radio SED modeling utilizes a larger dataset and frequency coverage, particularly at the lowest (74 MHz) and the highest (32.5 GHz) ends, our best-fit SED model is similar to that obtained by Clemens10 but with two electron populations. Moreover, our derived values of _2 and the turnover frequency are consistent within uncertainties with Clemens10 .

sources.summary: The paper revises UGC 05101 from a previously reported H2CO emission detection to a non-detection after new measurements fail to reproduce the 2006 result.

quotes.quote: UGC 05101: This was reported as a new detection of the 1_ 10 1_ 11 transition in emission in Mangum2008 . Additional measurements, though, have thrown this assignment into question as we do not see consistent results between our 2006 reported in Mangum2008 and our new 2013 measurements. We now believe that neither the 1_ 10 1_ 11 nor the 2_ 11 2_ 12 transitions have been detected in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The atlas gives UGC 5101's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: UGC 5101 is an infrared-ultra-luminous disturbed highly-inclined spiral galaxy sanders_ultraluminous_1988 at a redshift of z= 0.0394 ( D 182 Mpc) with an active nucleus containing both a starburst and an AGN. This lead to various optical classifications in the literature ranging from Sy 1 to Sy 2 or LINER, while we follow and treat the nucleus as AGN/starburst composite. The L band and X-ray properties provide evidence for a highly obscured AGN in UGC 5101 imanishi_strong_2001,imanishi_x-ray_2003 .

sources.summary: UGC 05101 is described as an advanced merger; its redshift removes one transition from spectral coverage, and extinction correction strongly changes its H2 ratios.

quotes.quote: Three of the galaxies (NGC 34, IRASF 05189--2524, and UGC 05101) are advanced major mergers.
For non detections, we state the 3 upper limits. a Because of the higher redshift of IRASF 05189--2524 and UGC 05101, the 4 \ transition does not lie in the \ spectral range.
This is clear for UGC 05101, whose 0--0 S(1) 0--0 S(3) ratios are 2.3 0.4 and 0.8 0.1 before and after the extinction correction.

sources.summary: The paper says UGC 05101 has a flat central radio spectral-index distribution that steepens beyond 1.1, and it is also an AGN in mid-infrared and Seyfert in optical diagnostics.

quotes.quote: UGC 05101 The map shows a flat distribution in the centre, steepening outwards with values of > 1.1. Based on our classification this is a radio-AGN. This object is also an AGN based on the mid-IR diagnostics presented in the analysis, and a Seyfert galaxy in the optical.

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 05101 as a likely TA-hotspot candidate, citing its star-forming nature, high far-infrared luminosity, and confinement time short enough for UHECR escape.

quotes.quote: The starburst galaxy M82 has the largest relative probability 99.8 , and the star-forming galaxy UGC 05101 and the blazar Mrk 180 also have comparably large relative probabilities of being the source.

The star-forming galaxy UGC 05101 has a high far-infrared luminosity L_ FIR =89 10^ 10 L_ . According to He2013 , assuming a half-light radius of 1  kpc , the energy-loss time is _ loss =1.8 10^ 4 yr l 100 pc with l as the scale of the dense region in the galaxy, and the confinement time is _ conf =2.7 10^3 yr E_ p 50 EeV ^ 0.5 , which is shorter than the energy-loss time for ultra-high energy cosmic rays, implying that ultra-high energy cosmic rays can escape from UGC 05101.

sources.summary: UGC 5101 is included despite falling slightly below the luminosity cutoff; its [C II] profile suggests an edge-on disk and marginal broad component.

quotes.quote: We include, however, NGC 6240 and UGC 5101 which are formally slightly below this cutoff, but in any relevant aspect for this study have similar charateristics as the rest of the sample.
The [ CII ] line profile in UGC 5101 suggests self-absorption or a 'double horn' profile as is typical for a disk seen edge-on.
UGC 5101 has a marginally detected broad [ CII ] component, while OH v_ 50 = -9 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 5101 as a ULIRG with a high modeled AGN contribution, a comparatively poor best fit, and red mid-infrared colors implying an AGN.

quotes.quote: The red 5.8--100 \ colours suggest an AGN is present UGC 5101 : UGC 5101 is a ULIRG. We measure the AGN contribution to be 76 , although the best-fit model is markedly worse than most of the late-stage mergers. dal14 estimated a value nearly a factor of 5 smaller. The red 4.5--24 \ SED implies an AGN is present.

sources.summary: UGC 5101 is described as a Compton-thick AGN host whose AGN continuum dominates the OSIRIS maps, with bright nuclear gas and enhanced line-ratio spaxels interpreted as a possible shock candidate.

quotes.quote: UGC 05101 UGC 5101 features a Compton-Thick AGN with strong mid-infrared and X-ray signature but lacks optical signatures . In our OSIRIS maps, the AGN continuum dominates though and emission can both be seen to peak at the same location. The gas is particularly bright near the nucleus such that enhanced / spaxels can be seen coming out of the center, and may be interpreted as a shock candidate.

gmw_00000277.name: UGC 12348
