quotes.quote: However in the case of Mkn 79 ( 10^ 8 ) we see correlated X-ray and optical variations on short timescales (tens of days, Fig. fig:79 ) but large amplitude changes in the optical band on years timescales which are not seen in the X-ray band. If we remove the long term trend in the optical light curve of Mkn 79, we find an extremely strong ( >99.5 significance) correlation between the X-ray and optical variations with a lag very close to zero days ( 2 d).

However the slower optical variations in both MR 2251-178 MR 2251-178 and Mkn 79 Mkn 79 cannot be explained by reprocessing from an X-ray emission region on an accretion disc both of constant geometry. As reprocessing from the accretion disc occurs on the light travel time which is, at most, a few days, it is impossible to remove all of the fast variations seen in the X-ray band. It might be possible to produce very smooth reprocessed light curves if the reprocessor is much larger than the accretion disc, e.g. if it is in the broad line region or torus. Another possibility is that the slow optical variations can be explained either by varying the accretion rate in the disc, or by varying the geometry of the X-ray source or accretion disc. The radius within which half of the thermal optical emission from the accretion disc in Mkn 79 is produced is 70 R_ G .
The viscous timescale at this radius is about 4.7 years.

sources.summary: The paper notes the host extends beyond the field, is fit with bulge, disturbed bar, and disk components, and contains prominent dust lanes.

quotes.quote: Mrk 79 (Fig. 11.14) --- \ \ The outer part of the galaxy is outside the detector field of view, thus the total brightness of the host might be an upper limit. The host galaxy is fit with components that resemble a bulge and a disturbed bar and a disk. Dust lanes are prominent. Malkan et al. (1998) and Crenshaw et al. (2003) classify the host as a barred disk galaxy. Bennert et al. (2010) find the ratio of B/T 0.41 to be a bit larger than our estimate ( B/T=0.16 ).

sources.summary: The paper reports three FUSE observations of Mrk 79 and treats a broad depression at the predicted X-ray-UFO location as a statistically significant but tentative feature.

quotes.quote: This Seyfert 1 has three FUSE observations in the archive. No UV absorption is detectable in the second observation, which has the highest S/N, but the first and third data sets show a broad, shallow depression at the location predicted for the X-ray UFO. Unfortunately, the center of that feature is coincident with the N 1 1134 airglow feature, which is quite prominent in the second observation. Also, thermal misalignments during the third observation led to very low fluxes in the LiF2 channel, so the absorption detected mostly in the LiF1 spectrum cannot be independently confirmed in the LiF2 spectrum. Although the feature is statistically significant (at a confidence level exceeding 99.9 ), we consider its detection tentative.

sources.summary: The paper reverberation-maps Mrk 79, finding a measured H-beta lag, black-hole mass estimate, and a complex velocity-delay map suggesting mixed inflowing and outflowing broad-line gas.

quotes.quote: We report the results of reverberation mapping three bright Seyfert galaxies, Mrk 79, NGC 3227, and Mrk 841, from a campaign conducted from December 2016 to May 2017 with the Wyoming Infrared Observatory (WIRO) 2.3-meter telescope. All three of these targets have shown asymmetric broad H emission lines in the past, although their emission lines were relatively symmetric during our observations. We measured \ time lags for all three targets and estimated masses of their black holes -- for the first time in the case of Mrk 841.

Mrk 79 shows smaller time lags for high-velocity gas but the distribution is not symmetric, and its complex velocity-delay map could result from the combination of both inflowing and outflowing \ emitting disks that may be part of a single larger structure.
Mrk 79 developed a strong blue asymmetry in \ during the 1990s peterson1998 .

sources.summary: The paper maps Mrk 79's central ionized gas, describing its strong-emission region, velocity field, and signs of nuclear outflow, rotation, density structure, or inflow.

quotes.quote: This Sy 1 galaxy shows the SER elongated towards the S-SW, at an angle of 55 ^ relative to the PMA, extending beyond the border of the FoV. This direction (P.A. 190 ^ ) can be identified with the ionisation axis of the AGN, as evidenced in the narrow-band image of Schmitt2003 , which is also the orientation of the jet-like 3.6 cm radio continuum image Schmitt2001 . In , we find blueshifts to the South that we attribute to an outflow along the ionisation axis, while in lower velocity blueshifts are also observed there. Blueshifts are also observed to the NW, but their origin is not clear, seeming to be a counterpart to a redshifted region observed to the SE in the emission. is enhanced approximately perpendicularly to the ionisation axis, this can again be attributed to an equatorial outflow due to the passage of the radio jet.

sources.summary: The paper reports Mrk 79 has a young stellar ring, older central stars, hot dust across the field, nuclear featureless continuum, and a 2 Gyr central population.

quotes.quote: Mrk 79: A clear ring-like structure is observed in the xy and xyo SPC, with a fraction reaching 50 percent for the xyo population. Negligible contributions of the intermediate age components are observed, with a smoother central distribution of older stars ( xo ) surrounded by the xyo ring-like ( 300 pc) SPC. Hot dust emission is observed over the entire FoV showing a maximum in the nuclear region. A FC emission of 40 percent is detected in the nuclear region. In the mean age map we see that the central region of this galaxy is dominated by a 2 Gyr population.

sources.summary: The paper reports narrow neutral iron emission in UGC 3973's first epoch and a broad Gaussian feature in the second, with line widths suggesting outer accretion-disk emission.

quotes.quote: In seven objects (ESO 602-- G 031, Mrk 728, UGC 12139, ESO 244-- G 017, UGC 3973 (first epoch), ESO 15-- IG 011 (second epoch), CGCG 039--167) the line was consistent with narrow, unresolved emission from neutral iron.

Four objects in the sample (UGC 3973 (second epoch), Mrk 590, MCG--02-14-009 (see also Porquet 2005), II Zw 177) were fitted better if a broad Gaussian profile was considered rather than an intrinsically narrow profile (see Table tab:adds ).

We note that in three objects: MCG--02-14-009, UGC 3973, and Mrk 590 (e.g. Figure fig:mrkln ), the full-width at half-maximum of the Gaussian profiles indicated velocities on the order of 10000 . For all three of these AGN, the widths of the iron lines are only about a factor of 2-4 greater then the velocities measured in the H emission line from the broad line region. This suggests that, at least in these cases, the iron emission could be originating from the outer regions of the accretion disc, where Doppler effects dominate relativistic effects.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 3973 with MRK 79 and contrasts observations, attributing a weaker Fe K line detection to absorber variability or lower data quality.

quotes.quote: UGC 3973 Laha et al. (2014) considered one XMM-Newton observation of UGC 3973 (or MRK 79) obtained in April 2008 (OBSID 0502091001). This observation was not in the sample of Tombesi et al. (2010a), who considered three observations obtained between 2006 and 2007. Tombesi et al. (2010a, 2011a) reported the detection of a blue-shifted Fe K absorption line at E = 7.63 keV indicative of a UFOs with velocity v_ out 0.09c in the first observation (OBSID 0400070201).

The fact that Laha et al. (2014) did not observe an intense Fe K absorption line in their spectrum could be due to variability of the absorber and/or to the lower statistics of this fainter observation (also limited by the use of single events only).

sources.summary: The paper rejects the optical spectrum for Mrk 79 because of variability, preferring a non-simultaneous SED from GALEX and OM photometry for accretion-disc analysis.

quotes.quote: The optical spectrum was taken in the lowest flux state, whilst both GALEX and XMM-Newton observed Mrk 79 in a higher flux states. The narrower spectral window of Mrk 79 and the large observed variability between the mean optical spectrum and the photometry rules out the spectrum. Taking advantage of the good agreement between the near-UV GALEX flux and the OM photometry, a non-simultaneous dataset is probably the best SED to study the accretion disc emission. Indeed, the low number of simultaneous photometric points does not allow to construct a contemporaneous dataset.

sources.summary: The paper uses archived Gemini/NIFS data for Mrk 79, noting the small field still probes the bulge enough for a velocity-dispersion measurement despite AGN contamination.

quotes.quote: Mrk 79 Data and Reduction Data taken with Gemini-North's Near-Infrared Integral Field Spectrometer (NIFS) are available for Mrk 79 from the Gemini Observatory Archive https://archive.gemini.edu/ NIFS . NIFS provides for very high spatial resolution ( 0 1 when used with their adaptive-optics system ALTAIR) and good spectral resolution over a small 3 3 field of view. The field of view of NIFS is significantly smaller than that of HexPak and WiFeS, so these data probe a smaller region of the galaxy than is probed in the other targets in this study. However the field of view does extend out to r_ e /2 , which is large enough to give us insight into the bulge stellar dynamics and allow for a good determination of _ .

sources.summary: The paper maps the inner 0.4-1.1 kpc of Mrk 79 with GMOS-IFU data and finds an electron-temperature fluctuation value t_A^2 of 0.135.

quotes.quote: We use Gemini GMOS-IFU observations of three luminous nearby Seyfert galaxies (Mrk 79, Mrk 348 and Mrk 607) to estimate the electron temperature ( T_ e ) fluctuations in the inner 0.4--1.1 kpc region of these galaxies. Based on T_ e determinations through the [ O iii ] 5007 / 4363 emission line ratio of each spaxel, temperature variations are quantified by computing the integrated value of the temperature fluctuation parameter ( t^ 2 ) projected in the plane of the sky t_ A ^ 2 , for the first time in Active Galactic Nuclei. We find t_ A ^ 2 values of 0.135, 0.039, and 0.015 for Mrk 79, Mrk 348, and Mrk 607, respectively, which are of the same order or larger than the maximum values reported in star-forming regions and planetary nebulae. Taking into account that t_ A ^ 2 should be considered a lower limit of the total t^2 in the nebular volume, the results suggest that the impact of such fluctuations on chemical abundance determinations can be important in some AGNs.

gmw_00000284.name: NGC 4619
gmw_00000284.overview: NGC 4619 is classified as a Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/9810214]. Observations reveal that the galaxy exhibits extended soft X-ray emission that is clearly elongated in the north-south direction [arXiv:astro-ph/9810214].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4619 as a Seyfert 1 galaxy whose ROSAT HRI radial profile and contours indicate extended, north-south elongated soft X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: Fig. fig:rad_sec presents the radial wobble-corrected profile of the Seyfert 1 galaxy NGC 4619 in comparison to the HRI PSF's. The radial profile indicates an extented soft X-ray emission up to a radius of about 30 . The excessive X-ray flux at > 100 is probably due to the uncertainty in background subtraction. The HRI contours of NGC 4619 show a clearly elongated X-ray emission in north-south direction (cf. Fig fig:cont ), while the Seyfert 1 galaxy NGC 4151 seems to be only slightly extended in north-east direction (north is on top, east at left).

gmw_00000285.name: NGC 4203
gmw_00000285.overview: NGC 4203 is an isolated, nearly face-on lenticular galaxy [arXiv:0908.3564, arXiv:1503.01836] hosting a low-luminosity LINER-type active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1001.1630, arXiv:1503.01836]. It features a large, misaligned atomic hydrogen disk [arXiv:1505.02981, arXiv:2401.17553] with a low dust-to-gas ratio [arXiv:1201.2762] and a variable, double-peaked broad Balmer emission line profile [arXiv:1508.04346, arXiv:1503.01836].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4203 as a nearby lenticular with a large low-column-density gas disc, finding inner star-forming and outer gas components with different efficiencies and likely distinct gas origins.

quotes.quote: NGC 4203 is a nearby early-type galaxy surrounded by a very large, low-column-density \ disc. In this paper we study the star formation efficiency in the gas disc of NGC 4203 by using the UV, deep optical imaging and infrared data. We confirm that the \ disc consists of two distinct components: an inner star forming ring with radius from 1 to 3 R _ eff , and an outer disc. The outer \ disc is 9 times more massive than the inner \ ring.

These two gas components have a different star formation efficiency likely due to the different metallicity and dust content. The inner component has a star formation efficiency very similar to the inner regions of late-type galaxies. Although the outer component has a very low star formation efficiency, it is similar to that of the outer regions of spiral galaxies and dwarfs. We suggest that these differences can be explained with different gas origins for the two components such as stellar mass loss for the inner \ ring and accretion from the inter galactic medium (IGM) for the outer \ disc.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4203 as a Type I LINER with archived Swift data, excess absorption, no significant variability, and only short-duration flickering in the monitoring.

quotes.quote: Our sample includes the brightest LINERs in the UV sample, M81, NGC 3998, NGC 4203, and NGC 4579, that are all Type I LINERs (i.e., with detected broad H components) according to . Data for two of these sources, M81 and NCG 4203 were already available in the Swift archive: M81 (net exposure of 33.7 ks) has an X-ray Telescope spectrum of excellent quality and even the suggestion of an Fe K line; NGC 4203 (5.3 ks) has a good XRT spectrum, although slightly under exposed with respect to M81. NGC 3998 and NGC 4579 were observed with Swift for the first time (27.4 and 20.8 ks, respectively).

NGC 4203 exhibits no significant variability on any time scale. One reason may be that our monitoring is short, so that we had no chance to detect variations, that occur stochastically. In fact, rapid variation of the amplitude seen for M81 and NGC 3998 do not seem to be present, although the errors on the individual points are similar to those of the other sources, so that variations of comparable level could have been appreciated. On the other hand, the errors associated with orbit-averaged points are rather large, compared with those of M81 and NGC 3998, so that inter-orbit variations albeit of amplitude similar to the inter-orbit variations of M81 and NGC3998, are never significant (Fig. liners:fig:xlc_all g,h). This indicates that, over the short duration of our monitoring, NGC 4203 only exhibits a flickering, without any real variability episode.

sources.summary: NGC 4203 is a non-reverberating dwarf Seyfert with a giant BLR off the usual relation; its double-peaked Balmer emission varied strongly over a decade without continuum change.

quotes.quote: In the present contribution, high quality spectra obtained with the Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph (STIS) are used to constrain the size of the BLR in the dwarf Seyfert nuclei of M81, NGC 3998, NGC 4203, NGC 3227, NGC 4051, and NGC 3516.

There are presently six LLAGNs whose BLR size has been deduced by modeling the shape of the H Balmer lines including three non-reverberating dwarf Seyferts (M81, NGC 3998 and NGC 4203), and three reverberating ones (NGC 3227, NGC 3516 and NGC 4051). Some basic observables are listed in Table 1, and their BLR size-luminosity relationship is illustrated in Figure 1. The three non-reverberating dwarf Seyferts lie far from the correlation defined by reverberating AGNs. The other three LLAGN are reverberating, and their inner radii consistently lie on the correlation defined by other reverberating AGN. This latter result is important as it shows that the line profile fitting method does yield a size that agrees with the reverberation size.

Fourth, at least two objects M81 and NGC 4203 exhibit time variable, double-peak broad Balmer emission lines Dev03,Dev11b . For the latter object, the Balmer line fluxes increased by a factor of two over a time interval of a decade with no perceptible change in the adjacent continuum. Such emission line variability is an order of magnitude larger than seen among the reverberating population.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 4203 has soft diffuse emission around its nucleus, severe Chandra pileup from a large X-ray flux, and no confusing nearby source for ASCA flux use.

quotes.quote: The nuclei of NGC 2787 and NGC 4203 are embedded in soft diffuse emission with diameters of 30^ and 50^ , respectively. The pileup effect for the three objects with the largest count rate per frame (NGC 4203, NGC 4579, and NGC 5033) is serious and we did not attempt detailed spectral fits.

NGC 4203 (L1.9) . --- A result on the same data set is presented in Ho et al. (2001). The nucleus of this object has a large X-ray flux and pileup is severe in this observation. A bright source is seen 2 ^ SE of the nucleus which was also separated from the nucleus with SIS observations (Iyomoto et al. 1998; Terashima et al. 2002b). The observations show that there is no source confusing the observation of the nucleus. Therefore, we used an flux in the discussions.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4203 as a clear Type II.o profile with tentative spheroidal outer excess and an ambiguous bar-related break likely classified as an OLR break.

quotes.quote: This is a clear Type II.o profile with a weak but significant excess over the outer exponential at 140 . The galaxy is close to face-on, which makes determining the shape of the outer isophotes difficult. However, there is evidence that the isophotes become systematically rounder for > 100 , so our tentative conclusion is that the outer light may be coming from a spheroid.

The nature of the inner (Type II.o) break is also somewhat ambiguous, since the bar size is uncertain; as noted by erwin03 and erwin05 , the large value of = 46 is almost certainly an overestimate of the bar size. So it is difficult to judge whether the break is too far out from the bar to be associated with its OLR. Nonetheless, the high surface brightness of the break suggests it is not due to a (star-formation-related) classical truncation see erwin07 , so we tentatively classify it as an OLR break.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes NGC4203 as an isolated barred S0 with weak UV star formation, a large irregular atomic-hydrogen reservoir, ring structures, and dynamics suggestive of accretion.

quotes.quote: NGC4203 is an, apparently isolated, barred S0 galaxy. UV images show low level star-formation at least in the south-east part of the galaxy, corresponding to the outer edge of the warm-dust spiral structure detected at 8 m in the galaxy center pahre04 . Despite its low star formation activity, NGC4203 has a large reservoir of atomic hydrogen, distributed in a peculiar, filamentary structure and extended up to 2.2 optical radii vandriel88,noordermeer05 . The atomic gas has a general sense of motion around the center, but it is clearly not on regular circular orbits noordermeer05 .

Again, the is mainly found in two ring structures: an inner ring (in correspondence with the UV features) and an outer ring which is likely rotating on a different plane vandriel88 . The peculiar dynamics of NGC4203 is thus suggestive of an accretion event vandriel88 .

sources.summary: NGC 4203 is modeled with stellar and H I kinematics; the paper finds significant gas-disc misalignment, increased dark-matter fractions, and stellar dominance inside two effective radii.

quotes.quote: However, only a subsample of four galaxies shows regular rotating features in their velocity fields in deeper observations with the WSRT: NGC 2685, NGC 4203, NGC 5582 and NGC 6798 (see Appendix sec:HI-fields for these velocity maps). We note that the observations of NGC 2685 and NGC 4203 were presented in 2009A&A...494..489J and 2015MNRAS.451..103Y , respectively, and refer the readers to 2015MNRAS.451..103Y for more details about the data reduction. We show the best-fitting model with the combined stellar and kinematics of NGC 4203 in Figure fig:kin as an example of our fit, in which the major structures of the stellar kinematics and velocity fields are recovered.

There is a significant misalignment between the stellar disc (or the equatorial plane of the stellar component) and the disc for NGC 4203, and a clear misalignment for NGC 2685 and NGC 6798 (about 15 ignoring counter-rotation). Among the four galaxies in the subsample, the dark matter fraction of NGC 2685 remains unchanged throughout all radii when include kinematics, while the dark matter fraction of NGC 4203 increases, and the dark matter fractions of NGC 5582 and NGC 6798 decrease, especially at f_ DM (5R_ e ) . For NGC 4203, the stellar mass dominates the enclosed mass within 2 R_ e (approximately 5 kpc), while dark matter dominates outside of 5 R_ e (approximately 10 kpc).

sources.summary: The survey identifies NGC 4203 as an S0/lenticular galaxy outside the Fisher-Tully late-type catalog, with an inclined outer gas ring causing Arecibo to miss part of its flux.

quotes.quote: All late-type members of our sample are listed in the Fisher and Tully (1981b) optically selected sample of nearby late-type galaxies; the only system not contained in Fisher and Tullys' Catalog is the S0 galaxy NGC 4203.

No. 13 (NGC 4203): This lenticular galaxy has an optical inclination of about 20 . Assuming that the gas rotates in the plane of the stellar disk, very high \ rotational velocities would be derived. However, radio synthesis imaging (van Driel 1988) has shown that the outer gas rotates in a highly inclined ring at an inclination of about 60 , the value adopted in correcting the profile widths (see Table 5).

This concerns No. 13 = NGC 4203 for which the \ survey flux is considerably higher than the flux measured at Arecibo. As the galaxy's outer \ ring is much larger than the Arecibo beam (see van Driel 1988) the \ measurement will reflect tht total flux.

sources.summary: The paper contrasts UGC 7256 with UGC 4173, describing strong A1 fluctuations, pronounced spiral arms, slightly unequal global-profile peaks, and a nearly constant phase indicating a global m=1 mode.

quotes.quote: UGC 7256 (Fig.  figexamples , lower row) is an example of a galaxy where we found strong fluctuations, so-called wiggles, in the distribution of A_1 . The \ intensity map shows a spiral galaxy with pronounced arms. The global profile has the typical two maxima. However, the intensities of the peaks slightly differ from each other, indicating lopsidedness. Furthermore, in both cases the phase _1 (R) is nearly constant with radius indicating a global m=1 mode.

sources.summary: The atlas labels NGC 4203 Unclear, describing extended ionized gas, an H I-rich dusty spiral-like host, complicated nuclear profiles, IFU kinematics, and possible north-south outflow motions.

quotes.quote: NGC 4203 (see Fig. Figure_AB5 ): The ionised gas morphology in our data set is classified as Unclear , as there is some extended emission that cannot be classified as coming from the disc or from outflowing emission exclusively from imaging data. characterised the object as a spiral-like, HI-rich, dusty galaxy (their Fig. C1), and gave an extinction map oriented east-west, in contrast to the north-south orientation of the H emission in our image. The nuclear spectrum of this galaxy has complicated profiles with several components Storchi2017,Cazzoli2018 . Stellar and gas kinematics from IFU spectroscopy were obtained by with the Mitchell spectrograph. The derived gas kinematics, even with sparser spatial resolution, seem to indicate that non-rotational motions, associated to outflows, could be present roughly along the north-south direction.

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 4203 to a nearby strong X-ray source, identifies it as a LINER-type AGN with broad Balmer lines, and notes adjacent ULX emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 4203: A remarkably similar object to the z = 0.615 BL Lac object near NGC 4151, is the strong X-ray source only 2.1 arcmin from NGC 4203. It has a redshift of z = 0.614. This is another source which was not included in the CP02 catalog due to previously known high redshift. NGC 4203 is a liner type AGN with broad Balmer lines. It is also a strong X-ray source and an IRAS infrared source.

sources.summary: NGC 4203 is described as an S0 galaxy with a LINER-type low-luminosity AGN, point-like radio emission, and X-rays dominated by a point source rather than diffuse gas.

quotes.quote: NGC 4203 is an S0 galaxy hosting a low-luminosity low-ionisation nuclear emission region (LINER) type AGN (LLAGN) Iyomoto98 . Point like radio emission is detected in our 4.9 VLA data. The \ observation shows an X-ray structure similar to IC 310. There is very little diffuse emission, with most of the X-rays coming from a point source coincident with the radio emission.

sources.summary: NGC4203 is treated as an HRS outlier with unusually low dust-to-H I ratio; the paper reports reliable excess atomic gas, peculiar H I morphology, and possible recent cold-gas accretion.

quotes.quote: In addition to deficient galaxies showing a higher M_ dust /M_ HI ratio than normal, it is interesting to note the presence of a few outliers having M_ dust /M_ HI significantly lower ( 10^ 3 ) than the average value for normal systems ( 10^ 2.1 ), namely: NGC3657, NGC4203, NGC4262, NGC4636 and UGC8045.

Conversely, we can exclude a measurement problem for NGC3657, NGC4203 and NGC4262 and conclude that these systems have significantly higher amounts of atomic hydrogen compared to their dust content. This is confirmed when we look in detail at the properties of these systems. NGC4203 and NGC4262 have peculiar \ morphologies krumm85,vandriel88 and several independent analyses have proposed that these galaxies might have recently accreted cold gas (see cortese09 and references therein).

Thus, we speculate that the unusually low M_ dust /M_ HI ratio observed in these systems may be due to the presence of a considerable amount of pristine gas not yet enriched by star formation, either because it has been recently accreted or because it has too low column density to form stars at high rate.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4203 as an S0 galaxy with point-like nuclear hard and soft X-ray emission, strongly suggesting that it hosts a low-luminosity AGN.

quotes.quote: Iyomoto et al. (1998) presented the ASCA results on the S0 galaxy NGC 4203, where they detect hard X-ray emission from a point-like source at the nucleus. A single power-law model with a photon index of 1.8, which is a typical value for Seyfert galaxies, fits the spectrum of NGC 4203 well. A point-like soft X-ray emission of the source was already found by Bregman et al. (1995) using ROSAT HRI data. The results on NGC 4203 strongly suggest that this object hosts a low-luminosity AGN.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 4203 has no significant X-ray variability, weak absorption evidence because of poor signal-to-noise, and optical-to-X-ray behavior similar to Seyferts.

quotes.quote: Although the uncertainties on the fitted hydrogen column densities (Table liners:tab:specfits , column \ 4) are not small, all sources -- except NGC 4203, whose spectrum has a poor signal-to-noise ratio -- show significant evidence for absorption in excess of the Galactic one (also reported in Table liners:tab:specfits ).
NGC 4203 exhibits no significant X-ray variability on any time scale, despite being, among the sources in Maoz2007:lowlumagns , the one that varied with the largest amplitude in the UV.

In M 81 and NGC 4203, the less UV-luminous sources in our sample (see Table 1), _ ox,obs has an intermediate value, similar to those of Seyferts, and the extrapolated _ ox is significantly flatter than the observed one and predicts X-ray luminosities that are 4-5 times higher than observed.

sources.summary: The paper calls UGC 7256 an extreme Type 5 galaxy, where receding and approaching sides change several times, the velocity field is warped, and a small H I cloud lies nearby.

quotes.quote: UGC 7256 is an extreme case of Type 5 as the rotation velocities of receding and approaching sides change sides several times. The velocity field of UGC 7256 looks slightly warped. Additionally, we located a small \ cloud nearby.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4203 as having a dominant X-ray nucleus and fits its XMM spectrum with a soft power law affected by Galactic absorption.

quotes.quote: A power-law fit to the ASCA spectrum resulted in a 1.8 iyomotoapj98:ngc4203 . ho01apjl gave NGC 4203 a class I X-ray morphology showing dominant X-ray nucleus. We find that the 40 ks \ spectrum is well fitted with a simple power-law affected by Galactic absorption with 2.3 , softer than the result reported for ASCA , most likely due to contamination from X-ray sources in the ASCA extraction region of 1 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4203 as a face-on SAB0 LINER with double-peaked broad H-alpha, point-like X-ray and radio detections, and near-infrared spectral comparison data.

quotes.quote: NGC 4203 (L1.9) This AGN is hosted by a SAB0 galaxy seen nearly face-on, with a few dust lanes visible in the bulge. The LINER has prominent, double-peaked broad H lines Shields00 and fairly symmetric optical emission lines Ho97 . The AGN is also detected at various other wavelengths: as a point source in X-rays Ho01 , as a variable UV source Maoz05 , and as a point source with an inverted radio spectrum Nagar00 .

sources.summary: UGC 7256 is described as an asymmetric, low-SFR S0 galaxy: symmetric in the infrared but not in H I, and the only early-type object in the WHISP sample.

quotes.quote: The two exceptions are UGC 7256 and UGC 7989, which are symmetric in the IR but not the .
The discrepant point in the lower right corner is the asymmetric galaxy UGC 7256 (NGC 4203) with low SFR. It is the only early type galaxy (S0) in our sample.

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 4203 with control galaxies, finding bluer UV colors and local high-density HI clumps with star-formation surface densities like late-type inner regions.

quotes.quote: As an example, we show the colour profiles for NGC 4203 together with its control galaxies NGC 3245 and NGC 3757 in Fig. fig:colour_colour . As can be seen from the colour profiles, NGC 4203 is generally bluer than its control galaxies, especially the colour FUV-NUV see also 2015MNRAS.451..103Y .

Although the peak of \ is not beyond the R _ SFTH , a similar case is also true for NGC 4203: 2015MNRAS.451..103Y show that some local high density \ clumps can have SFR surface density as high as the inner regions of late-type galaxies, but this information may be lost when using radial profiles or quantities integrated over large apertures.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4203 as a z=0.004 local high-column-density absorption system, notes its galaxy-QSO alignment and DLA-like metal lines, and includes it among known z<1 DLA galaxies.

quotes.quote: miller1999 and bowen2001 study the absorption line systems seen in NGC 4203 and SBS 1543+593, at redshifts of z=0.004 and z= 0.009 , respectively. For these systems, the galaxy-QSO alignment was identified before the absorption was found and in one case (NGC 4203) Ly- absorption has not been seen to date, but the structure and strength of observed metal lines resemble those of DLAs. All together, including the z 0 systems NGC 4203 and SBS 1543+593, there are now 20 DLA galaxies known at z<1 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4203 as a LINER with a variable radio core, large UV fluctuations, adopted Chandra/ASCA X-ray fluxes, and an assumed black hole mass.

quotes.quote: NGC 4203 - VLBA measurements by Anderson et al. (2004) of the unresolved (at the mas scale) core provide the following fluxes: 9.9 mJy at 0.7 cm; 9.0 mJy at 1.35 cm; 10.2 mJy at 1.9 cm; 8.5 mJy at 3.6 cm; 8.1 mJy at 6 cm. Nagar et al. (2002) have found that the radio core is variable. In the UV monitoring by M05, the nuclear source showed large fluctuations, 1.5 between maximum and minimum in F250W (mean: 58 10^ 17 ), and 1.4 in F330W (mean: 37 10^ 17 ).

gmw_00000286.overview: The active galaxy 1823+568 is classified as a BL Lac object [arXiv:1712.06314, arXiv:1208.0896] or a quasar [arXiv:1712.06314, arXiv:1307.3700] with an elliptical host [arXiv:1307.3700]. It features a complex, highly variable radio jet [arXiv:1208.0896, arXiv:1005.2177] surrounded by a diffuse halo [arXiv:1712.06314, arXiv:1208.0896].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1823 + 568/4C + 56.27 as a Fermi-detected BL Lac or quasar with a halo and rich pc-scale jet structure containing moving and stationary knots.

quotes.quote: 1823 + 568 (J1824 + 5651) 1823 + 568 (4C + 56.27) is a BL Lac object at a redshift z = 0.664 1986AJ.....91..494L , but 2006A&A...455..773V classified this source as a quasar. The source was listed in the Fermi /LAT AGN catalogue as 3FGL J1824.2+5649 and was detected at a significance level of 34.5 2015ApJS..218...23A . The VLA images at 1.6 GHz 1993MNRAS.264..298M and 5 GHz 1992AJ....104.1687K show a prominent core--jet morphology with the jet extending toward the east up to 5 . A diffuse halo surrounds the compact core--jet structure. The radio spectrum is flat above 1 GHz, but steep below 1 GHz, indicative of an extended steep-spectrum halo.

Our high-sensitity 43- and 86-GHz images (Fig. fig:1823+568 d) reveal rich jet structures on a scale of 2 mas (14 pc) and 0.5 mas (3.5 pc), respectively. Four jet components are identified at 43 GHz, and they were also detected at 15 GHz 2016AJ....152...12L . These jet components show complex motions, with a mixture of superluminally moving (J1 and J2) and stationary jet knots (J3 and J4).

sources.summary: The paper describes [ HB89 ] 1823+568 as a gamma-ray BL Lac object with kpc and VLBI jets, strong polarization, superluminal motion, and a time-lagged flux-PA correlation.

quotes.quote: [ HB89 ] 1823+568 The source 1823+568 (4C +56.27, z=0.664), is also a BL Lac object, and it is detected in gamma-ray by Fermi LAT. It shows a halo and one-sided kpc-scale jet to east (Kollgaard et al. 1992). VLA observation at 5 GHz by O'Dea, Barvainis Challis (1986) shows a curved jet extending southward from the core and turning to the east. The object is highly variable and has a strong linear polarization both in radio and optical bands (Lister 2001).

VLBI maps reveal a compact core and jets extending to the south, e.g., at 5 GHz (Fomalont et al. 2000). The 15 GHz MOJAVE result shows a super-luminal jet motion of 20.8 (Lister et al. 2009b). Our model-fit result from the MOJAVE 15 GHz `core', suggests a significant correlation between the peak flux density and the T/4 time-shift-forward PA of the major axis of inner-jet in Table tab1 . The variability period of T=7.0 0.4 yr is estimated with the PSD (see, Fig. fig4 and Fig. fig5 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1823+568 as a redshift 0.664 quasar with an elliptical host and complex polarized jet, then models component S1 as evidence for a roughly 60 microarcsecond binary black-hole system.

quotes.quote: 1823+568 is an quasar at a redshift of 0.664 0.001 LaPe+:86 . The host galaxy is elliptical according to HST observations FaUr+:97 . The jet morphology on kpc-scales is complex - a mirrored S in observations with the MTRLI at 1666 MHz and with the VLA at 2 and 6 cm ODeBa+:88 . The largest extension of 1823+568 is 15'', corresponding to 93 kpc. On pc-scales the jet is elongated and points in a southern direction from the core PeRe:88 in accordance with the kpc-structure.

We applied the method to component S1 of 1823+568 and to component C5 of 3C 279, which presents a large offset of the space origin from the VLBI core. We found that 1823+568 contains a binary black hole system whose size is R_ bin 60 as ( as is a microarcsecond) and 3C 279 contains a binary black hole system whose size is R_ bin 420 as .

sources.summary: The VLBA paper describes 1823+568 as a BL Lac object with flat core RM, possible positive transverse gradient away from the core, and epoch-dependent RM sign changes.

quotes.quote: Polarization observations of 6 radio--quasars (0133+476, 0420-014, 0745+241, 0906+430, 1633+382 and 1954+513) and 2 BL Lac objects (0256+075 and 1823+568) were carried out in a 24 hour session on November 2, 2008 using the VLBA.

1823+568 shows a very flat rotation measure in the core, with values of 1200 rad/m ^2 . As we move away from the VLBI core some indications of a transverse gradient taking only positive values arise. This is in general agreement with the idea that finite--beam effects blend different regions and make the detection of reliable features of the RM in the core impossible.
In our case, this is particularly true for 0745+241, 0906+430 or 1823+568, sources that show a change in the sign of the RM for the two epochs.

sources.summary: The paper models 1823+568's parsec-scale jet, finding that observed brightness temperatures match shock-in-jet predictions and that polarization supports a transverse magnetic field.

quotes.quote: Results of such an analysis are presented in Figs. Tb_1823 B-field_1823 for the BL Lac object 1823+568 and in Figs. Tb_CTA102 B-field_CTA102 for the quasar CTA 102. In both cases, the model brightness temperatures agree with the measured values. Our conclusion about the transverse B-field in 1823+568 (Fig. Tb_1823 ) is supported by the polarization map at 8.4 GHz in Fig. B-field_1823 Pushkarev08 . Measured sizes and brightness temperatures of VLBI components in BL Lac object 1823+568 and quasar CTA 102 are consistent with emission from relativistic shocks dominated by adiabatic losses.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1823+568 as a BL Lac with non-relaxed near-infrared isophotes, a possible close companion, radio variability, and misaligned large-scale radio structure.

quotes.quote: * 1823+568 (4C 56.27) (BL Lac, z=0.664 ): Source exhibiting non-relaxed isophotes in the NIR, indirect sign of past merger activity. A possible close companion is also observed (projected distance of 46 kpc, no redshift information). The source is variable in the radio and was claimed to show almost periodicity. On VLBA scales, the jet appears fairly straight, while on VLA scales the jet appears to be misaligned with the inner jet.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1823+568 as a BL Lac jet with an unusually fast component, quasar and FR II classifications, and possible intermediate BL Lac/quasar nature.

quotes.quote: 1823+568: This BL Lac jet at z = 0.664 has a very fast component speed: 26.2 2.6 , as compared with the next fastest BL Lac component ( 15.1 0.4 for 0851+202). It has been classified as a quasar by , and as a powerful FR II jet by based on its extended radio emission. It therefore may be an intermediate BL Lac/quasar type object.

sources.summary: The note describes 1823 + 568 as having a fast pc-scale jet and a bending kpc jet detected by Chandra near 2.5 arcseconds from the core.

quotes.quote: 1823 + 568 (4C + 56.27) There is a pc-scale jet at a PA of -160 \ with maximum apparent speed of 26.17 2013AJ....146..120L . The kpc scale jet is first oriented almost due south but bends about 1 \ from the core to the east, where it is clearly detected in the Chandra image at 2.5 \ from the core. There are no apparent features in the HST image that are associated with radio or X-ray emission, although the southern portion lies along a diffraction spike.

gmw_00000287.name: CTA 102
gmw_00000287.overview: CTA 102 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1304.2453] located at a redshift of 1.037 [arXiv:0911.1481]. It hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1711.06117] and exhibits a curved, highly polarized relativistic jet [arXiv:1011.4831, arXiv:1805.04299] that undergoes extreme multiwavelength flaring [arXiv:1805.04692, arXiv:1910.03609].

sources.summary: The paper presents CTA 102 as a redshift 1.037 quasar/radio source, analyzing optical microvariability and contemporaneous radio, optical, and X-ray flaring during elevated states.

quotes.quote: CTA 102, also known as PKS J2232+1143, has a redshift of 1.037 sch65 , and was first identified as a radio source by the Caltech Radio Survey har60 . san65 confirmed that it was a quasar, and observations of a blue stellar optical counterpart were published the same year wyn65 . CTA 102's optical polarization, observed at nearly 11 , put it in the class of High Polarization Quasars (HPQs), objects which exhibit optical polarization of at least 3 moo81 .

We present analysis of both the short-term optical and long-term multiwavelength variability of CTA 102. In 2004, this object was observed in an intense optical flaring state. Extensive R-band microvariability observations were carried out during this high state. In 2005, we obtained several weeks of contemporaneous radio, optical, and X-ray observations of CTA 102. These observations recorded distinct flaring activity in all three wavebands.

sources.summary: The paper studies CTA 102 during its 2006 radio flare, describing its quasar identification, lobes and curved VLBI jet, and interpreting the flare as a shock interaction.

quotes.quote: The radio source CTA 102 ( z=1.037 ) is one of the most observed AGN in the northern sky. Its observational history started in the late 1950s Har60 . Flux density variations were reported in the source Sho65 , which lead other authors to suggest that the signal was coming from an extraterrestrial civilization Kar64 . Later, CTA 102 was identified as a quasar.

High resolution 15 GHz VLBI images show a curved jet with components which exhibit apparent velocities up to 15.4 0.9 , adopting a cosmology with _m=0.27 , _ =0.73 and H_0=71 km s^ 1 Mpc^ 1 . Lis09b . Jor05 and Hov09 derived bulk Lorentz factors, , between 15 and 17 and Doppler factors, , between 15 and 22 from 43 GHz VLBI observations and from single-dish light curves. A major flux density outburst occurred in April 2006, which offers a unique opportunity to study CTA 102 under these conditions Fro10 .
The observed flare in CTA 102 could be explained by the propagation of a relativistic shock wave.

sources.summary: The paper identifies CTA 102 as a z=1.037 flat-spectrum radio quasar and reports dense optical/IR monitoring after a 2012 gamma-ray flare, finding polarization swings and ordered-field emission components.

quotes.quote: CTA 102 (also known as PKS J2232+1143, R.A. = 22^h 32^m 36.4^s , decl. = +11 \ 43 \ 50 .8 , J2000, z=1.037, 1989ApJS...69....1H ) was first identified as a strong radio source 1960PASP...72..237H and classified as a flat spectrum radio quasar FSRQ, 2010ApJ...722..520A from multi-wavelength observations. CTA 102 showed micro variability of optical flux and color in the 2004 flare 2009AJ....138.1902O . In this flare, the ``redder when brighter'' trend was reported and it was able to be explained by the superposition of variable synchrotron component from radio to optical bands and non-variable ``blue bump'' component which is thought to be thermal disk radiation connecting to the UV band. Recently, CTA 102 showed an extreme activity in the optical and GeV ray bands on September 2012 2012ATel.4397....1L,2012ATel.4409....1O . In this paper, we present results of high-temporal-density monitoring observations of CTA 102 just after the September 2012 ray flare.

To summarize, we performed dense optical/IR photometric and polarimetric monitoring of CTA 102 following strong ray flare in 2012 September with OISTER program. We found (i) smooth and gradual PA swing by 100 deg over the two nights on MJD 56195 and 56196, (ii) orphan polarized flux flare on MJD 56197, (iii) significant brightening on MJD 56202 and (iv) grossly perpendicular PAs to the jet direction during the two flares. Combined with recent VLBA detection of RM gradient across the jet, we infer that helical magnetic field would be present at the emission region responsible for the long-term baseline component.

sources.summary: The paper finds a continuous jet-sheath signature in CTA 102, estimates the sheath as an important seed-photon source, and models a June 2011 orphan gamma-ray flare.

quotes.quote: In Figure fig16 we present a stacked radio map of the blazar CTA 102. Similar to 4C 71 . 07, a continuous polarimetric signature of a jet sheath is evident down the edges of the jet in this stacked radio image. Given the continuous nature of the jet sheath present, we again compute an estimate of the bolometric luminosity of the sheath. The extent of the polarized intensity profile along each of the slices was used to trace the shaded gray regions shown in Figure fig16 . This was done by using the width of the sheath on each side of each slice (shown explicitly in Figure fig19 ) after determining, arbitrarily, the location where the polarized flux falls below 0.85 times the peak value. An estimate of the bolometric luminosity ( L_ bol ) of the sheath is computed by adding all of the deconvolved `sheath' flux (see Figures fig18 fig19 ) contained within the gray shaded regions of the stacked map above.

Similar to 4C 71 . 07, our estimate above indicates that the jet sheath in CTA 102 is an important source of seed photons even at parsec scales. We again, however, point out that convolution with the beam adds a contribution of flux from the spine to this sheath flux estimate due to our limited resolution transverse to the jet axis. An estimate of the inner ( r_ min ) and outer ( r_ max ) radii of the sheath was also obtained: we find that r_ min 0.84 pc and r_ max 1.28 pc . Similar to the sheath in 4C 71 . 07, these values are much larger than the inner and outer radii used in our model of the ring (see Table tab1 ) that we posit to exist upstream of the radio core. The Fermi LAT detected an orphan ray flare from CTA 102 during June 2011 (Figure fig17 ). Table tab2 lists the Ring of Fire model parameters used to obtain a fit to this orphan flare. The VLBA also observed the ejection of two blobs from the radio core of CTA 102, one occurring roughly 200 days before the onset of the orphan flare and the other roughly 200 days after. The times of the blob ejections are demarcated by vertical arrows in the lower panel of Figure fig17 . performed a detailed analysis of the kinematics of the blobs detected by the VLBA during these epochs and reported bulk Lorentz factors of the order 26 . These VLBI speeds are not all that dissimilar from the blob speed used in our model of this orphan flare ( _ final 35 ). The blob that passed through the core of CTA 102 roughly 200 days after this orphan flare may have supplied the electrons required to scatter the sheath photons.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes CTA 102 during a 2016 gamma-ray flare, deriving pc-scale jet morphology, component motions, polarization behavior, helical-jet variability, core distance, and magnetic-field estimates.

quotes.quote: The ray bright blazar CTA 102 is studied using imaging (new 15 GHz and archival 43 GHz Very Long Baseline Array, VLBA data) and time variable optical flux density, polarization degree and electric vector position angle (EVPA) spanning between 2015 June 1 and 2016 October 1, covering a prominent ray flare during 2016 January. The pc-scale jet indicates expansion with oscillatory features upto 17 mas. Component proper motions are in the range 0.04 - 0.33 mas yr ^ 1 with acceleration upto 1.2 mas followed by a slowing down beyond 1.5 mas.

A jet bulk Lorentz factor 17.5 , position angle of 128 3 , inclination angle 6 6 and intrinsic half opening angle 1 8 are derived from the VLBA data. These inferences are employed in a helical jet model to infer long term variability in flux density, polarization degree, EVPA and a rotation of the Stokes Q and U parameters. A core distance of r_ core, 43 \ GHz = 22.9 pc, and a magnetic field strength at 1 pc and the core location of 1.57 G and 0.07 G respectively are inferred using the core shift method.

CTA 102 is a high-polarization 1981ApJ...243...60M and optically violent variable quasar 1986ApJ...310..325M . It was identified as a flat-spectrum radio-loud quasar (FSRQ; a sub-class of blazars) based on a 1 - 22 GHz spectral index of 0.5 ( S ^ ) obtained from RATAN-600 telescope observations 1999A&AS..139..545K,2004ApJ...609..539K .

sources.summary: The paper studies the FSRQ CTA 102 with high-resolution mm-VLBI polarimetry, finding Faraday-rotation evidence for a helical inner-jet magnetic field during a 2016-2017 multiwavelength flaring state.

quotes.quote: We study the magnetic field structure of the quasar CTA 102 with 3 and 7 mm VLBI polarimetric observations, reaching an unprecedented resolution ( 50 as). We also investigate the variability and physical processes occurring in the source during the observing period, which coincides with a very active state of the source over the entire electromagnetic spectrum. We perform the Faraday rotation analysis using 3 and 7 mm data and we compare the obtained rotation measure (RM) map with the polarization evolution in 7 mm VLBA images.

We present mm-VLBI polarimetric images of the FSRQ CTA 102 with the highest possible resolution currently achievable ( 50 as). The images were obtained with the GMVA at 86 GHz in May 2016, September 2016, and March 2017. Combining 43 GHz VLBA observations from the VLBA-BU-BLAZAR program with our 86 GHz GMVA data, we obtained the first high-resolution Faraday rotation image between these two frequencies. The Faraday rotation structure and the intrinsic EVPA orientation highlighted by the high-resolution image reveal the existence of a large-scale helical magnetic field in the very inner regions ( 2.46 10 ^ 5 gravitational radii, deprojected) of CTA 102 jet. From late 2016 to the beginning of 2017, CTA 102 was in a very active state, displaying extraordinary flares from radio to ray frequencies.

sources.summary: The paper describes C.T.A. 102 as a redshift-1.037 blazar whose 2016-2017 giant flare can be modeled by jet particles from an ablated gas cloud.

quotes.quote: \ was detected as a radio source in 1960 . It presented itself as a rather peculiar source, which culminated in \ becoming the content of a pop song The Byrds: ``C.T.A. 102'' (1967, Album: Younger than yesterday) , which inspired the title of this contribution. While parts of 's peculiarities have been explained by the fact that it is a blazar, it is still able to surprise. As many blazars , \ (located at a redshift of =1.037 ) is a highly variably source.

In November 2016, fluxes began rising steadily over the course of two months culminating in an extreme high state with fluxes reaching more than 50 times pre-November levels around new year 2017. At this time, \ was among the brightest ray sources in the sky despite its large distance from Earth.

We have successfully reproduced the tremendous flux changes during the 2016-2017 flare by invoking a significant increase in particle number in the jet. These additional particles originate from a gas cloud that collides with the jet and which is gradually ablated causing the four-months-long flare.

sources.summary: The paper treats CTA 102 as a highly polarized z=1.037 FSRQ with multiwavelength variability, including a 2016 gamma-ray flare correlated with optical and X-ray flaring.

quotes.quote: CTA 102 This FSRQ at a redshift of z = 1.037 1965ApJ...141.1295S is highly polarized 1981ApJ...243...60M and variable in the optical band 1986ApJ...310..325M . It was detected in the ray band both by EGRET 1994ApJS...94..551F and Fermi 2009ApJ...700..597A . It has been studied for flux variations across different wavebands 2018A&A...617A..59K and minute like time scales of variability were detected in the optical 2009AJ....138.1902O and ray bands 2018ApJ...854L..26S .

CTA 102 The source was found to be in a steady and low brightness state during most of the time between 2008 August to 2018 August, except for a spectacular ray flare in the beginning of 2016. The multi-wavelength light curves are shown in Fig. figure-8 . We have identified 2 epochs in this source for studying the correlation between optical and ray variations. A summary of these two epochs is given in Table table-2 and expanded plots of these two epochs are shown in Fig. figure-9 .
Epoch B: The source showed a major ray flare during this epoch. This flaring in the ray band was also accompanied by flaring behaviour in the X-ray and optical wavelengths.

sources.summary: The paper identifies CTA 102 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar with simultaneous optical and gamma-ray QPOs of 7.6 days, best explained by a helical emitting blob in its jet.

quotes.quote: CTA 102, a flat spectrum radio quasar at a redshift of 1.032, has displayed significant activity since 2016. The multi-waveband light curve of CTA 102 shows signs of quasi-periodic oscillations during the 2016--2017 flare. Our goal is to rigorously quantify the presence of any possible periodicity in the emitted flux during the mentioned period and to explore the possible causes that can give rise to it.

Highly significant, simultaneous quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) were observed in the ray and optical fluxes of blazar CTA 102 during its highest optical activity episode in 2016--2017. The periodic flux modulation had a dominant period of 7.6 days and lasted for 8 cycles (MJD 57710--57770). All of the methods used point toward significant ( >4 ) quasi-periodic modulation in both ray and optical fluxes. Several possible models were explored while probing the origin of the periodicity, and by extension, the 2016--2017 optical flare. The best explanation for the detected QPO appears to be a region of enhanced emission (blob), moving helically inside the jet.

sources.summary: The paper finds most sub-day waiting times arise from CTA 102, with grouped fast flares and a one-hour fast component affected by Fermi-LAT exposure and temporal pile-up.

quotes.quote: We will show for a physical scenario that close flares could not be resolved individually (pile-up effect), causing a reduction of short waiting time statistics. a case study: CTA 102 We note that the majority of short waiting times ( _t \ < \ 1d ) come from CTA 102 . They are mainly grouped around MJD 57738 and MJD 57752. The unbinned light curve of the source for a 100d period (obtained with a chance probability threshold P_ thr \ = \ 1.3 10^ 3 ) is reported in fig fig_ulc_cta102 . The peculiar waiting times distribution is reported in Figure fig_waiting_times_single . The unbinned light curve can be compared with the results reported in dammando2019 and in meyer2019 .

The Fast component and the gamma-ray data of CTA 102 We observed a few tens of short waiting times (for waiting times < 1d), mainly detected for CTA 102. The typical timescale of this component is 1 hour. The detailed simulations, performed taking into account for the FERMI-LAT exposure to the source, and realistic duration of flaring activity show that temporal pile-up of close-by flares acts for waiting times < 1.5-4d.

sources.summary: The paper models CTA 102 as a prominent flaring FSRQ, fitting its broadband SED and finding bimodal posterior solutions for the emission-region location in the jet.

quotes.quote: CTA 102, at a redshift of z = 1.037 1965ApJ...141.1295S , is another prominent FSRQ that hosts a black hole with an estimated mass of 8.5 10^8 M_ BH 2014Natur.510..126Z . The source periodically enters a state of active emission. For instance, between 2016 and 2017, CTA 102 was in a prolonged active state, exhibiting an extraordinary outburst across all wavebands see Fig. 1 of for the broadband evolution of the flux of the source 2022MNRAS.517.2757S .
The SED of CTA 102 between MJD 56196.7-56202.3 was retrieved from 2022MNRAS.517.2757S . It is displayed alongside with the results of the modeling on the right panel of Figure sed .

The posterior distributions of the model parameters shown in Appendix, reveal a bimodal distribution for the radius R , at R 4 10^ 15 cm and R 5 10^ 17 cm. The location of the emission region, and consequently the main field contributing to the formation of the HE emission in FSRQs, remains an open question. In our framework, the fit drives the selection of the relevant photon field to explain the provided data by finding suitable parameter sets. This bimodality suggests the existence of two sets of conditions compatible with modeling the observed SED of CTA 102. Specifically, this indicates two distinct physical scenarios with different geometrical locations within the blazar jet that can produce the emission.

sources.summary: The paper applies the shock-in-jet model to CTA 102, finding compatible magnetic-field orientations, jet polarization behavior, and a bright outer component likely affected by ambient interaction.

quotes.quote: Results of such an analysis are presented in Figs. Tb_1823 B-field_1823 for the BL Lac object 1823+568 and in Figs. Tb_CTA102 B-field_CTA102 for the quasar CTA 102. In both cases, the model brightness temperatures agree with the measured values. In the quasar CTA 102, both transverse and longitudinal magnetic field orientations are consistent with the model (Fig. Tb_CTA102 ). This is confirmed by MOJAVE Lister05 observations of this source (Fig. B-field_CTA102 ). In the innermost part of the jet, the magnetic field is predominantly transverse, and it becomes longitudinal at larger distances, implying dissipation of shocks and creation of polarization sheath around the jet.

It should also be noted that the observed brightness temperature of the largest ( d=3.1 mas) jet component located at 12 mas from the core is higher than the model brightness temperature ( T_ b ^ obs /T_ b ^ model 6 ). This can be explained by an interaction with the ambient medium, also seen in the spectral index map (Fig. alpha_CTA102 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats CTA 102 as a blazar/quasar with redshift 1.037, long-term multiwavelength variability, and a major radio flare modeled through jet spectral evolution.

quotes.quote: The blazar CTA 102 (B2230+114) has a redshift of z=1.037 Hewitt:1989p1143 . It is classified as a highly polarized quasar (HPQ) with a linear optical polarization above 3 VeronCetty:2003p1264 and a V-band magnitude of 17.33. The source was observed for the first time by Harris:1960p1135 . Kardashev:1964p1156 reported on possible signals from an extraterrestrial civilization coming from CTA 102.

Sholomitskii:1965p1247 found the first variation in flux density for a radio source. Later observations identified CTA 102 as a quasar. Since that time CTA 102 has been the target of numerous observations at different wavelengths.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes multifrequency VLBA observations of CTA 102 during its 2006 radio flare, deriving jet kinematics, physical parameters, recollimation shocks, and a possible shock-shock interaction.

quotes.quote: We performed multifrequency multiepoch Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) observations of the blazar CTA 102 during its 2006 radio flare, the strongest ever reported for this source. These observations provide an excellent opportunity to investigate the evolution of the physical properties of blazars, especially during these flaring events We want to study the kinematic changes in the source during the strong radio outburst in April 2006 and test the assumption of a shock-shock interaction. This assumption is based on the analysis and modeling of the single-dish observations of CTA 102 (Paper I). In this paper we study the kinematics of CTA 102 at several frequencies using VLBI observations.

We cross-identified seven features throughout our entire multifrequency observations and find evidence of two possible recollimation shocks around 0.1 mas (deprojected 18 pc at a viewing angle =2.6^ ) and 6.0 mas (deprojected 1 kpc ) from the core. The 43 GHz observations reveal a feature ejected at epoch t_ ej =2005.9 0.2 , which could be connected to the 2006 April radio flare. Furthermore, this feature might be associated with the traveling component involved in the possible shock-shock interaction, which gives rise to the observed double peak structure in the single-dish light curves reported in Paper I.

sources.summary: The paper studies CTA 102 during its 2012 and 2016 optical outbursts, highlighting unprecedented brightness, rapid intra-night variability, and a favored changing-Doppler-factor explanation.

quotes.quote: In this work, we explore the optical variations of the FSRQ CTA 102 during its two latest outbursts. Normally, the object stays at a rather low quiescent state of about 17 -- 18 mag; however, in 2012, during an unprecedented back then outburst it reached 13 mag (Larionov et al. 2016). Even more strikingly, in the late 2016 the object reached 11 mag (Calcidese et al. 2016).

This is an extremely high state not for this blazar only, but also for any blazar. Such bright states (if any) have been reported throughout the years only for very near objects with often-significant host galaxy contribution. CTA 102, on the other hand is a point-like object at z=1.04
We discuss different variability scenarios and favor the changing Doppler factor of the emitting blobs as the most plausible one to account for the observed intra-night variability.

sources.summary: The paper finds CTA 102 has highly polarized jet emission, rapid EVPA rotation, rest-frame RMs of several times 10^4 rad/m^2, and a one-month RM sign change likely tied to flaring.

quotes.quote: CTA 102 This source shows a high degree of polarization, up to 40 , in its jet at 22 GHz but becomes compact at higher frequencies. The EVPAs rotate rapidly as function of frequency, with different slopes in the EVPA-- ^2 diagram between 22/43 GHz and 43/86 GHz. RMs at 43/86 GHz are a few times 10^4 \ rad/m^2 in the source rest frame in both epochs. However, a value in the 2016 December data is much larger than that in the 2017 January data.

The signs of RMs are different in our two epochs, while their absolute values are of the same order of magnitude. This behavior suggests that the line-of-sight component of the jet magnetic field changed its direction within 1 month, while magnetic field strength and electron density (or at least their product) did not vary substantially. This sign change might be related to a strong flare that occurred during our KVN observations raiteri2017 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes CTA 102's 2016-17 outburst, finding record multiwavelength fluxes, rapid variability, color and spectral trends, and co-spatial high-energy emission regions.

quotes.quote: Such an unprecedented flaring activity in FSRQ CTA 102, during 2016 November - 2017 January, is used for a detailed study to understand flaring mechanisms at short and long time scales, spectral behaviour in different energy regimes and to estimate sizes and location of the high-energy emitting region in the jet. Multiwavelength (MW) data ( ray, X-ray, UV, optical and radio) for CTA 102 during its outburst period, are obtained from Fermi-LAT, Swift-XRT/UVOT, Steward Observatory, Mt Abu Infrared Observatory and OVRO. These are analyzed to construct MW light curves, extract the spectral information, and to perform the correlated variability studies. Our study shows that CTA 102 attained the highest ever flux levels across the electromagnetic spectrum (EMS) while flaring and otherwise, with rapid and prolonged activities at all the frequencies.

CTA 102 was in extremely bright phase during 2016-17, due possibly to, successive high energy particle injections into the jet, creating shocks traveling down the jet which lead to the overall flux enhancement across the EMS. Alternatively, a decreasing viewing angle could also lead to the enhanced flux. The study reveals correlated variations in all the energy bands, with lags within time bins, indicating to co-spatial origin of the emissions.

sources.summary: The paper studies CTA 102 as a luminous FSRQ/blazar, analyzing its 2016-2017 gamma-ray flares and modeling multiwavelength emission with a single region.

quotes.quote: The blazar CTA 102 (FSRQ) is a luminous and well studied quasar located at a redshift of z = 1.037 ( Schmidt (1965) ). Like other blazars it also has a jet, oriented close to our line of sight. Because of the relativistic beaming effect all the emission is beamed along the jet axis and as a result we observe a violent variability at all wavelengths. As a quasar it was first identified by Sandage et al. (1965) and classified as a highly polarised quasar by Moore et al. (1981) .

It is highly variable in the optical band and its variability has been investigated by Osterman Meyer et al.(2009) , who found that faster variability is associated with higher flux states. Due to this behaviour, the object has been termed as an Optically Violent Variable (OVV) quasar ( Maraschi et al. (1986) ). Variability in the form of flares has also been observed for this source in centimeter and millimeter wavelengths as well as in the X-rays (RXTE observation, Osterman Meyer et al.(2009) ).

sources.summary: The paper observes quasar CTA 102 twice after optical and gamma-ray activity reports, finding polarization consistent with non-thermal emission and strong brightness variability.

quotes.quote: CTA 102 CTA 102 was first discovered at radio wavelengths in 1960 Harris1960 and was subsequently classified as a Quasar via follow-up optical observations Sandage1965 . We observed the source twice during our observing runs. The first was in response to increased optical activity reported on 2016 June 9 Larionov2016 and we observed the source 11 days later and measured a polarisation of P = 22.53( 0.14) in V band. The second was in response to report of an increase of gamma-ray emission detected on 2017 July 7 Bulgarelli2017 . We observed the source approximately one month after the report and measured a polarisation of P = 6.58( 0.45) in Z band.

The polarisation measurements are both consistent with non-thermal emission. Figure fig:cta102_plot shows that this source exhibits significant variability - both in brightness and polarisation over very short timescales.

sources.summary: The paper presents 14 years of multiwavelength CTA 102 observations, modeling 117 SEDs to infer relativistic electron and jet properties during quiescent and bright flaring states.

quotes.quote: We present long-term multiwavelength observations of blazar CTA 102 ( z=1.037 ). Detailed temporal and spectral analyses of ray, X-ray and UV/optical data observed by Fermi LAT, Swift XRT, NuSTAR and Swift-UVOT over a period of 14 years, between August 2008 and March 2022, was performed. We found strong variability of source emission in all the considered bands, especially in the ray band it exhibited extreme outbursts when the flux crossed the level of 10^ 5 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 . Using the Bayesian Blocks algorithm, we split the adaptively binned ray light curve into 347 intervals of quiescent and flaring episodes and for each period built corresponding multiwavelength spectral energy distributions (SEDs), using the available data.

Among the considered SEDs, 117 high-quality (quasi) contemporaneous SEDs which have sufficient multiwavelength data, were modeled using JetSeT framework within a one-zone leptonic synchrotron and inverse Compton emission scenario assuming the emitting region is within the broad-line-region and considering internal and external seed photons for the inverse Compton up-scattering. As a result of modeling, the characteristics of the relativistic electron distribution in the jet as well as jet properties are retrieved and their variation in time is investigated. The applied model can adequately explain the assembled SEDs and the modelling shows that the data in the bright flaring periods can be reproduced for high Doppler boosting and magnetic field.

sources.summary: The paper uses multi-frequency VLBI and single-dish monitoring of CTA 102's 2006 radio flare to infer core shifts, magnetic fields, particle density, and shock interactions.

quotes.quote: The dense sampling of the 2006 radio flare in the active galaxy CTA 102 ( z =1.037) with multi--frequency single--dish as well as VLBI observations provides an excellent laboratory to study the temporal and the spatial variation in the physical conditions of the source during the quiet and active states. The analysis and modelling of the single dish light curves revealed a travelling shock--recollimation shock interaction in a non-pressure matched jet as a possible explanation of the observed behaviour . Additional evidences supporting this hypothesis were found from the kinematic analysis of the long-term monitoring of the source at 43 GHz within the Boston University Blazar Monitoring Program , at 15 GHz with the MOJAVE program , and from eight multi-frequency VLBI observations (2 GHz - 86 GHz) during the 2006 outburst.

Summarising, we derived the magnetic field and particle density of the jet in CTA 102 during the 2006 radio flare and the obtained variation is in agreement with the passage of a relativistic shock through the core region. Based on the combination of the spectral analysis presented here, and the results of the kinematic analysis in we claim that the observed behaviour in CTA 102 is the result of the propagation and interaction of traveling shock waves with recollimation shocks in an over-pressured jet. Further analysis of the interaction between these features will be conducted using relativistic hydrodynamic (RHD) and emission simulations following .

sources.summary: The paper reports dense optical/near-IR monitoring of CTA 102 during enhanced gamma-ray activity, detecting orphan polarized and intranight flares attributed to a high-polarization emission component.

quotes.quote: CTA 102 We densely monitored CTA 102 in the optical and near-infrared bands for the subsequent ten nights using OISTER , following Fermi LAT detection of the enhanced gamma-ray activity. On MJD 56197 (2012 September 27, 4-5 days after the peak of bright gamma-ray flare), a polarized flux showed a transient increase, while a total flux and PA remained almost constant during the ``orphan polarized-flux flare''. We also detected an intra-night and prominent flare on MJD 56202. Emergence of a new emission component with high PD up to 40 would be responsible for the observed two flares, and such a high PD indicates a presence of highly ordered magnetic field at the emission site. The observed directions of PA is perpendicular to the jet.
The total and polarized fluxes showed quite similar temporal variations, but PA again remained constant during the flare.

sources.summary: The paper compares simulations with CTA 102, linking its double-hump radio flare, VLBI standing features, and frequency-dependent flare evolution to traveling-shock and standing-shock interactions.

quotes.quote: The spectral analysis of single-dish observations in the cm-mm and sub-millimetre regime for the blazar CTA 102 during a major outburst leads to double hump structure in the turnover frequency -- turnover flux density plane 2011A&A...531A..95F and, at the same time, the kinematic analysis of this source from VLBI observations revealed several standing features, one of them 18 pc (de-projected) from the core 2013A&A...551A..32F . The spectral analysis applied to the VLBI observations exhibit an increase in the particle density and magnetic field strength at the location of the standing features 2013A&A...557A.105F . A conclusion derived from those works was that the double hump in the light curve was caused by the interaction between the traveling perturbation and a standing shock.

In this subsection, we discuss the similarities and differences found when comparing the results shown in the single-dish light-curves (Fig. lcdk3 and lcdk1 ) with the observed light-curve from the 2006 flare in CTA 102 Fig. 1 in 2011A&A...531A..95F and between the radio maps at different frequencies (Figs. 15-22_PM and 15-22_OP ) and VLBI observations 2013A&A...551A..32F,2013A&A...557A.105F . Regarding flux variations, we must recall that the highest frequencies start to show an increase in flux before the low frequencies in CTA 102, whereas this does not happen in the simulated models by construction: We simulate a jet that does not include the most compact regions, thus the lack of the necessary opacity to reproduce this effect. Despite this difference, we find relevant similar behaviours in the flare evolution: All cases show a pre-bump in flux at intermediate frequencies (14.5 and 37 GHz in the case of CTA 102, 43 and 86 GHz in the case of the PM jet, and 22, 43 and 86 in the OP jet); the perturbation produces little or no increase of the flux at low frequencies, which is seen both in the light-curve of CTA 102 and in the simulated models, and the flux increase is maximum at tens of GHz in all cases.

sources.summary: The paper applies a cloud-ablation model to CTA 102’s 2016-2017 flare, treating it as a distant FSRQ with a luminous disk and massive central black hole.

quotes.quote: We apply this model to a recent flare in CTA 102 , where fluxes varied significantly over several months. is an FSRQ at a redshift = 1.037 , roughly half-way across the observable Universe. The accretion disk luminosity is L_ disk = 3.8 10^ 46 erg/s zcst14 . The mass of the central black hole is estimated at M_ bh 8.5 10^8 M_ zcst14 giving an Eddington luminosity of L_ Edd 1.1 10^ 47 erg/s. The BLR properties have been derived by using UV spectroscopy observations with the Hubble Space Telescope , resulting in a luminosity of L_ BLR = 4.14 10^ 45 erg/s, and a radius of R_ BLR = 6.7 10^ 17 cm (all quantities given in the AGN frame).
This AGN has been reported in all the available catalogs, and is identified as 3FGL J2232.5+1143 in the third source catalog aFea15 .

sources.summary: The paper studies CTA 102 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar/blazar whose 2016-2017 flare showed correlated gamma-ray, optical, near-infrared, UV, and X-ray variability explained through jet-geometry changes.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present multifrequency observations of the blazar CTA 102 during 2013--2017. CTA 102 (also known as 4C + 11.69) is an FSRQ at redshift z = 1.037 schmidt65 . Flaring activity in the optical band has been observed from this source in 1978 pica88 , 1996 katajainen00 , and 2004 osterman09 .

In 2016 November, CTA 102 entered a new very-high-activity state in rays, as observed by Fermi LAT, reaching a daily flux higher than 1 10 ^ 5 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 on 2016 December 16 ciprini16 . This flaring activity continued for a few weeks in rays e.g., bulgarelli16, xu16 . A significant increase of activity was observed over the entire electromagnetic spectrum e.g., calcidese16,ojha16,righini16 .

sources.summary: CTA 102 is presented as a flat-spectrum radio quasar at z=1.032 whose 2016-2018 outburst is well fit by a cloud-ablation model, with shorter spikes attributed to cloud or jet structure.

quotes.quote: CTA 102 is a flat spectrum radio quasar at a redshift of 1.032 . Between early 2016 and early 2018 it underwent several major outbursts, of which the brightest was roughly in the middle of this time span in early January 2017. It became so bright that it was briefly the brightest source in rays, and became visible in the optical band to the human eye through small telescopes.

The multiwavelength light curves of the main event are shown in Fig. fig:cta102 . We use the cloud ablation model to reproduce the long-term trend, and achieve an excellent fit. The shorter-term spikes are not of our concern, and could be caused by sub-structures in the cloud, or instabilities in the jet during the ablation process.

sources.summary: The paper describes CTA 102 as a distant, bright FSRQ observed by NuSTAR during a major flare that probed its inverse-Compton component.

quotes.quote: CTA 102 CTA 102 is among the brightest FSRQs in the high energy ray band despite its large distance, at z = 1.037. Fermi-LAT observations revealed that the flux of this blazar sometimes exceeds 10^ 5 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 . CTA 102 was observed by \ only once in 2016, during a major flare when it was also bright in rays. As shown by the SED of Fig. ctased , \ data probe the rise of the IC component at its maximum state ever observed in the hard X-rays.

Our result ( 1.3 ) is fully consistent with an earlier work , which also showed that Swift monitoring detected a harder when brighter behaviour. The broadband SED of CTA 102, including the \ data (see Fig. ctased ) was modeled assuming that the jet dissipation occurs close (within BLR) or far from (outside BLR) the central source.

sources.summary: The paper uses 2230+114 as a quasar example whose optical and VLBA-core polarization angles can be fitted with high Faraday rotation and an optically thick radio core.

quotes.quote: Fig. 1 shows our images for the quasar 2230+114. The nominal observed values for the polarization angles are 70^ at , 34^ at and 94^ at , which clearly do not yield a reasonable ^2 fit. A good fit is obtained if both the and polarization angles are rotated by 90^ , with the implied offset between _0 - _ opt 60^ . An inspection of the core degrees of polarization at our three wavelengths and the -- and -- core spectral indices ( 0.46 ) provide no evidence for an optical-depth transition in our wavelength range, but suggest that the VLBA core is probably optically thick at all three wavelengths. Alternatively, a comparably good fit is obtained if the polarization angle is rotated by and the polarization angle by 2 ; in this case, the inferred rotation measure is about 16,700 rad/m ^2 and the inferred zero-wavelength polarization angle is _0 45^ , offset from _ opt by roughly 80^ , close to the offset expected if the radio and optical emission regions are cospatial, but the radio core emission is optically thick.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the blazar CTA 102, measuring VLBI core shifts to infer the black-hole-to-86-GHz-core distance and describing its redshift, supermassive black hole, and radio morphology.

quotes.quote: We have performed an accurate analysis of the frequency-dependent shift of the VLBI core location for a multi-wavelength set of images of the blazar CTA 102 including data from 6 cm down to 3 mm. The measure of the position of the central black hole, with mass 10^ 8.93 M_ , in the blazar CTA 102 reveals a distance of 8 10^4 gravitational radii to the 86 GHz core, in agreement with similar measures obtained for other blazars and distant radio galaxies, and in contrast with recent results for the case of nearby radio galaxies, which show distances between the black hole and the radio core that can be two orders of magnitude smaller.

The blazar CTA 102 has a redshift z=1.037 (Schmidt ; a luminosity distance of 6.7 Gpc, and an image scale of 8.11 pc/milliarcsec) and hosts a supermassive black hole (SMBH) of 10^ 8.93 M_ (Zamaninasab et al. \ ).
